(3) Frontotemporal dementia: personality and behavioural changes BEFORE memory loss, disinhibition, apathy, language problems, frontal/temporal atrophy on MRI, younger onset (45-65). (4) Depression (pseudodementia): low mood, subjective memory complaints often exceed objective deficits, responds to antidepressant treatment. NICE NG97 recommends cognitive assessment, blood screen to exclude reversible causes, and structural brain imaging.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 72-year-old man is brought to his GP by his wife. She is concerned because over the past year he has had vivid visual hallucinations (seeing children and animals in the house), fluctuating alertness (some days he is lucid, other days profoundly confused), and he has developed a shuffling gait with stiffness. He also acts out his dreams violently at night (REM sleep behaviour disorder). His memory is relatively preserved compared to his visuospatial function (he can no longer navigate or judge distances). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alzheimer disease
Option_B: Charles Bonnet syndrome
Option_C: Delirium
Option_D: Lewy body dementia
Option_E: Parkinson disease dementia
Explanation: This is dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) — the second most common neurodegenerative dementia after Alzheimer disease. The revised McKeith criteria for DLB identify core clinical features: (1) Fluctuating cognition with pronounced variations in attention and alertness. (2) Recurrent, well-formed visual hallucinations (typically of people, children, or animals — often detailed and non-threatening). (3) REM sleep behaviour disorder (acting out dreams — may precede cognitive symptoms by years). (4) Spontaneous features of parkinsonism (bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor — though tremor may be less prominent than in PD). All four are present in this patient. Cognitive profile: visuospatial and attentional deficits are prominent early (unlike AD where memory is the earliest deficit). CRITICAL prescribing safety: patients with DLB have severe neuroleptic sensitivity — antipsychotics (even atypical ones) can cause life-threatening reactions (severe rigidity, obtundation, autonomic instability) and are relatively contraindicated. If antipsychotic treatment is absolutely necessary, low-dose quetiapine or clozapine may be considered with extreme caution. Treatment: acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil or rivastigmine — NICE-approved for DLB, can help hallucinations and cognitive fluctuations). Parkinson disease dementia is the same pathology but PD motor symptoms precede dementia by >1 year (the '1-year rule').

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 78-year-old man with a diagnosis of mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease is reviewed in the memory clinic. His cognitive function has been declining gradually over 2 years. He is living at home with support from his wife. According to NICE NG97, what is the first-line pharmacological treatment?
Option_A: Donepezil (or rivastigmine or galantamine) — an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor
Option_B: Ginkgo biloba supplement
Option_C: Memantine as first-line for mild-to-moderate disease
Option_D: No pharmacological treatment is available or recommended
Option_E: Prednisolone to reduce neuroinflammation
Explanation: NICE NG97 (dementia) recommends acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors as first-line pharmacological treatment for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease. The three options are: (1) Donepezil — most commonly used, once daily, generally well tolerated. (2) Rivastigmine — available as oral or transdermal patch (patch has fewer GI side effects). (3) Galantamine — also has nicotinic receptor modulation. These drugs inhibit acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine levels in the brain (compensating for the cholinergic deficit in AD). They provide modest symptomatic benefit — stabilising or slowing cognitive decline for 6-12 months, and may improve behavioural symptoms. Common side effects: nausea, diarrhoea, insomnia, bradycardia (caution in cardiac conduction disease). For moderate-to-severe Alzheimer disease: memantine (an NMDA receptor antagonist) can be used as monotherapy or added to an AChE inhibitor. Memantine is NOT first-line for mild disease. Non-pharmacological interventions are equally important: cognitive stimulation therapy (CST), structured group activities, exercise, and carer support. AChE inhibitors are NOT recommended for mild cognitive impairment or frontotemporal dementia. They ARE recommended for DLB and Parkinson disease dementia.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An FY2 doctor is asked to assess a 78-year-old woman on the medical ward who has been confused since admission 3 days ago with a urinary tract infection. She has a background of mild Alzheimer disease but was at her baseline cognitive function before admission. The nurse asks whether she has delirium or worsening dementia. Which feature most reliably distinguishes delirium from dementia?
Option_A: Delirium has an acute onset with a fluctuating course — dementia has a gradual onset with a progressive course
Option_B: Delirium always causes visual hallucinations while dementia never does
Option_C: Delirium only occurs in patients without pre-existing cognitive impairment
Option_D: Dementia causes acute confusion while delirium causes chronic confusion
Option_E: There is no reliable way to distinguish delirium from dementia
Explanation: Distinguishing delirium from dementia is a fundamental clinical skill. The key differentiating features are: DELIRIUM: (1) ACUTE onset (hours to days). (2) FLUCTUATING course (lucid intervals alternating with confusion). (3) Impaired attention (the hallmark — patient cannot focus, is easily distractible). (4) Usually caused by an identifiable precipitant (infection, medication, metabolic disturbance). (5) Potentially REVERSIBLE if the cause is treated. (6) Altered level of consciousness (may be hyperactive/agitated or hypoactive/drowsy — hypoactive delirium is often missed). DEMENTIA: (1) GRADUAL onset (months to years). (2) PROGRESSIVE course (steadily worsening). (3) Memory impairment is typically the earliest feature. (4) Attention is relatively preserved until late stages. (5) Consciousness is usually clear (alert). (6) Irreversible (though symptoms may fluctuate in DLB). CRITICAL: delirium and dementia frequently COEXIST — pre-existing dementia is the single strongest risk factor for developing delirium. Delirium superimposed on dementia is very common in hospitalised elderly patients (this patient has both). The presence of pre-existing dementia does not exclude delirium — both must be assessed and delirium actively treated. The 4AT tool screens for delirium regardless of baseline cognitive status.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: A 68-year-old man presents to the memory clinic with a 2-year history of personality change. His wife reports he has become socially disinhibited (making inappropriate sexual comments to strangers), apathetic (lost interest in his hobbies), and has developed compulsive eating behaviours (consuming large quantities of sweet foods). His memory is relatively preserved. On examination, he has primitive reflexes (grasp reflex). An MRI brain shows bilateral frontal and anterior temporal lobe atrophy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alzheimer disease
Option_B: Bipolar disorder — manic episode
Option_C: Frontotemporal dementia (behavioural variant)
Option_D: Huntington disease
Option_E: Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Explanation: This is behavioural variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD) — the most common form of frontotemporal dementia. Key distinguishing features: (1) Personality and behavioural changes are the EARLIEST and most prominent features — BEFORE memory decline. This is the key distinction from Alzheimer disease. (2) Disinhibition (socially inappropriate behaviour, loss of manners, impulsive actions). (3) Apathy and loss of empathy. (4) Compulsive/ritualistic behaviours and dietary changes (particularly sweet food craving — 'sweet tooth'). (5) Executive dysfunction. (6) Relative preservation of memory and visuospatial function (initially). (7) Younger onset than AD — typically 45-65 years. (8) MRI: frontal and/or anterior temporal lobe atrophy (the 'knife-edge' atrophy pattern). (9) Primitive reflexes (grasp, palmomental, pout) indicate frontal lobe dysfunction. There are no effective pharmacological treatments for bvFTD — management is supportive (behavioural strategies, carer support, environmental modification, SALT assessment for swallowing difficulties as disease progresses). AChE inhibitors are NOT recommended (may worsen behavioural symptoms). The other main FTD variants are semantic dementia and progressive non-fluent aphasia (language-predominant forms). Bipolar mania is episodic, not progressive.

Subject: Psychiatry / Neurology
Question: An 85-year-old woman with advanced dementia is admitted to hospital with pneumonia. She is treated with IV antibiotics and recovers from the infection. However, her family notice that her cognitive function has deteriorated significantly compared with her pre-admission baseline and has not returned to normal after 3 weeks. What does this represent and what should the family be counselled about?
Option_A: She has developed a new stroke — arrange CT head
Option_B: She was misdiagnosed with dementia — this is purely delirium and she will recover fully
Option_C: The cognitive deterioration is temporary and will fully reverse once the infection has resolved
Option_D: This likely represents a step-change decline in her dementia trajectory accelerated by the delirium — delirium in dementia patients is associated with faster long-term cognitive decline and the family should be counselled that she may not return to her previous baseline
Option_E: This represents normal hospital deconditioning and requires only physiotherapy
Explanation: This is an important and commonly encountered scenario. Delirium superimposed on dementia is extremely common in hospitalised elderly patients and has significant prognostic implications. Evidence shows: (1) Delirium accelerates the trajectory of cognitive decline in patients with pre-existing dementia. (2) Following an episode of delirium, many patients with dementia do NOT return to their pre-delirium cognitive baseline — there is a permanent 'step-change' decline. (3) Delirium in dementia patients is associated with increased risk of: faster progression to severe dementia, increased mortality (up to 2-fold increased risk), prolonged hospital stay, and increased likelihood of institutionalisation. (4) This does not mean recovery is impossible — some patients do improve, but often not to their previous level. The family should be counselled sensitively: (1) Explain that the infection-related delirium has likely caused a step-change in her dementia. (2) She may improve further with time, rehabilitation, and return to a familiar environment, but may not return to her previous level. (3) Discuss ongoing care needs, advance care planning, and support for carers. (4) This is an appropriate time to involve palliative care if not already involved. (5) Ensure comprehensive discharge planning with community support.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 62-year-old man presents to the emergency department with an acutely hot, swollen, and exquisitely painful right knee. He is febrile (38.8°C) and unable to weight-bear. He has a background of type 2 diabetes and a previous episode of gout 2 years ago. Joint aspiration yields turbid yellow fluid. Synovial fluid analysis shows: WCC 65,000/mm³ (predominantly neutrophils), no crystals seen on polarised light microscopy, Gram stain shows Gram-positive cocci. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute gout
Option_B: Haemarthrosis
Option_C: Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate deposition)
Option_D: Reactive arthritis
Option_E: Septic arthritis
Explanation: This is septic arthritis — a medical emergency. The key differentiating features from crystal arthropathy are: (1) Very high synovial fluid WCC (>50,000/mm³ with >90% neutrophils is highly suggestive of septic arthritis, though there is overlap with crystal arthropathies). (2) Positive Gram stain — Gram-positive cocci indicate Staphylococcus aureus, the most common causative organism in non-gonococcal septic arthritis. (3) NO crystals on polarised light microscopy — this effectively excludes gout (negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals) and pseudogout (weakly positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped calcium pyrophosphate crystals). (4) Fever and systemic features are more prominent in septic arthritis. Risk factors include diabetes, immunosuppression, pre-existing joint disease (including gout), prosthetic joints, IV drug use, and skin infections. CRITICAL: septic arthritis and gout can coexist — always send synovial fluid for both crystal analysis AND culture/Gram stain. The synovial fluid analysis is the single most important investigation in the hot swollen joint and is the key discriminator tested in the MSRA.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 2-day history of an acutely swollen, hot, and painful left knee. She is unable to flex the knee and has a temperature of 39.1°C. She has rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate and takes prednisolone 5 mg daily. Blood tests show: WCC 18 × 10⁹/L, CRP 210 mg/L. What is the most important immediate management step?
Option_A: Commence oral colchicine for presumed acute gout
Option_B: Increase her prednisolone dose to treat a flare of rheumatoid arthritis
Option_C: Prescribe oral flucloxacillin and review in 48 hours
Option_D: Request an X-ray of the knee before any treatment
Option_E: Urgent joint aspiration for synovial fluid analysis (microscopy — Gram stain and crystals — and culture) followed by empirical IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin or as per local protocol) if septic arthritis is suspected
Explanation: This is a hot swollen joint in an immunosuppressed patient (methotrexate + prednisolone) — septic arthritis must be excluded as the top priority. NICE CKS and BSR guidelines are clear: (1) URGENT joint aspiration BEFORE starting antibiotics (to obtain synovial fluid for Gram stain, crystal analysis, WCC, and culture). (2) If septic arthritis is suspected (and it must be in any acute monoarthritis with fever), start empirical IV antibiotics IMMEDIATELY after aspiration — do NOT wait for culture results. First-line is IV flucloxacillin 2 g QDS (or vancomycin if MRSA risk or penicillin allergy). (3) Orthopaedic referral for consideration of arthroscopic washout or repeated aspiration. (4) Blood cultures should also be taken. (5) X-ray is of limited value acutely (may show soft tissue swelling or pre-existing joint disease but does not diagnose or exclude septic arthritis). The key teaching point is: aspiration is MANDATORY in any acute hot joint — do not treat empirically for gout without excluding infection, especially in immunosuppressed patients. Delayed diagnosis of septic arthritis leads to joint destruction, sepsis, and significant mortality (10-15%).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 38-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 4-month history of bilateral pain, swelling, and morning stiffness lasting over 90 minutes in her hands and feet. On examination, there is symmetrical synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints, proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints, and metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joints bilaterally. Blood tests show: RF positive, anti-CCP antibodies strongly positive, CRP 45 mg/L, ESR 55 mm/hr. What is the most likely diagnosis and the recommended initial treatment?
Option_A: Fibromyalgia — amitriptyline
Option_B: Osteoarthritis — paracetamol and exercise
Option_C: Psoriatic arthritis — topical treatment only
Option_D: Rheumatoid arthritis — urgent referral to rheumatology and commence DMARD therapy (methotrexate is first-line) within 3 months of symptom onset
Option_E: Systemic lupus erythematosus — hydroxychloroquine alone
Explanation: This is a classic presentation of rheumatoid arthritis (RA): symmetrical, inflammatory polyarthritis affecting the small joints of the hands and feet with prolonged early morning stiffness (>30 minutes indicates inflammatory arthritis), positive RF and anti-CCP antibodies, and raised inflammatory markers. Anti-CCP antibodies are highly specific (>95%) for RA and predict erosive disease. NICE NG100 (rheumatoid arthritis) recommends: (1) URGENT referral to rheumatology if persistent synovitis of undetermined cause — ideally seen within 3 weeks. (2) Start DMARD therapy as soon as possible and within 3 months of symptom onset — early aggressive treatment prevents irreversible joint damage. (3) First-line DMARD: methotrexate (oral or subcutaneous) with folic acid supplementation (5 mg weekly, taken on a different day to methotrexate). (4) NICE recommends a treat-to-target strategy aiming for remission or low disease activity. (5) Short-term bridging therapy with corticosteroids (oral or IM) while waiting for DMARD effect. (6) If methotrexate alone is insufficient, combination DMARDs (methotrexate + sulfasalazine + hydroxychloroquine) or biologic therapy (anti-TNF agents) may be needed.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 50-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis has been started on methotrexate 15 mg once weekly. His rheumatologist asks the GP to arrange shared-care monitoring. Which blood tests are essential for monitoring methotrexate therapy?
Option_A: Blood glucose and HbA1c every 3 months
Option_B: Bone profile and vitamin D levels only
Option_C: Coagulation screen (INR/APTT) monthly
Option_D: Full blood count (FBC) and liver function tests (LFTs) — initially every 2 weeks then monthly once stable — plus U&Es (renal function)
Option_E: Thyroid function tests every 6 months
Explanation: Methotrexate requires regular blood monitoring due to its potential for serious adverse effects: (1) Bone marrow suppression — pancytopenia, particularly macrocytic anaemia, neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia. FBC must be monitored regularly. (2) Hepatotoxicity — elevated transaminases, and rarely cirrhosis with prolonged use. LFTs must be monitored. (3) Renal impairment reduces methotrexate clearance, increasing toxicity risk. U&Es must be monitored. NICE NG100 and shared care protocols recommend: FBC and LFTs every 2 weeks until dose stable for 6 weeks, then monthly. U&Es at baseline and periodically. Patients must be counselled about: (1) ONCE WEEKLY dosing — daily methotrexate is a fatal prescribing error (causes severe pancytopenia). (2) Folic acid 5 mg once weekly (different day to methotrexate) reduces side effects. (3) Avoid alcohol (increases hepatotoxicity). (4) Avoid trimethoprim and co-trimoxazole (increased risk of pancytopenia due to anti-folate synergy). (5) Report immediately: sore throat, bruising, bleeding, mouth ulcers (signs of bone marrow suppression). (6) Effective contraception — methotrexate is teratogenic. (7) Avoid live vaccines while immunosuppressed.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of excruciating pain, redness, and swelling of his right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint. He describes it as the worst pain he has ever experienced and cannot bear even a bedsheet touching his foot. He drinks 30 units of alcohol per week and takes bendroflumethiazide for hypertension. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute gout
Option_B: Cellulitis
Option_C: Osteoarthritis of the first MTP joint
Option_D: Psoriatic arthritis
Option_E: Septic arthritis
Explanation: This is a classic presentation of acute gout — the most common inflammatory arthritis in men. Key features: (1) Acute onset of excruciating pain, swelling, redness, and tenderness — often described as the worst pain ever experienced. (2) First MTP joint (podagra) is the most commonly affected joint (~70% of first attacks), though any joint can be involved. (3) Exquisite tenderness — even light touch is unbearable. (4) Risk factors present: male sex, alcohol excess (beer and spirits in particular — contain purines), thiazide diuretics (reduce renal urate excretion — bendroflumethiazide is a common precipitant). (5) Other risk factors: obesity, chronic kidney disease, high-purine diet, loop diuretics, ciclosporin. Diagnosis: synovial fluid aspiration showing negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate (MSU) crystals under polarised light microscopy is the gold standard. Serum urate may be normal during an acute attack (it is an acute-phase reactant and can fall) so a normal level does NOT exclude gout. First-line acute treatment (NICE CKS/BSR): NSAIDs (e.g. naproxen 750 mg then 250 mg TDS) or colchicine 500 micrograms BD-TDS (if NSAIDs contraindicated) or short course of prednisolone.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 55-year-old man with recurrent gout (4 attacks in the past 12 months) attends his GP. He is currently well with no acute symptoms. His serum urate is 520 μmol/L. His eGFR is 65 mL/min. He has no history of urolithiasis. According to BSR guidelines and NICE CKS, when should urate-lowering therapy (ULT) be initiated and what is the first-line agent?
Option_A: Allopurinol should be started during an acute attack for maximum benefit
Option_B: Allopurinol should be started now — at a low dose (100 mg daily — reduced to 50 mg given his eGFR) and titrated to target serum urate <360 μmol/L — but NOT during an acute attack
Option_C: Febuxostat is always first-line regardless of renal function
Option_D: Lifelong colchicine is the standard urate-lowering therapy
Option_E: No treatment is needed — urate-lowering therapy is only indicated after 10 or more attacks
Explanation: BSR guidelines and NICE CKS recommend urate-lowering therapy (ULT) for: (1) ≥2 attacks in 12 months, (2) tophi, (3) joint damage, (4) renal impairment, (5) uric acid renal stones, (6) use of diuretics that cannot be stopped. This patient has 4 attacks in 12 months — clear indication. First-line ULT: allopurinol (a xanthine oxidase inhibitor). Key prescribing points: (1) Start LOW dose: 100 mg daily (or 50 mg if eGFR <30 — this patient's eGFR of 65 means start at 100 mg but some guidelines suggest starting lower). (2) Titrate gradually (increase by 100 mg every 4 weeks) aiming for target serum urate <360 μmol/L (or <300 μmol/L in tophaceous gout). (3) Do NOT start during an acute attack — wait at least 2-4 weeks after the acute episode has fully settled, as starting ULT can precipitate further attacks. (4) Provide colchicine 500 micrograms OD-BD or low-dose NSAID cover for the first 6 months of ULT to prevent flares during urate mobilisation. (5) Febuxostat (another xanthine oxidase inhibitor) is second-line if allopurinol is not tolerated or contraindicated. (6) Allopurinol can cause severe hypersensitivity reactions — more common in renal impairment and certain HLA types (HLA-B*5801).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 75-year-old woman presents with an acutely swollen and painful right knee. She has a background of osteoarthritis. Joint aspiration yields mildly turbid fluid. Polarised light microscopy shows weakly positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped crystals. What is the diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute gout
Option_B: Basic calcium phosphate deposition
Option_C: Haemarthrosis
Option_D: Pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition disease)
Option_E: Septic arthritis
Explanation: This is pseudogout — calcium pyrophosphate deposition (CPPD) disease. The diagnostic finding is weakly positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped (brick-shaped) crystals on polarised light microscopy. This is the key crystal distinction tested in the MSRA: (1) GOUT: negatively birefringent, needle-shaped crystals (monosodium urate). Mnemonic: Negative Needles for Gout. (2) PSEUDOGOUT: weakly positively birefringent, rhomboid-shaped crystals (calcium pyrophosphate). Mnemonic: Positive, Pseudogout, Pyrophosphate. CPPD typically affects the knee and wrist (unlike gout which classically affects the first MTP). It is more common in elderly patients and is associated with: osteoarthritis, hyperparathyroidism, haemochromatosis, hypothyroidism, hypomagnesaemia, and hypophosphataemia. X-ray may show chondrocalcinosis (calcification of cartilage, particularly the knee menisci and wrist triangular fibrocartilage). Acute treatment is the same as for gout: NSAIDs, colchicine, or corticosteroids (intra-articular or oral). There is no equivalent of urate-lowering therapy — no specific treatment to prevent CPPD deposition. Underlying metabolic causes should be investigated (calcium, PTH, ferritin, transferrin saturation, TFTs, magnesium).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 24-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of pain and swelling in his right knee and left ankle. He also reports a red, painful right eye and pain when passing urine. He had an episode of gastroenteritis (Campylobacter) 4 weeks ago. On examination, both joints are warm and swollen with effusions. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Gonococcal arthritis
Option_B: Gout
Option_C: Reactive arthritis
Option_D: Rheumatic fever
Option_E: Septic arthritis
Explanation: This is reactive arthritis — a sterile inflammatory arthritis triggered by a preceding infection, typically genitourinary (Chlamydia trachomatis — most common urogenital trigger) or gastrointestinal (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia). The classic triad (formerly Reiter syndrome) is: (1) Arthritis — typically asymmetric oligoarthritis (large joints of the lower limbs). (2) Urethritis — dysuria, urethral discharge. (3) Conjunctivitis or anterior uveitis (painful red eye). Additional features may include: circinate balanitis (painless penile lesions), keratoderma blennorrhagica (pustular skin lesions on palms and soles — indistinguishable from pustular psoriasis), mouth ulcers, enthesitis (especially Achilles tendon and plantar fascia), and dactylitis (sausage digit). It is associated with HLA-B27 (present in ~75% of cases). The arthritis is sterile — joint aspiration does not grow the triggering organism (unlike septic arthritis). Management: NSAIDs are first-line for symptomatic relief. If persistent or severe: intra-articular corticosteroid injections, DMARDs (sulfasalazine or methotrexate). The underlying infection should be treated if still active (e.g., azithromycin for Chlamydia). Most cases are self-limiting (resolve within 3-12 months) but ~20% develop chronic arthritis.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 28-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive lower back pain and stiffness that is worst in the early morning and improves with exercise. He is waking in the second half of the night due to pain. On examination, he has reduced lumbar spine flexion (modified Schober test shows <5 cm expansion) and reduced chest expansion. His peripheral joints are normal. An X-ray of the sacroiliac joints shows bilateral sacroiliitis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Ankylosing spondylitis
Option_B: Disc prolapse at L4/L5
Option_C: Mechanical low back pain
Option_D: Osteoarthritis of the lumbar spine
Option_E: Spinal stenosis
Explanation: This is ankylosing spondylitis (AS) — the prototype axial spondyloarthropathy. Key features distinguishing inflammatory back pain from mechanical back pain: (1) Age of onset <40 years. (2) Insidious onset. (3) Morning stiffness >30 minutes improving with exercise. (4) NOT improved by rest (mechanical back pain improves with rest). (5) Waking in the second half of the night due to pain. (6) Improvement with NSAIDs. Examination findings: reduced lumbar spine flexion (Schober test: mark 10 cm above and 5 cm below the dimples of Venus — normal expansion >5 cm on flexion), reduced chest expansion (<5 cm is abnormal — due to costovertebral joint involvement), and loss of lumbar lordosis. Imaging: X-ray shows bilateral sacroiliitis (the hallmark — early erosions followed by sclerosis and eventual fusion). Late: bamboo spine (syndesmophytes bridging vertebral bodies). MRI is more sensitive than X-ray for early disease (bone marrow oedema at sacroiliac joints). Associations: HLA-B27 (>90%), anterior uveitis (25-30%), aortitis, apical lung fibrosis, IgA nephropathy, amyloidosis. Treatment (NICE NG65): first-line NSAIDs, physiotherapy (essential), biologic therapy (anti-TNF — adalimumab, etanercept) if NSAIDs fail.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 42-year-old woman presents with pain and swelling of her right index finger (entire finger appears sausage-shaped) and her left knee. She has had psoriatic plaques on her elbows and scalp for many years. On examination, there are pitting and onycholysis of several fingernails. X-rays of her hands show pencil-in-cup deformities at the distal interphalangeal joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Gout with tophaceous deposits
Option_B: Osteoarthritis with Heberden nodes
Option_C: Psoriatic arthritis
Option_D: Rheumatoid arthritis
Option_E: Systemic lupus erythematosus
Explanation: This is psoriatic arthritis (PsA) — an inflammatory arthritis associated with psoriasis. Key distinguishing features: (1) Dactylitis (sausage digit) — diffuse swelling of an entire finger or toe due to flexor tendon sheath inflammation. This is highly characteristic of PsA and other spondyloarthropathies. (2) Nail changes — pitting, onycholysis (nail plate separation from nail bed), ridging, and hyperkeratosis occur in ~80% of PsA patients (compared with ~40% of psoriasis patients without arthritis). Nail involvement correlates strongly with DIP joint disease. (3) DIP joint involvement — preferential involvement of distal interphalangeal joints distinguishes PsA from RA (which spares the DIPs). (4) Asymmetric oligoarthritis is the most common pattern (~70%). (5) Radiographic pencil-in-cup deformity is characteristic of PsA (erosive remodelling of the DIP joints). (6) Enthesitis — inflammation at tendon/ligament insertion points. (7) RF and anti-CCP are typically NEGATIVE (seronegative arthritis). Five patterns of PsA: asymmetric oligoarthritis (most common), symmetric polyarthritis (mimics RA), DIP-predominant, spondylitis, and arthritis mutilans (severe destructive). Treatment: NSAIDs, DMARDs (methotrexate), biologic agents (anti-TNF, IL-17 inhibitors — secukinumab).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 60-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has been on methotrexate 20 mg weekly and adalimumab (anti-TNF biologic) for 3 years with good disease control. She presents with a 2-week history of cough, night sweats, weight loss, and haemoptysis. A chest X-ray shows a right upper lobe cavity with surrounding consolidation. What is the most important diagnosis to exclude and what action should be taken regarding her immunosuppressive therapy?
Option_A: Continue all immunosuppression — this is likely a simple chest infection
Option_B: Lung cancer is the only concern — refer for CT-guided biopsy
Option_C: Pneumocystis pneumonia — start high-dose co-trimoxazole immediately
Option_D: Stop adalimumab only but continue methotrexate — this is likely drug-induced pneumonitis
Option_E: Tuberculosis must be excluded urgently — withhold adalimumab (and consider withholding methotrexate) and send sputum for AFB smear/culture and arrange urgent respiratory review
Explanation: This presentation (cough, night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis, upper lobe cavitating lesion) in a patient on anti-TNF therapy is tuberculosis until proven otherwise. Anti-TNF agents (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) carry a significantly increased risk of tuberculosis — both reactivation of latent TB and new infection — because TNF-alpha is critical for granuloma formation and maintenance (containing Mycobacterium tuberculosis). This is why ALL patients must be screened for latent TB (with chest X-ray and interferon-gamma release assay/Mantoux test) BEFORE starting anti-TNF therapy. Management: (1) Withhold adalimumab immediately — it should not be restarted until TB is excluded or adequately treated. (2) Consider withholding methotrexate (further immunosuppression). (3) Urgent sputum specimens for AFB smear and culture (3 samples on consecutive days). (4) Chest CT for detailed characterisation. (5) Urgent respiratory/infectious disease review. (6) If TB is confirmed, standard anti-TB therapy (RIPE: rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol). Other infections increased with anti-TNF therapy include opportunistic fungal infections, listeria, and hepatitis B reactivation.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 12-hour history of severe lower back pain radiating down both legs, difficulty passing urine (he has not been able to void for 8 hours), numbness around his perineum and buttocks (saddle anaesthesia), and reduced sensation when wiping after opening his bowels. He lifted heavy boxes at work yesterday. On examination, there is reduced anal tone, bilateral leg weakness (MRC 3/5 ankle dorsiflexion), absent ankle jerks bilaterally, and reduced sensation in the S2-S4 dermatomes. What is the diagnosis and what is the most critical immediate action?
Option_A: Cauda equina syndrome — emergency MRI of the whole spine and urgent neurosurgical/spinal surgical referral for decompression within hours
Option_B: Conus medullaris syndrome — arrange routine outpatient MRI
Option_C: Lumbar disc prolapse — conservative management with analgesia and physiotherapy
Option_D: Lumbar spinal stenosis — outpatient referral to spinal clinic
Option_E: Mechanical low back pain — prescribe NSAIDs and review in 6 weeks
Explanation: This is cauda equina syndrome (CES) — a surgical emergency requiring decompression within hours to prevent permanent neurological damage. CES is caused by compression of the cauda equina nerve roots (below the conus medullaris at L1/L2), most commonly by a large central disc prolapse. The RED FLAGS for cauda equina are: (1) Bilateral sciatica. (2) Saddle anaesthesia (numbness in the perineum and buttocks — S2-S4 distribution). (3) Bladder dysfunction — urinary retention (painless, overflow incontinence) is the most sensitive indicator. (4) Loss of anal tone and faecal incontinence. (5) Progressive bilateral leg weakness. (6) Sexual dysfunction. This patient has ALL of these features. Management: (1) EMERGENCY MRI of the whole spine (within hours — this is one of the very few true spinal emergencies). (2) Urgent neurosurgical/spinal surgical referral for emergency decompressive laminectomy. (3) Catheterise the bladder. (4) The timing of surgery is critical — delayed decompression is associated with permanent urinary incontinence and sexual dysfunction. Other back pain red flags include: age >55 or <20 with new back pain, unexplained weight loss, history of cancer, thoracic pain, fever, immunosuppression, structural spinal deformity, and progressive neurological deficit.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 68-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-year history of worsening pain in both knees. The pain is worse at the end of the day and after prolonged activity, and improves with rest. She has 10-15 minutes of morning stiffness. On examination, there is bony enlargement of both knees with crepitus on movement, reduced range of motion, and small effusions bilaterally. There is no warmth or erythema. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Gout
Option_B: Osteoarthritis
Option_C: Pseudogout
Option_D: Rheumatoid arthritis
Option_E: Septic arthritis
Explanation: This is osteoarthritis (OA) — the most common joint disease worldwide. Key clinical features distinguishing OA from inflammatory arthritis: (1) Pain worse with activity and at the end of the day (mechanical pain) — NOT worse in the morning (inflammatory arthritis causes prolonged morning stiffness >30 minutes). (2) Brief morning stiffness (<30 minutes — often called 'gelling'). (3) Bony enlargement (osteophytes) — Heberden nodes (DIP joints) and Bouchard nodes (PIP joints) are characteristic in hand OA. (4) Crepitus on movement. (5) Reduced range of motion. (6) Joint effusion may be present but is non-inflammatory (cool, no erythema). (7) No systemic features. NICE CG177 (osteoarthritis) states OA can be diagnosed CLINICALLY without investigations if the patient is ≥45 years, has activity-related joint pain, and has either no morning stiffness or morning stiffness lasting <30 minutes. Commonly affected joints: knees, hips, hands (DIPs, first CMC), spine, and first MTP. Risk factors: age, obesity (strongest modifiable risk factor for knee OA), previous joint injury, occupation (manual work), female sex, family history.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 70-year-old man with knee osteoarthritis asks his GP about treatment options. He has tried regular paracetamol with limited benefit. He has a history of peptic ulcer disease and takes aspirin 75 mg daily for cardiovascular prevention. He has chronic kidney disease (eGFR 35 mL/min). What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?
Option_A: Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS — NSAIDs are safe in all patients
Option_B: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection as an adjunct to core treatments
Option_C: Oral codeine 60 mg QDS as a long-term analgesic strategy
Option_D: Oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 2 weeks
Option_E: Start naproxen with a PPI — NSAIDs are the best next option
Explanation: NICE CG177 (osteoarthritis) recommends a stepwise approach: (1) Core treatments for ALL patients (FIRST): exercise and physical activity (most important non-pharmacological intervention), weight loss if overweight/obese (reduces knee OA symptoms significantly), written information and self-management support. (2) Pharmacological: paracetamol and/or topical NSAIDs first-line (topical NSAIDs are preferred for knee/hand OA due to lower systemic side effects). (3) If paracetamol and topical NSAIDs insufficient: oral NSAIDs at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration with a PPI — BUT this patient has TWO contraindications to oral NSAIDs: (a) history of peptic ulcer disease, (b) CKD with eGFR 35 (NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow via prostaglandin inhibition — contraindicated in significant CKD). NSAIDs also interact with aspirin. (4) Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is recommended as an adjunct for moderate-to-severe OA when other measures are insufficient — provides short-to-medium term pain relief (weeks to months). (5) Opioids are NOT recommended for long-term use in OA (NICE) due to limited evidence of benefit, dependence, falls risk in elderly, and adverse effects. (6) Glucosamine and chondroitin are NOT recommended by NICE.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 55-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of low back pain radiating down the back of his right leg to his foot. The pain is sharp and shooting, worsened by sitting and coughing. On examination, the straight leg raise on the right is positive at 30 degrees (reproducing the leg pain). He has reduced sensation over the lateral border of his right foot and weak ankle plantarflexion. His ankle jerk is absent on the right. What is the most likely nerve root involved?
Option_A: L3
Option_B: L4
Option_C: L5
Option_D: S1
Option_E: S2
Explanation: This is an S1 radiculopathy caused by a lumbar disc prolapse (most commonly L5/S1 disc prolapse compressing the S1 nerve root). The key dermatome/myotome/reflex patterns for lumbar radiculopathy: (1) L3: anterior thigh pain, weak hip flexion and knee extension, reduced knee jerk. (2) L4: medial leg/shin pain, weak knee extension and ankle dorsiflexion, reduced knee jerk. (3) L5: lateral leg and dorsum of foot pain, weak ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop) and big toe extension (EHL), no specific reflex loss (or reduced tibialis posterior). (4) S1: posterior leg and lateral/sole of foot pain, weak ankle plantarflexion (calf raise), reduced sensation lateral border of foot, absent ankle jerk. This patient has S1 features: pain radiating to the foot, reduced sensation lateral foot, weak plantarflexion, absent ankle jerk. The straight leg raise (Lasègue test) is positive when pain is reproduced at <60 degrees — it stretches the L5 and S1 nerve roots. Management of uncomplicated sciatica: conservative (analgesia, maintain activity, physiotherapy). Urgent referral for MRI and surgical consideration if: progressive neurological deficit, cauda equina syndrome, or failure to improve after 6-8 weeks.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A GP diagnoses a 72-year-old woman with knee osteoarthritis based on clinical features. The patient asks whether she needs an X-ray. According to NICE CG177, when is imaging required for the diagnosis of osteoarthritis?
Option_A: All patients with suspected OA need an MRI before diagnosis
Option_B: All patients with suspected OA require an X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
Option_C: Imaging is NOT required to diagnose OA if the patient is ≥45 with activity-related joint pain and either no morning stiffness or morning stiffness ≤30 minutes — imaging should be considered if the diagnosis is uncertain or atypical features are present
Option_D: Only blood tests are needed — imaging is never useful
Option_E: X-ray is mandatory before any treatment can be started
Explanation: NICE CG177 states that OA can be diagnosed clinically WITHOUT imaging if: (1) Patient aged ≥45 years, (2) Activity-related joint pain, (3) No morning stiffness OR morning stiffness lasting ≤30 minutes. This is an important teaching point — OA is a clinical diagnosis and routine X-rays are NOT required. X-rays should be considered when: (1) The diagnosis is uncertain (atypical features such as prolonged morning stiffness, young age, rapid onset, significant joint swelling). (2) To exclude other pathology. (3) If considering referral for surgery (pre-operative planning). If X-rays are performed, classic OA features include: Loss of joint space, Osteophytes, Subchondral sclerosis, Subchondral cysts — mnemonic LOSS. However, there is poor correlation between X-ray severity and symptoms — patients with severe X-ray changes may have mild symptoms and vice versa. MRI is NOT routinely indicated for OA diagnosis. Blood tests (RF, anti-CCP, inflammatory markers) should be requested only if inflammatory arthritis is suspected.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 60-year-old woman with severe hip osteoarthritis has failed all conservative measures including exercise, weight loss, paracetamol, topical and oral NSAIDs, and intra-articular injections. She has significant pain and disability affecting her daily activities and sleep. According to NICE guidelines, when should referral for joint replacement surgery be considered?
Option_A: Joint replacement should be offered immediately at diagnosis before trying conservative measures
Option_B: Joint replacement should only be performed when the patient is completely immobile
Option_C: Referral should be considered when OA has a substantial impact on quality of life and is refractory to non-surgical treatment — regardless of age
Option_D: Referral should only be made if the patient is under 65 years old
Option_E: Surgery is never appropriate for hip OA — only knee OA can be replaced
Explanation: NICE CG177 recommends referral for joint replacement (arthroplasty) should be based on the impact on quality of life and functional limitation, NOT on arbitrary thresholds of age, radiographic severity, or BMI. The key criteria are: (1) Joint symptoms (pain, stiffness, reduced function) have a SUBSTANTIAL impact on quality of life. (2) The patient has FAILED non-surgical treatments (core treatments, pharmacological, and non-pharmacological measures). (3) Referral should not be delayed solely because of age, obesity, or comorbidities — these factors may influence surgical risk but should not prevent referral for assessment. Hip and knee replacements are among the most cost-effective interventions in medicine with excellent outcomes: >90% of patients report significant pain relief and improved function. Prosthesis survival is typically >90% at 15 years for hip replacements and >80% at 15-20 years for knee replacements. Pre-operative optimisation (weight loss, smoking cessation, diabetic control, physiotherapy prehabilitation) improves outcomes. Complications include infection, VTE, dislocation (hip), periprosthetic fracture, and prosthetic loosening.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 72-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of bilateral leg pain and heaviness that develops after walking 200 metres and is relieved by sitting down and leaning forward. He also reports tingling in both legs when standing upright. He has no pain at rest. His peripheral pulses are all present and normal. Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) is 1.1 (normal). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Lumbar disc prolapse
Option_B: Lumbar spinal stenosis (neurogenic claudication)
Option_C: Osteoarthritis of the hips
Option_D: Peripheral arterial disease (vascular claudication)
Option_E: Peripheral neuropathy
Explanation: This is lumbar spinal stenosis causing neurogenic (spinal) claudication — compression of the cauda equina within a narrowed spinal canal. Key features distinguishing neurogenic from vascular claudication: NEUROGENIC (spinal stenosis): (1) Pain/heaviness/tingling in BOTH legs on walking. (2) Relieved by sitting AND leaning forward (flexion opens the spinal canal). (3) Can walk further pushing a shopping trolley (flexed posture). (4) Normal peripheral pulses and ABPI. (5) Symptoms may also occur on standing still (prolonged extension). VASCULAR (PAD): (1) Cramping pain typically in calves. (2) Relieved by standing still (does not need to sit). (3) Consistent walking distance to onset. (4) Absent or reduced pulses. (5) Abnormal ABPI (<0.9). This patient has normal pulses and ABPI, relief with sitting/flexion, and bilateral symptoms — classic neurogenic claudication. Lumbar spinal stenosis is usually due to degenerative changes (disc bulging, ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, facet joint osteoarthritis, osteophytes) narrowing the central spinal canal. Investigation: MRI lumbar spine. Management: physiotherapy and analgesia first; surgical decompressive laminectomy if refractory.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of widespread pain affecting both sides of her body above and below the waist, fatigue, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive difficulties ('brain fog'). She has tender points on examination but no joint swelling, warmth, or deformity. Blood tests including FBC, ESR, CRP, TFTs, and autoimmune screen are all normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Chronic fatigue syndrome
Option_B: Fibromyalgia
Option_C: Polymyalgia rheumatica
Option_D: Rheumatoid arthritis
Option_E: Systemic lupus erythematosus
Explanation: This is fibromyalgia — a chronic widespread pain condition characterised by: (1) Widespread pain involving both sides of the body, above and below the waist, plus axial skeleton — present for ≥3 months. (2) Fatigue — often severe and disabling. (3) Unrefreshing sleep/sleep disturbance. (4) Cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog'). (5) Tender points on examination (historically 11 of 18 tender points — though the 2010/2016 ACR criteria no longer require tender point count). (6) NORMAL inflammatory markers and blood tests — this is critical for excluding inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. (7) No objective joint swelling or deformity. Associated features: headaches, irritable bowel syndrome, temporomandibular dysfunction, depression, anxiety. It is NOT an inflammatory or autoimmune disease. NICE CG (chronic pain) recommends: (1) Supervised exercise programme (most effective treatment). (2) Psychological therapy (CBT). (3) Pharmacological options: amitriptyline (low dose 10-25 mg at night), duloxetine, or pregabalin. (4) NSAIDs and opioids are NOT recommended. (5) Avoid repeated investigations once the diagnosis is established. Polymyalgia rheumatica causes shoulder/hip girdle stiffness with markedly raised ESR/CRP.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 78-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of severe bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness for 3 weeks. She can barely raise her arms to brush her hair and has difficulty getting out of a chair. The stiffness is worst in the morning and lasts over 2 hours. She also reports fatigue and weight loss. Blood tests show: ESR 75 mm/hr, CRP 65 mg/L, CK normal. She has no temporal headache or jaw claudication. What is the most likely diagnosis and the first-line treatment?
Option_A: Bilateral frozen shoulder — physiotherapy only
Option_B: Fibromyalgia — amitriptyline
Option_C: Osteoarthritis of shoulders and hips — paracetamol
Option_D: Polymyalgia rheumatica — prednisolone 15 mg daily with dramatic response expected within 1-3 days
Option_E: Polymyositis — high-dose prednisolone and immunosuppression
Explanation: This is polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) — an inflammatory condition predominantly affecting adults >50 years (peak age 70-80). Key features: (1) Bilateral shoulder AND hip girdle pain and stiffness (proximal distribution). (2) Morning stiffness >45 minutes. (3) Systemic features (fatigue, malaise, weight loss, low-grade fever). (4) Markedly raised ESR (>40 mm/hr) and CRP. (5) Normal CK (distinguishes from polymyositis which causes elevated CK due to muscle inflammation). (6) Dramatic and rapid response to low-dose corticosteroids is virtually diagnostic — BSR/BHPR guidelines recommend prednisolone 15 mg daily as the starting dose. Significant improvement within 1-3 days is expected. If no response, reconsider the diagnosis. The CRITICAL association is with giant cell arteritis (GCA) — approximately 15-20% of PMR patients develop GCA, and ~50% of GCA patients have PMR symptoms. ALL PMR patients must be asked about GCA symptoms at every visit: new headache, temporal artery tenderness, jaw claudication, visual disturbance. Steroid regimen: 15 mg daily, then slow taper over 12-18 months guided by symptoms and inflammatory markers. Relapses are common during taper. Consider steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate) for refractory cases.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 68-year-old woman attends her GP for a medication review. She sustained a low-trauma Colles fracture (distal radius) 3 months ago after tripping on a kerb. She has not had a DEXA scan. She is otherwise well, takes no regular medications, and has no contraindications to bisphosphonates. According to NICE TA464, what is the most appropriate next step?
Option_A: Arrange a DEXA scan first — treatment depends on the T-score
Option_B: No investigation or treatment needed — a single fracture is not significant
Option_C: Prescribe calcium and vitamin D supplements alone
Option_D: Start alendronate 70 mg once weekly without waiting for a DEXA scan — she has had a fragility fracture and is over 65
Option_E: Start denosumab as first-line treatment
Explanation: NICE TA464 and NOGG guidelines recommend that women aged ≥65 (and men ≥75) who have sustained a fragility fracture can be started on oral bisphosphonate therapy WITHOUT the need for a DEXA scan. A fragility fracture is defined as a fracture sustained from a fall from standing height or less — this Colles fracture from tripping qualifies. Alendronate 70 mg once weekly is first-line. Key prescribing points: (1) Take on an empty stomach with a full glass of water. (2) Remain upright for at least 30 minutes after taking (to prevent oesophageal irritation/ulceration). (3) Take at least 30 minutes before food, drink, or other medications. (4) Ensure adequate calcium (700 mg/day) and vitamin D (400 IU/day minimum) — supplement if dietary intake is insufficient. (5) Contraindications: oesophageal abnormalities (stricture, achalasia), inability to sit/stand upright for 30 minutes, hypocalcaemia. For younger patients (<65 women, <75 men) with fragility fractures, a DEXA scan should be arranged and treatment guided by the T-score and FRAX score. Risedronate is an alternative if alendronate is not tolerated. Denosumab and IV zoledronate are second-line.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 72-year-old woman has a DEXA scan which reports: lumbar spine T-score -2.8, femoral neck T-score -2.1. What do these results indicate?
Option_A: Normal bone density — no treatment needed
Option_B: Osteomalacia requiring vitamin D replacement
Option_C: Osteopenia — lifestyle advice only
Option_D: Osteoporosis — treatment with a bisphosphonate is indicated
Option_E: These results are inconclusive — repeat in 12 months
Explanation: The World Health Organization (WHO) defines osteoporosis based on DEXA T-scores: (1) Normal: T-score ≥ -1.0. (2) Osteopenia: T-score between -1.0 and -2.5. (3) Osteoporosis: T-score ≤ -2.5. (4) Severe (established) osteoporosis: T-score ≤ -2.5 WITH a fragility fracture. The T-score compares the patient's bone mineral density (BMD) to that of a healthy young adult of the same sex. This patient has a lumbar spine T-score of -2.8 (below -2.5) = osteoporosis. Treatment is indicated. The femoral neck T-score of -2.1 is in the osteopenic range but the diagnosis is based on the LOWEST T-score at any measured site. DEXA scans measure BMD at the lumbar spine (L1-L4) and proximal femur (femoral neck and total hip). Note: T-scores should not be confused with Z-scores (Z-scores compare to age-matched controls and are used in premenopausal women and men under 50). Osteomalacia is a different condition (defective bone mineralisation due to vitamin D deficiency) and is NOT diagnosed by DEXA.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 60-year-old woman is concerned about osteoporosis. She has no history of fragility fractures. Her mother had a hip fracture at age 70. She takes oral prednisolone 7.5 mg daily for polymyalgia rheumatica and has been on this for 4 months. She is a current smoker and drinks 20 units of alcohol per week. According to NICE and NOGG guidelines, which risk factors in this patient are most important for fracture risk assessment?
Option_A: Alcohol intake is the only significant risk factor
Option_B: Family history alone is sufficient to start treatment
Option_C: Glucocorticoid use (≥7.5 mg prednisolone daily for ≥3 months) is the single most important secondary cause of osteoporosis — combined with her other risk factors (family history of hip fracture, smoking, alcohol) she needs FRAX assessment and likely treatment
Option_D: Smoking is the only modifiable risk factor — address this alone
Option_E: She is too young to be at risk of osteoporosis
Explanation: Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is the most common secondary cause of osteoporosis. NICE and NOGG guidelines identify oral glucocorticoids (≥7.5 mg prednisolone equivalent daily for ≥3 months) as a major risk factor requiring proactive assessment and often treatment. This patient has MULTIPLE risk factors: (1) Oral corticosteroid use — the most significant. Bone loss is most rapid in the first 6-12 months of steroid use. (2) Parental history of hip fracture — a strong independent risk factor in FRAX. (3) Current smoking — reduces BMD and increases fracture risk. (4) Alcohol >3 units/day — increases fracture risk. (5) Age and female sex. FRAX (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool) incorporates these risk factors to calculate 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture. NOGG guidelines recommend: patients on long-term glucocorticoids should be assessed with FRAX (with adjustment for steroid dose) and often started on bone protection (bisphosphonate + calcium/vitamin D) WITHOUT waiting for a DEXA scan if FRAX indicates high risk. Treatment thresholds are LOWER for glucocorticoid users.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: An 80-year-old woman is found on the floor of her care home. She is unable to weight-bear on her right leg. On examination, her right leg appears shortened and externally rotated. She has pain on axial loading of the right hip. An X-ray confirms a displaced intracapsular fracture of the right neck of femur. What is the most appropriate surgical management?
Option_A: Conservative management with bed rest and analgesia — surgery is not appropriate at her age
Option_B: Dynamic hip screw (DHS) fixation
Option_C: Hemiarthroplasty (replacement of the femoral head)
Option_D: Internal fixation with cannulated screws
Option_E: Total hip replacement
Explanation: NICE NG111 (hip fracture) provides clear guidance on surgical management based on fracture type: INTRACAPSULAR fractures (within the hip joint capsule): (1) DISPLACED intracapsular fractures: arthroplasty is recommended because internal fixation has high failure rates (30-40% — due to disruption of the blood supply to the femoral head via the medial circumflex femoral artery retinacular vessels, leading to avascular necrosis and non-union). (2) For patients who were INDEPENDENTLY MOBILE and are not cognitively impaired: total hip replacement (THR) is recommended — better functional outcomes and lower revision rates. (3) For patients who are NOT independently mobile, have cognitive impairment, or are medically unfit for longer surgery: hemiarthroplasty is recommended. This patient is in a care home (likely not independently mobile), so hemiarthroplasty is appropriate. (4) UNDISPLACED intracapsular fractures: internal fixation with cannulated screws (blood supply may be preserved). EXTRACAPSULAR fractures: (1) Intertrochanteric: dynamic hip screw (DHS). (2) Subtrochanteric: intramedullary nail. All patients should have surgery within 36 hours of admission (NICE quality standard).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 78-year-old man is admitted with a neck of femur fracture. He is started on alendronate for secondary prevention of osteoporotic fractures. His junior doctor asks about the important counselling points for oral bisphosphonates. Which of the following is correct advice?
Option_A: Alendronate can be taken at any time of day with food
Option_B: Alendronate should be taken after breakfast for best absorption
Option_C: Take alendronate on an empty stomach first thing in the morning with a full glass of plain water and remain upright (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes before eating or drinking anything else
Option_D: Take alendronate with a glass of milk to improve calcium absorption
Option_E: Take alendronate with orange juice to improve bioavailability
Explanation: Oral bisphosphonates (alendronate and risedronate) have very specific administration requirements due to their extremely poor oral bioavailability (<1%) and potential for oesophageal irritation: (1) Take FIRST THING in the morning on an EMPTY stomach (no food for at least 8 hours). (2) Swallow whole with a FULL GLASS of PLAIN WATER (not mineral water, tea, coffee, juice, or milk — calcium and other minerals chelate bisphosphonates and prevent absorption). (3) Remain UPRIGHT (sitting or standing) for at least 30 MINUTES after taking — to prevent oesophageal irritation, ulceration, and stricture. (4) Do not eat, drink anything else, or take other medications for at least 30 minutes after (some guidelines say 60 minutes for alendronate). (5) Do not lie down after taking. (6) Alendronate is taken ONCE WEEKLY (70 mg) — this improves compliance compared to daily dosing. (7) Side effects: GI (dyspepsia, oesophagitis, abdominal pain), musculoskeletal pain. Rare but important: osteonecrosis of the jaw (especially with IV bisphosphonates and dental procedures) and atypical femoral fractures (with prolonged use >5 years — consider drug holiday).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman asks her GP about osteoporosis prevention. She went through menopause at age 48. She has no fragility fractures and no family history of osteoporosis. Her BMI is 22. She takes no regular medications. She asks about hormone replacement therapy (HRT) for bone protection. What is the correct advice regarding HRT and osteoporosis?
Option_A: HRT has no effect on bone density
Option_B: HRT is contraindicated in all postmenopausal women
Option_C: HRT is first-line treatment for established osteoporosis in all women
Option_D: HRT is now banned due to cancer risk and should never be prescribed
Option_E: HRT does maintain bone density and reduce fracture risk during use, but it is not recommended as first-line treatment for osteoporosis — it is considered for bone protection in women with premature menopause (<40) or early menopause who also have vasomotor symptoms
Explanation: HRT (oestrogen with or without progestogen) does maintain bone mineral density and reduce vertebral and non-vertebral fracture risk during use. However, NICE NG23 (menopause) clarifies: (1) HRT is NOT recommended as first-line treatment purely for osteoporosis prevention — bisphosphonates are first-line for established osteoporosis. (2) HRT IS appropriate for women with premature ovarian insufficiency (menopause <40) and early menopause (40-45) — these women should be offered HRT at least until the average age of natural menopause (51) for cardiovascular and bone protection. (3) For women with menopausal symptoms (vasomotor symptoms, mood changes) who also have osteoporosis risk factors, HRT provides dual benefit (symptom relief + bone protection). (4) The bone protective effect is LOST when HRT is stopped — bone density declines rapidly after discontinuation. (5) Risks include: small increased risk of breast cancer with combined HRT (>5 years use), VTE with oral HRT (but not transdermal), and stroke. (6) Benefits and risks should be individualised — the risk-benefit ratio is most favourable when started within 10 years of menopause or before age 60.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 75-year-old woman with osteoporosis has been taking alendronate for 6 years. She presents with sudden-onset pain in her right thigh while walking. There was no trauma. An X-ray shows a transverse fracture of the lateral cortex of the femoral shaft with cortical thickening (beaking) at the fracture site. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Bone metastasis with pathological fracture
Option_B: Insufficiency fracture due to osteomalacia
Option_C: Paget disease with pathological fracture
Option_D: Stress fracture from overuse
Option_E: Atypical femoral fracture associated with prolonged bisphosphonate use
Explanation: This is an atypical femoral fracture (AFF) — a recognised complication of prolonged bisphosphonate therapy (typically >3-5 years). Key features distinguishing AFFs from typical osteoporotic fractures: (1) Location: subtrochanteric or femoral shaft (NOT the femoral neck — which is the typical osteoporotic site). (2) Transverse or short oblique fracture pattern (typical osteoporotic fractures are spiral or comminuted). (3) Cortical thickening or beaking at the fracture site on the lateral cortex. (4) Minimal or no trauma (low-energy). (5) Often preceded by prodromal thigh pain for weeks to months before complete fracture. (6) May be bilateral (check contralateral femur). The mechanism is thought to be suppression of normal bone remodelling (bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclasts), leading to accumulation of microdamage and stress fractures. Management: (1) Stop bisphosphonate immediately. (2) Surgical fixation (intramedullary nail). (3) Check contralateral femur. (4) Consider drug holiday for patients on bisphosphonates >5 years with reassessment of fracture risk. MHRA safety advice recommends reviewing bisphosphonate need after 5 years of oral therapy or 3 years of IV zoledronate.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: An 82-year-old woman is admitted with a hip fracture. She is scheduled for surgery tomorrow morning. She normally takes warfarin for atrial fibrillation (INR today is 2.8) and ramipril for hypertension. She has type 2 diabetes controlled with metformin. Which of the following is the most important pre-operative consideration to ensure safe surgery within the NICE 36-hour target?
Option_A: Cancel surgery until INR is below 1.5 — this may take several days
Option_B: No changes needed — proceed with surgery regardless of INR
Option_C: Reverse warfarin anticoagulation (with vitamin K and/or prothrombin complex concentrate) to achieve INR ≤1.5 and enable surgery within 36 hours — while balancing VTE and stroke risk
Option_D: Stop all medications and give IV insulin for glucose control only
Option_E: Wait for the warfarin to wear off naturally — there is no urgency
Explanation: NICE NG111 mandates that hip fracture surgery should be performed within 36 hours of admission. Delay beyond this is associated with increased mortality, complications (pneumonia, pressure ulcers, VTE), and prolonged hospital stay. For patients on anticoagulants: (1) Warfarin must be reversed to achieve INR ≤1.5 for safe regional or general anaesthesia. Options: IV vitamin K (phytomenadione) 1-5 mg (takes 6-12 hours to reduce INR), prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC — Beriplex/Octaplex) for urgent reversal (reduces INR within 30 minutes). (2) The choice depends on urgency — PCC is preferred if surgery is planned within hours. (3) Balance the bleeding risk of surgery against the thromboembolic risk of stopping anticoagulation. (4) Restart therapeutic anticoagulation post-operatively when haemostasis is secured. (5) Other pre-operative optimisation: pain management (femoral nerve block is recommended by NICE), dehydration correction, medical optimisation (but should NOT delay surgery). (6) Metformin: withhold on the day of surgery and restart when eating and drinking normally. (7) VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (intermittent pneumatic compression) started on admission; chemical (LMWH) started 6-12 hours post-operatively.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 70-year-old woman with osteoporosis has been on alendronate for 3 years. She now reports severe dyspepsia and difficulty swallowing despite taking the medication correctly. An endoscopy shows oesophageal ulceration attributed to alendronate. What is the most appropriate alternative treatment?
Option_A: Calcium and vitamin D alone — no other osteoporosis treatment is available
Option_B: Calcitonin nasal spray
Option_C: Oral risedronate — it has no GI side effects
Option_D: Raloxifene (selective oestrogen receptor modulator) as the only non-bisphosphonate option
Option_E: Switch to either denosumab (subcutaneous injection every 6 months) or IV zoledronate (annual infusion) — both avoid oral GI side effects
Explanation: When oral bisphosphonates are not tolerated due to GI side effects (oesophagitis, ulceration, dysphagia), NICE recommends the following alternatives: (1) IV zoledronate 5 mg as a single annual infusion — avoids GI tract entirely, excellent adherence. Side effects: acute phase reaction (flu-like symptoms for 1-3 days after first infusion), renal impairment risk (check eGFR), hypocalcaemia (ensure vitamin D replete before infusion). (2) Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneous injection every 6 months — a monoclonal antibody against RANKL (inhibits osteoclast differentiation and activity). Side effects: increased infection risk, hypocalcaemia, rebound vertebral fractures if discontinued (must not stop abruptly — transition to a bisphosphonate). (3) Raloxifene (SERM) — reduces vertebral fractures but NOT hip fractures. Side effects: VTE, hot flushes. (4) Teriparatide (recombinant PTH) — reserved for severe osteoporosis with multiple fractures unresponsive to other treatments (specialist initiation only, maximum 2-year course). Risedronate is another oral bisphosphonate and would likely cause similar GI problems. Calcitonin is no longer routinely used. Calcium and vitamin D alone are insufficient for established osteoporosis.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 74-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden painless loss of vision in her right eye. She has had a 3-week history of new-onset right-sided headache, tenderness over her right temple, jaw pain when chewing (jaw claudication), and general malaise. On examination, the right temporal artery is thickened, tender, and non-pulsatile. Blood tests show: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 78 mg/L. What is the most critical immediate management step?
Option_A: Arrange a temporal artery biopsy before starting any treatment
Option_B: Prescribe oral prednisolone 15 mg daily as for polymyalgia rheumatica
Option_C: Refer to ophthalmology outpatient clinic within 2 weeks
Option_D: Start high-dose corticosteroids IMMEDIATELY — IV methylprednisolone 500 mg-1 g daily for 3 days (as visual symptoms are present) followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily — do NOT wait for biopsy results
Option_E: Start paracetamol and arrange an outpatient ESR recheck in 4 weeks
Explanation: This is giant cell arteritis (GCA/temporal arteritis) with visual loss — a medical emergency. GCA is a large vessel vasculitis affecting branches of the external carotid artery, predominantly the temporal artery. Key features: new-onset headache in patients >50 years, temporal artery tenderness and thickening, jaw claudication (highly specific — due to masseter muscle ischaemia), scalp tenderness, constitutional symptoms, markedly raised ESR/CRP. The CRITICAL teaching point: visual loss in GCA is due to anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION) from occlusion of the posterior ciliary arteries. It is often IRREVERSIBLE and can become BILATERAL within days if untreated. BSR/BHPR guidelines mandate: (1) When visual symptoms are present: IV methylprednisolone 500 mg-1 g daily for 3 days IMMEDIATELY, followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily. (2) Without visual symptoms: oral prednisolone 40-60 mg daily. (3) Treatment must be started on clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for temporal artery biopsy. Biopsy should be arranged within 2 weeks of starting steroids (histological changes persist for weeks after steroid initiation). (4) Add low-dose aspirin 75 mg daily. (5) Bone protection (bisphosphonate + calcium/vitamin D) for long-term steroid use. PMR dose (15 mg) is inadequate for GCA.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of fatigue, joint pain, a photosensitive rash across her cheeks and nose, painless mouth ulcers, hair loss, and Raynaud phenomenon. On examination, she has a malar (butterfly) rash sparing the nasolabial folds, non-erosive synovitis of her MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, and bilateral small pleural effusions. Blood tests show: Hb 98 g/L (normocytic), WCC 3.1 × 10⁹/L, platelets 110 × 10⁹/L, ESR 65 mm/hr, CRP 12 mg/L (only mildly raised). Urinalysis shows protein 2+ and blood 1+. Which autoantibody profile is most consistent with this diagnosis?
Option_A: ANA negative, anti-dsDNA negative, complement normal
Option_B: ANA positive, anti-CCP positive, RF positive
Option_C: ANA positive, anti-dsDNA positive, low complement (C3 and C4)
Option_D: ANCA positive (p-ANCA/MPO pattern)
Option_E: Anti-centromere antibody positive
Explanation: This is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) — a multi-system autoimmune connective tissue disease. The key diagnostic features in this case are: (1) Malar (butterfly) rash SPARING the nasolabial folds (distinguishes from rosacea which involves the nasolabial folds). (2) Photosensitivity. (3) Oral ulcers (typically painless). (4) Non-erosive arthritis (unlike RA which is erosive). (5) Serositis (pleural effusions). (6) Haematological: pancytopenia (anaemia, leucopenia, thrombocytopenia) — due to autoimmune cytopenias. (7) Renal involvement (proteinuria and haematuria — suggesting lupus nephritis, the most serious organ manifestation). (8) Raised ESR with NORMAL or only mildly raised CRP — this discordance is characteristic of SLE (CRP rises mainly with infection or serositis in SLE). The AUTOANTIBODY PROFILE for SLE: (1) ANA positive (>95% sensitivity — but low specificity). (2) Anti-dsDNA antibodies — highly specific for SLE (~95%), correlate with disease activity, particularly lupus nephritis. (3) Low complement (C3 and C4) — consumed by immune complex formation, correlates with active disease. (4) Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) antibodies — most specific for SLE but less sensitive. (5) Anti-Ro/La — associated with neonatal lupus, Sjogren overlap, and congenital heart block.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 35-year-old woman presents with episodic colour changes in her fingers triggered by cold exposure. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, then red, associated with numbness and tingling. The episodes last 15-20 minutes and resolve on rewarming. She has no skin changes, joint symptoms, or systemic features. Her blood tests including FBC, ESR, CRP, and ANA are all normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Carpal tunnel syndrome
Option_B: Peripheral arterial disease
Option_C: Primary Raynaud phenomenon
Option_D: Secondary Raynaud phenomenon due to systemic sclerosis
Option_E: Thoracic outlet syndrome
Explanation: This is primary Raynaud phenomenon — episodic vasospasm of the digital arteries in response to cold or emotional stress. The classic triphasic colour change is: WHITE (pallor — vasospasm causing digital ischaemia) → BLUE (cyanosis — deoxygenation of stagnant blood) → RED (reactive hyperaemia on reperfusion). Key distinction between primary and secondary Raynaud: PRIMARY (Raynaud disease): (1) Young women (onset typically 15-30 years). (2) Symmetrical involvement. (3) No tissue damage (no ulceration or gangrene). (4) Normal investigations (ANA negative, normal nailfold capillaroscopy, normal ESR). (5) No underlying connective tissue disease. SECONDARY (Raynaud phenomenon): (1) Onset >30 years or asymmetric. (2) Digital ulceration, scarring, or gangrene. (3) Associated with connective tissue diseases (systemic sclerosis — most common association, SLE, mixed connective tissue disease, dermatomyositis). (4) Abnormal ANA, specific autoantibodies (anti-centromere, anti-Scl-70). (5) Abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy (dilated, distorted capillary loops). Management of primary Raynaud: (1) Conservative: keep warm, avoid cold triggers, stop smoking, avoid beta-blockers. (2) If severe: nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) is first-line pharmacological treatment.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 52-year-old woman presents with progressive skin tightening of her fingers and hands over 18 months, difficulty opening her mouth fully, heartburn, and breathlessness on exertion. On examination, she has sclerodactyly (tight, shiny skin over her fingers), digital pitting scars, telangiectasia on her face, and calcinosis over her finger joints. Her blood pressure is 180/110 mmHg (previously normal). Blood tests show: ANA positive, anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) antibody positive, creatinine 280 μmol/L (previously 85). What is the most serious complication occurring in this patient?
Option_A: Cardiac tamponade
Option_B: Malignant hypertension due to a phaeochromocytoma
Option_C: Pulmonary embolism
Option_D: Scleroderma renal crisis — characterised by accelerated hypertension and rapidly progressive renal failure requiring urgent ACE inhibitor therapy
Option_E: Stroke
Explanation: This patient has diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc) — characterised by widespread skin thickening extending proximal to the elbows/knees, rapid progression, and visceral organ involvement. Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) is the characteristic antibody for dcSSc. The features described include: sclerodactyly, digital pitting scars (healed digital ulcers from vascular insufficiency), telangiectasia, calcinosis — collectively part of the CREST features (though CREST is more associated with limited cutaneous disease). The acute complication is SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS — a life-threatening emergency occurring in ~10% of dcSSc patients (more common with anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies). Features: (1) Accelerated (malignant) hypertension — often abrupt onset. (2) Rapidly progressive renal failure (oliguria, rising creatinine). (3) Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA) and thrombocytopenia. (4) Treatment: ACE inhibitors (captopril) are the specific treatment — they have dramatically improved survival from ~10% to ~65% at 5 years. ACE inhibitors must NOT be stopped even if creatinine continues to rise initially. (5) Other visceral complications of SSc: pulmonary fibrosis (leading cause of death in dcSSc), pulmonary arterial hypertension (more common in limited cutaneous SSc), oesophageal dysmotility, and cardiac fibrosis.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents to the obstetric clinic. She has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages. She also has a history of a deep vein thrombosis in her left leg at age 26, which was unprovoked. Blood tests show: APTT prolonged at 42 seconds (normal 25-35), lupus anticoagulant positive, anti-cardiolipin antibodies (IgG) strongly positive, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies positive. Her platelet count is 95 × 10⁹/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Antiphospholipid syndrome
Option_B: Factor V Leiden thrombophilia
Option_C: Haemophilia A
Option_D: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
Option_E: Von Willebrand disease
Explanation: This is antiphospholipid syndrome (APS/Hughes syndrome) — an autoimmune prothrombotic disorder characterised by: (1) Recurrent arterial or venous thrombosis. (2) Pregnancy morbidity (recurrent miscarriage — typically late first trimester or second trimester, pre-eclampsia, placental insufficiency, IUGR, stillbirth). (3) Persistent antiphospholipid antibodies (at least one positive on two occasions ≥12 weeks apart): lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin antibodies (IgG or IgM), anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies. The PARADOX of APS: despite prolonging the APTT in vitro (due to lupus anticoagulant interfering with phospholipid-dependent clotting assays), patients have a PROTHROMBOTIC state in vivo. Other features: livedo reticularis, thrombocytopenia (mild), cardiac valve disease (Libman-Sacks endocarditis). APS can be primary (isolated) or secondary (associated with SLE in ~30-40% of cases). Management: (1) Thrombosis: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (target INR 2-3, or 3-4 for arterial events). DOACs are NOT recommended. (2) Obstetric APS: low-dose aspirin 75 mg daily from pre-conception PLUS prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy. (3) Catastrophic APS (simultaneous multi-organ thrombosis): medical emergency requiring anticoagulation, high-dose steroids, plasma exchange, and IV immunoglobulin.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of progressively dry eyes and dry mouth. She reports feeling like she has sand in her eyes and has difficulty swallowing dry food without water. She also has fatigue, joint pain, and intermittent bilateral parotid gland swelling. On examination, she has reduced tear production (Schirmer test: 3 mm in 5 minutes — normal >10 mm). Blood tests show: ANA positive, anti-Ro (SSA) antibodies positive, anti-La (SSB) antibodies positive, RF positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Allergic conjunctivitis
Option_B: Sarcoidosis
Option_C: Sjogren syndrome
Option_D: Systemic lupus erythematosus
Option_E: Vitamin A deficiency
Explanation: This is Sjogren syndrome — a chronic autoimmune condition characterised by lymphocytic infiltration and destruction of exocrine glands, particularly the lacrimal and salivary glands. Key features: (1) Keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eyes) — gritty sensation, burning, photosensitivity. Schirmer test measures tear production (<10 mm in 5 minutes is abnormal).
