(2) Xerostomia (dry mouth) — difficulty swallowing dry food, increased dental caries, oral candidiasis. (3) Bilateral parotid gland enlargement (intermittent or persistent). (4) Systemic features: fatigue (often the most debilitating symptom), arthralgia, Raynaud phenomenon, interstitial lung disease, renal tubular acidosis, peripheral neuropathy. Autoantibodies: (1) Anti-Ro (SSA) — present in 60-70%, associated with neonatal lupus and congenital heart block. (2) Anti-La (SSB) — present in 30-40%, more specific for Sjogren than anti-Ro. (3) ANA positive (~80%). (4) RF positive (~60-70%). Primary Sjogren is isolated; secondary Sjogren occurs with other autoimmune diseases (RA, SLE, SSc). IMPORTANT complication: 5-10% lifetime risk of non-Hodgkin B-cell lymphoma (particularly MALT lymphoma of the parotid gland) — monitor for persistent parotid enlargement or lymphadenopathy. Management: artificial tears, saliva substitutes, pilocarpine (muscarinic agonist) for severe symptoms, hydroxychloroquine for systemic features.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 58-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of malaise, weight loss, epistaxis, haemoptysis, and worsening breathlessness. He has also developed a saddle-nose deformity. On examination, he has a purpuric rash on his lower legs. Urinalysis shows blood 3+ and protein 2+. Blood tests show: creatinine 320 μmol/L (previously normal 3 months ago), CRP 85 mg/L. An urgent ANCA test returns: c-ANCA positive, anti-PR3 antibodies positive. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most important immediate investigation?
Option_A: Churg-Strauss syndrome — check eosinophil count
Option_B: Goodpasture syndrome — check anti-GBM antibodies
Option_C: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener) — urgent renal biopsy to confirm rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and guide immunosuppressive treatment
Option_D: Microscopic polyangiitis — this does not cause upper airway disease
Option_E: Polyarteritis nodosa — arrange mesenteric angiography
Explanation: This is granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener granulomatosis) — an ANCA-associated small vessel vasculitis. The classic triad involves: (1) UPPER AIRWAY: saddle-nose deformity (nasal cartilage destruction), epistaxis, sinusitis, subglottic stenosis, otitis media. (2) LUNGS: pulmonary haemorrhage (haemoptysis), cavitating lung nodules, diffuse alveolar haemorrhage. (3) KIDNEYS: rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis — the most life-threatening manifestation (presents with haematuria, proteinuria, rapidly rising creatinine). Serology: c-ANCA pattern with anti-proteinase 3 (anti-PR3) antibodies — highly specific for GPA (~90%). Urgent renal biopsy shows focal segmental necrotising glomerulonephritis with crescents (pauci-immune — few or no immune deposits, unlike lupus nephritis). Treatment: (1) Induction: cyclophosphamide or rituximab PLUS high-dose glucocorticoids (IV methylprednisolone then oral prednisolone). Plasma exchange if severe renal involvement or pulmonary haemorrhage. (2) Maintenance: azathioprine or rituximab with tapering steroids. Without treatment, GPA has >90% mortality at 2 years. Microscopic polyangiitis is p-ANCA/anti-MPO positive and does NOT cause granulomatous upper airway disease.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 48-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of progressive proximal muscle weakness — she has difficulty climbing stairs and raising her arms above her head. She also has a violaceous (heliotrope) discolouration of her upper eyelids and raised, scaly, erythematous papules over her knuckles. Her CK is 4,500 U/L (normal <200). What is the most likely diagnosis and what important associated condition must be screened for?
Option_A: Dermatomyositis — screen for underlying malignancy (age-appropriate cancer screening and CT thorax/abdomen/pelvis)
Option_B: Fibromyalgia — no screening required
Option_C: Polymyalgia rheumatica — screen for giant cell arteritis
Option_D: Polymyositis — no cancer association
Option_E: Statin-induced myopathy — stop the statin
Explanation: This is dermatomyositis — an idiopathic inflammatory myopathy characterised by: (1) Proximal muscle weakness (symmetric, affecting hip and shoulder girdles — difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chairs, lifting arms). (2) Characteristic skin features: HELIOTROPE RASH (violaceous/purple discolouration of the upper eyelids — pathognomonic), GOTTRON PAPULES (raised erythematous/violaceous papules over the knuckles — pathognomonic), shawl sign (V-sign distribution rash on upper chest/back), mechanic's hands (rough, cracked skin on fingers). (3) Markedly elevated CK (often >10x normal — reflects muscle damage). Other investigations: EMG shows myopathic pattern, muscle biopsy shows perifascicular atrophy with inflammatory infiltrate, MRI shows muscle oedema. CRITICAL association: dermatomyositis has a strong association with UNDERLYING MALIGNANCY — approximately 15-25% of adult dermatomyositis patients have an associated cancer (ovarian, lung, breast, colon, pancreatic, gastric, non-Hodgkin lymphoma). Cancer risk is highest in the first 3 years after diagnosis. ALL adult patients require comprehensive malignancy screening: CT thorax/abdomen/pelvis, age-appropriate cancer screening (mammography, colonoscopy), and consideration of PET-CT. Polymyositis has a weaker cancer association. Treatment: high-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg) plus steroid-sparing agents (methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate, or IV immunoglobulin).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 45-year-old woman who works as a secretary presents with a 3-month history of pain, tingling, and numbness in her right hand, predominantly affecting her thumb, index finger, middle finger, and the radial half of her ring finger. The symptoms are worse at night and she is woken from sleep. She describes shaking her hand to relieve the symptoms. On examination, Tinel test and Phalen test are both positive. Thenar wasting is present. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: C6 radiculopathy
Option_B: Carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve compression at the wrist)
Option_C: De Quervain tenosynovitis
Option_D: Trigger finger
Option_E: Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow
Explanation: This is carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) — compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel at the wrist. It is the most common peripheral nerve entrapment. Key features: (1) Pain, paraesthesiae, and numbness in the median nerve distribution — thumb, index finger, middle finger, and RADIAL HALF of the ring finger. (2) Symptoms worse at NIGHT (waking the patient) and with sustained wrist flexion (e.g., driving, holding a phone). (3) Flicking/shaking the hand to relieve symptoms (flick sign — highly specific). (4) Tinel test positive (tapping over the carpal tunnel reproduces paraesthesiae). (5) Phalen test positive (holding wrists in maximal flexion for 60 seconds reproduces symptoms). (6) Late signs: thenar wasting and weakness of thumb opposition and abduction (indicates significant nerve damage). Risk factors: female sex, pregnancy, obesity, hypothyroidism, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, repetitive wrist movements, acromegaly. Investigation: nerve conduction studies (NCS) confirm the diagnosis — showing delayed median nerve sensory and motor conduction across the wrist. Treatment: (1) Wrist splints at night (neutral position). (2) Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel. (3) Surgical decompression (carpal tunnel release) for persistent or severe symptoms with thenar wasting.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of progressive pain and stiffness in her right shoulder. She initially had pain at night and with certain movements, but now she has marked restriction of both active AND passive range of motion in all directions. She cannot reach behind her back or raise her arm above shoulder height. There is no history of trauma. An X-ray of the shoulder is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis
Option_B: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)
Option_C: Cervical radiculopathy
Option_D: Rotator cuff tear
Option_E: Subacromial impingement
Explanation: This is adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) — a condition of progressive fibrosis and contracture of the glenohumeral joint capsule. Key diagnostic feature: global restriction of BOTH active AND passive range of motion in ALL directions (external rotation is typically the most restricted early on). This distinguishes it from: (1) Rotator cuff pathology — active movement reduced but PASSIVE movement preserved (the examiner can move the arm further than the patient can). (2) Impingement — painful arc (60-120 degrees) but full passive range. Three phases: (1) FREEZING (painful phase) — 2-9 months: gradual onset of diffuse shoulder pain, worse at night. (2) FROZEN (stiff phase) — 4-12 months: pain gradually improves but stiffness worsens with marked restriction of movement. (3) THAWING (recovery phase) — 12-36 months: gradual return of movement. Total duration typically 1-3 years. Risk factors: diabetes (10-20% of diabetics develop frozen shoulder — often bilateral and more refractory), thyroid disease, previous shoulder injury/surgery, female sex, age 40-60. Investigation: X-ray is NORMAL (excludes other pathology). MRI shows thickened joint capsule. Management: analgesia, physiotherapy, corticosteroid injection (intra-articular), hydrodilatation, or manipulation under anaesthesia for refractory cases.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 32-year-old new mother presents with pain on the radial (thumb) side of her wrist that has developed over the past 6 weeks. The pain is worsened by gripping and lifting her baby. On examination, there is tenderness over the radial styloid. Finkelstein test is positive (pain reproduced by ulnar deviation of the wrist with the thumb flexed inside the fist). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Carpal tunnel syndrome
Option_B: De Quervain tenosynovitis
Option_C: Scaphoid fracture
Option_D: Trigger thumb
Option_E: Wrist osteoarthritis
Explanation: This is de Quervain tenosynovitis — inflammation of the tendons of the first dorsal compartment of the wrist (abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis) as they pass over the radial styloid. Key features: (1) Pain over the RADIAL side of the wrist (thumb side). (2) Worsened by gripping, pinching, and thumb movements. (3) Common in new mothers (repetitive lifting of babies) — hence sometimes called "mother's wrist" or "baby wrist." (4) Finkelstein test is the key clinical test — positive when ulnar deviation of the wrist with the thumb enclosed in the fist reproduces the radial-sided wrist pain. It stretches the inflamed tendons over the radial styloid. (5) Tenderness and sometimes swelling/thickening over the radial styloid. Differential diagnosis: (1) Scaphoid fracture — follows trauma (fall on outstretched hand), tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox, not over the radial styloid. (2) Carpal tunnel — numbness in median nerve distribution, not radial wrist pain. (3) First CMC joint OA — pain at the base of the thumb, positive grind test. Management: (1) Rest, thumb spica splint, NSAIDs. (2) Corticosteroid injection into the first dorsal compartment (high success rate ~80%). (3) Surgical release if refractory.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department following a motorcycle accident. He has a closed tibial shaft fracture that has been splinted. Over the next 6 hours, he develops excruciating pain in his lower leg that is disproportionate to the injury and not relieved by IV morphine. The pain is significantly worsened by passive extension of his toes. His leg feels tense and swollen. His foot pulses are still palpable. What is the most likely diagnosis and the definitive management?
Option_A: Acute limb ischaemia — urgent embolectomy
Option_B: Compartment syndrome — urgent fasciotomy within 6 hours to prevent irreversible muscle necrosis
Option_C: Deep vein thrombosis — start anticoagulation
Option_D: Fat embolism syndrome — supportive management
Option_E: The pain is expected — increase the morphine dose
Explanation: This is acute compartment syndrome — a surgical emergency. It occurs when pressure within a closed fascial compartment rises to the point where it compromises tissue perfusion, leading to ischaemia and necrosis. CRITICAL: the 5 Ps of compartment syndrome (in order of appearance): (1) PAIN — out of proportion to the injury, not relieved by opioids. PAIN ON PASSIVE STRETCH of the muscles in the affected compartment is the EARLIEST and MOST RELIABLE sign. (2) PRESSURE — the compartment feels tense and swollen on palpation. (3) PARAESTHESIAE — numbness (indicates nerve ischaemia). (4) PARALYSIS — late sign (indicates muscle death). (5) PULSELESSNESS — VERY LATE sign. KEY TEACHING POINT: pulses may be PRESENT even with established compartment syndrome — compartment pressures only need to exceed capillary perfusion pressure (~30 mmHg), not arterial pressure. The presence of pulses does NOT exclude compartment syndrome. Diagnosis is clinical (or confirmed by intra-compartmental pressure measurement >30 mmHg, or within 30 mmHg of diastolic pressure). DEFINITIVE TREATMENT: emergency fasciotomy — all compartments of the affected limb must be released. Delay beyond 6 hours results in irreversible Volkmann ischaemic contracture (upper limb) or muscle necrosis requiring amputation. Common causes: fractures (tibial shaft is most common), crush injuries, tight casts/dressings, burns, reperfusion after vascular repair.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 30-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a fall from scaffolding. He has an open fracture of his right tibia with a 4 cm wound through which bone is visible. The wound is contaminated with soil. After primary survey (ATLS), cervical spine clearance, and stabilisation, what is the specific management protocol for this open fracture?
Option_A: Clean the wound with antiseptic, apply a cast, and discharge with oral antibiotics
Option_B: Close the wound immediately with sutures in the emergency department to prevent further contamination
Option_C: No specific treatment is needed beyond standard fracture management — open fractures are not more serious than closed fractures
Option_D: Photograph the wound, cover with saline-soaked dressing, administer IV co-amoxiclav (within 1 hour), tetanus prophylaxis, and arrange urgent combined orthopaedic and plastic surgery assessment for debridement and stabilisation within 12-24 hours (BOAST 4 guidelines)
Option_E: Take the patient directly to theatre for amputation as limb salvage is not possible
Explanation: Open fractures are orthopaedic emergencies requiring protocolised management. BOAST 4 (British Orthopaedic Association Standards for Trauma) and NICE guidelines recommend: (1) Photograph the wound ONCE (to avoid repeated exposure), then cover with a saline-soaked gauze and an occlusive film dressing — do NOT repeatedly inspect or probe the wound. (2) IV ANTIBIOTICS within 1 hour of injury (time-critical — reduces infection rates significantly): co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV (or clindamycin if penicillin allergic). Add gentamicin for heavily contaminated wounds. Continue for 72 hours or until soft tissue closure. (3) TETANUS prophylaxis — check immunisation status, give booster or immunoglobulin as indicated. (4) Limb realignment and splintage. (5) Urgent combined ORTHOPAEDIC and PLASTIC SURGERY assessment — debridement (removal of contaminated and devascularised tissue), fracture stabilisation (external fixator or definitive fixation), and soft tissue coverage planning. (6) Definitive soft tissue coverage ideally within 72 hours. Gustilo-Anderson classification: Type I (<1 cm wound), Type II (1-10 cm), Type IIIA (>10 cm, adequate soft tissue), Type IIIB (requires flap coverage), Type IIIC (vascular injury requiring repair). Do NOT close open fracture wounds primarily in the ED.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 55-year-old man presents with right shoulder pain for 2 months. He has difficulty reaching overhead and pain lying on his right side at night. On examination, he has a painful arc between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction, pain on resisted abduction (supraspinatus), and positive Neer and Hawkins impingement tests. Passive range of motion is full. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Adhesive capsulitis
Option_B: Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis
Option_C: Cervical radiculopathy (C5)
Option_D: Rotator cuff impingement syndrome (subacromial impingement)
Option_E: Supraspinatus tendon rupture
Explanation: This is rotator cuff impingement syndrome (subacromial impingement) — compression of the supraspinatus tendon and subacromial bursa beneath the acromion during shoulder elevation. Key clinical features: (1) PAINFUL ARC — pain between 60-120 degrees of abduction (the arc where the supraspinatus tendon is maximally compressed under the acromion). Pain resolves above 120 degrees as the greater tuberosity clears the acromion. (2) Positive impingement tests: NEER test (pain on passive forward flexion of the arm with the scapula stabilised) and HAWKINS test (pain on internal rotation of the arm at 90 degrees flexion). (3) Pain on resisted abduction (supraspinatus testing) — weak but POSSIBLE abduction suggests tendinopathy or partial tear, NOT complete tear. (4) FULL passive range of motion — this distinguishes impingement from adhesive capsulitis (which has reduced passive range). (5) Night pain (lying on the affected side). Key distinction from complete rotator cuff tear: in a complete supraspinatus tear, the patient CANNOT initiate abduction (drop arm test positive — cannot slowly lower the arm from 90 degrees). In impingement, active abduction is possible but painful. Management: (1) Physiotherapy (rotator cuff strengthening exercises — mainstay of treatment). (2) Analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs). (3) Subacromial corticosteroid injection if physiotherapy insufficient. (4) Surgical subacromial decompression for refractory cases.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A GP is reviewing autoantibody results for patients with suspected autoimmune rheumatic diseases. Which of the following autoantibody-disease associations is correct?
Option_A: Anti-CCP antibodies — systemic lupus erythematosus
Option_B: Anti-centromere antibodies — diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis
Option_C: Anti-dsDNA antibodies — rheumatoid arthritis
Option_D: Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) antibodies — diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis
Option_E: Anti-Smith antibodies — ankylosing spondylitis
Explanation: The correct autoantibody-disease associations are essential knowledge for the MSRA: (1) RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS: RF (rheumatoid factor — sensitive but not specific), anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide — highly specific ~95%, predicts erosive disease). (2) SLE: ANA (sensitive but not specific), anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with disease activity and nephritis), anti-Smith (most specific for SLE). (3) LIMITED CUTANEOUS SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS (CREST): anti-centromere antibodies. (4) DIFFUSE CUTANEOUS SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS: anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) — associated with pulmonary fibrosis and renal crisis. Anti-RNA polymerase III — associated with renal crisis. (5) SJOGREN SYNDROME: anti-Ro (SSA) and anti-La (SSB). (6) MYOSITIS: anti-Jo-1 (antisynthetase syndrome — myositis, interstitial lung disease, mechanic's hands). (7) MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE: anti-U1 RNP. (8) ANTIPHOSPHOLIPID SYNDROME: lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I. (9) ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS: c-ANCA/anti-PR3 (GPA), p-ANCA/anti-MPO (microscopic polyangiitis, eosinophilic GPA). (10) ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS: HLA-B27 (not an autoantibody but a genetic marker).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A GP reviews X-rays of the hands of two patients with joint pain. Patient A has joint space narrowing at the DIP joints with osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. Patient B has periarticular erosions at the MCP and PIP joints with periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following correctly describes the radiographic findings?
Option_A: Both patients have osteoarthritis
Option_B: Both patients have rheumatoid arthritis
Option_C: Patient A has gout and Patient B has psoriatic arthritis
Option_D: Patient A has osteoarthritis and Patient B has rheumatoid arthritis
Option_E: Patient A has rheumatoid arthritis and Patient B has osteoarthritis
Explanation: X-ray findings in OA vs RA are classic MSRA discriminators: OSTEOARTHRITIS (Patient A): mnemonic LOSS — (1) Loss of joint space (asymmetric narrowing). (2) Osteophytes (bony spurs at joint margins). (3) Subchondral Sclerosis (increased bone density beneath the joint surface). (4) Subchondral cysts. Distribution: DIP joints (Heberden nodes), PIP joints (Bouchard nodes), first CMC joint, hips, knees, spine. NO periarticular osteopenia, NO erosions. RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS (Patient B): (1) Periarticular erosions (marginal erosions — bony defects at the joint margins). (2) Periarticular osteopenia (reduced bone density around the joints — due to inflammatory hyperaemia). (3) Soft tissue swelling (synovitis). (4) Joint space narrowing (symmetric/uniform). (5) Subluxation and deformity in late disease (ulnar deviation at MCPs, swan neck and boutonniere deformities). Distribution: MCP joints, PIP joints, wrists, MTP joints — characteristically SPARES the DIP joints. Other radiographic patterns: GOUT — asymmetric punched-out periarticular erosions with overhanging edges and preserved bone density. PSORIATIC ARTHRITIS — pencil-in-cup deformity at DIPs, periostitis, ankylosis.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 65-year-old woman is investigated for bone pain and fatigue. Her bone profile results show: corrected calcium 3.2 mmol/L (high, normal 2.2-2.6), phosphate 0.6 mmol/L (low, normal 0.8-1.5), ALP 95 U/L (normal), PTH 12.5 pmol/L (high, normal 1.6-6.9). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Bone metastases
Option_B: Osteomalacia (vitamin D deficiency)
Option_C: Paget disease of bone
Option_D: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Option_E: Sarcoidosis
Explanation: This bone profile shows: HIGH calcium, LOW phosphate, HIGH PTH — the classic pattern of primary hyperparathyroidism. PTH-dependent vs PTH-independent hypercalcaemia is a key discriminator: PTH-DEPENDENT (PTH raised): (1) Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common cause of hypercalcaemia in the community) — usually a solitary parathyroid adenoma (85%). PTH is INAPPROPRIATELY raised for the calcium level. Causes: high calcium, low phosphate (PTH increases renal phosphate excretion), normal or raised ALP (if bone involvement). (2) Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia (FHH) — benign, check urinary calcium:creatinine clearance ratio. PTH-INDEPENDENT (PTH suppressed/low): (1) Malignancy — most common cause in hospitalised patients. Mechanisms: PTHrP secretion (squamous cell cancers), osteolytic metastases (breast, myeloma), calcitriol production (lymphoma). (2) Sarcoidosis/granulomatous disease — macrophage-mediated 1-alpha hydroxylation of vitamin D. (3) Vitamin D toxicity. (4) Thiazide diuretics, lithium. Other patterns: OSTEOMALACIA — low calcium, low phosphate, high ALP, high PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism), low vitamin D. PAGET DISEASE — normal calcium, normal phosphate, VERY high ALP, normal PTH.

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A foundation doctor is asked to interpret the results of a FRAX assessment for a 62-year-old postmenopausal woman with no fragility fractures. Her FRAX 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture is 18% and hip fracture is 4.5%. According to NOGG guidelines, what is the recommended next step?
Option_A: Ignore the FRAX result and arrange a DEXA scan for all patients
Option_B: No further action needed — these results indicate very low risk
Option_C: Plot the FRAX result on the NOGG intervention threshold chart — if above the treatment threshold, start treatment; if in the intermediate zone, arrange a DEXA scan to refine the risk estimate; if below the lower threshold, reassure
Option_D: Start alendronate immediately based on the FRAX result alone
Option_E: The FRAX tool is not used in the UK
Explanation: FRAX (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool) calculates the 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture based on clinical risk factors (with or without BMD). NOGG (National Osteoporosis Guideline Group) guidelines use FRAX results plotted against age-specific intervention thresholds: (1) ABOVE the upper (treatment) threshold — treatment is recommended WITHOUT needing a DEXA scan (risk is high enough on clinical factors alone). (2) INTERMEDIATE zone — arrange a DEXA scan and recalculate FRAX WITH BMD to refine the estimate. (3) BELOW the lower (reassurance) threshold — lifestyle advice only, reassess if risk factors change. This traffic-light system ensures efficient use of DEXA scanning — not everyone needs one. Important FRAX limitations: (1) It underestimates risk with very high glucocorticoid doses, multiple fractures, or recent fractures. (2) FRAX adjustments: increase risk for glucocorticoid dose >7.5 mg/day, type 2 diabetes (paradoxically increases fracture risk despite normal/high BMD), and falls risk. (3) FRAX is validated for untreated patients aged 40-90. (4) For patients with fragility fractures, treatment decisions can often be made without FRAX (especially in women ≥65 with fragility fractures — treat directly with bisphosphonate).

Subject: Musculoskeletal
Question: A 40-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of lower back pain. He has no leg symptoms, no bladder or bowel symptoms, and no neurological deficit on examination. He has no history of cancer, no weight loss, no fever, and no night pain. His BMI is 30. According to NICE NG59, which of the following is the most appropriate approach to investigation?
Option_A: Arrange an MRI of the lumbar spine immediately to exclude disc prolapse
Option_B: CT scan of the lumbar spine should be performed within 1 week
Option_C: No imaging is required — reassure that this is non-specific low back pain, provide self-management advice, and encourage return to normal activities. Only investigate if red flags are present or symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks without improvement
Option_D: Order blood tests including ESR, CRP, and PSA urgently
Option_E: X-ray of the lumbar spine to rule out fractures
Explanation: NICE NG59 (low back pain and sciatica) is clear: do NOT routinely offer imaging for non-specific low back pain without red flags. Key points: (1) Non-specific low back pain (no red flags, no neurological deficit, no leg symptoms) is extremely common and self-limiting in >90% of cases. (2) RED FLAGS requiring urgent investigation include: cauda equina symptoms (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, bilateral leg weakness), suspected cancer (history of cancer, unexplained weight loss, age >55 with new onset), suspected spinal infection (fever, IV drug use, immunosuppression), suspected fracture (major trauma, osteoporosis, steroid use), progressive neurological deficit. (3) Routine lumbar spine X-rays are NOT recommended — they expose the patient to significant radiation, frequently show age-related degenerative changes that poorly correlate with symptoms, and rarely change management. (4) MRI is only indicated for: suspected serious pathology (red flags), sciatica not improving after 6-8 weeks of conservative management, or pre-surgical planning. (5) Management of non-specific low back pain: self-management advice (stay active, return to normal activities), avoid bed rest, consider group exercise programmes, NSAIDs short-term, consider psychological therapies (CBT). Do NOT offer opioids, paracetamol alone (limited evidence in back pain), gabapentinoids, or spinal injections for non-specific back pain.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department from a nursing home with confusion, fever (39.2°C), heart rate 118 bpm, respiratory rate 26 breaths/min, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, and oxygen saturations of 91% on air. His NEWS2 score is 12. A provisional diagnosis of sepsis secondary to a urinary source is made. According to NICE NG51 (sepsis), which of the following best describes the immediate management priority?
Option_A: Administer the Sepsis Six bundle within 1 hour — give oxygen, take blood cultures, give IV antibiotics, give IV fluids, measure lactate, and measure urine output
Option_B: Arrange a CT abdomen to confirm the urinary source before starting antibiotics
Option_C: Give oral co-amoxiclav and arrange GP follow-up within 48 hours
Option_D: Obtain a mid-stream urine sample and await culture results before starting antibiotics
Option_E: Start IV fluids and arrange an urgent urology referral before giving antibiotics
Explanation: This patient has sepsis with red flag features (NEWS2 ≥7, systolic BP <90, HR >130 approaching, altered consciousness). NICE NG51 recommends the SEPSIS SIX bundle be delivered within 1 HOUR of recognition: (1) Give high-flow OXYGEN (target SpO2 ≥94%). (2) Take BLOOD CULTURES (before antibiotics but do not delay antibiotics). (3) Give IV ANTIBIOTICS (empirical broad-spectrum — do not wait for culture results). (4) Give IV FLUID resuscitation (500 mL crystalloid stat). (5) Measure serum LACTATE (lactate ≥2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion; ≥4 mmol/L indicates severe sepsis). (6) Measure hourly URINE OUTPUT (catheterise if needed). NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score 2) is a standardised scoring system using physiological parameters (respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, systolic BP, heart rate, consciousness, temperature) to identify acutely deteriorating patients. A score ≥7 or a score of 3 in any single parameter triggers an emergency response. Delaying antibiotics to await imaging or cultures significantly increases mortality — every hour of antibiotic delay in sepsis increases mortality by approximately 7-8%.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 35-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 3-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. She is systemically well with no fever, no loin pain, and no vaginal discharge. She is not pregnant. A urine dipstick shows nitrites positive and leucocytes 3+. What is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Prescribe a 3-day course of nitrofurantoin 100 mg modified-release BD without sending a urine culture
Option_B: Prescribe trimethoprim 200 mg BD for 14 days
Option_C: Request a mid-stream urine for culture and sensitivity before starting any antibiotic
Option_D: Request an ultrasound of the renal tract before treatment
Option_E: Start IV co-amoxiclav and arrange hospital admission
Explanation: This is an uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis) in a non-pregnant woman. NICE NG109 (UTI — lower) recommends: (1) Empirical antibiotics can be started based on symptoms and dipstick without sending a urine culture in uncomplicated cases. (2) First-line: nitrofurantoin 100 mg MR BD for 3 days (if eGFR ≥45). (3) Second-line: trimethoprim 200 mg BD for 3 days (if low risk of resistance). (4) Pivmecillinam is an alternative. Urine culture SHOULD be sent in: complicated UTI, recurrent UTI, treatment failure, pregnant women, men, children, catheterised patients, or atypical symptoms. This patient has classic uncomplicated cystitis — no red flags for pyelonephritis (no fever, no loin pain, no rigors) and is not pregnant. Trimethoprim for 14 days is far too long. IV antibiotics are not indicated for simple cystitis. Renal tract imaging is not needed for a single episode of uncomplicated UTI.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 78-year-old woman on a medical ward develops profuse watery diarrhoea (6-8 episodes/day) 5 days after starting co-amoxiclav for a chest infection. She has a temperature of 38.1°C and mild abdominal tenderness. A stool sample for Clostridioides difficile toxin is positive. According to NICE and PHE guidance, what is the most appropriate initial management?
Option_A: Add metronidazole IV to the current antibiotic regimen
Option_B: Continue co-amoxiclav and add loperamide to control diarrhoea
Option_C: Stop co-amoxiclav, start oral vancomycin 125 mg QDS for 10 days, implement contact precautions, and review all other antibiotics
Option_D: Stop co-amoxiclav and observe without specific C. difficile treatment
Option_E: Switch to oral metronidazole 400 mg TDS as first-line C. difficile treatment
Explanation: This is Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI). The key management principles are: (1) STOP the causative antibiotic (co-amoxiclav is a high-risk antibiotic for CDI along with clindamycin, cephalosporins, and fluoroquinolones). (2) First-line treatment: ORAL VANCOMYCIN 125 mg QDS for 10 days — this is now preferred over metronidazole as first-line per updated PHE/NICE guidance, as vancomycin has superior cure rates. Metronidazole 400 mg TDS for 10 days is an alternative if vancomycin is unavailable. (3) Fidaxomicin is recommended for recurrent CDI. (4) Implement INFECTION CONTROL measures: contact (enteric) precautions, side room isolation, hand washing with SOAP AND WATER (alcohol gel does NOT kill C. difficile spores), enhanced environmental cleaning with chlorine-based agents. (5) Do NOT use loperamide or other anti-motility agents — these increase the risk of toxic megacolon. (6) Review and rationalise ALL antibiotics. Severity markers include: WCC >15, rising creatinine, temperature >38.5°C, signs of colitis on imaging. Severe/life-threatening CDI may require IV metronidazole PLUS oral/rectal vancomycin and surgical review.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 22-year-old university student presents with a 2-day history of severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, fever (39.5°C), and a non-blanching petechial rash on his trunk and limbs. He is drowsy (GCS 13). What is the single most important immediate action?
Option_A: Arrange a CT head before any treatment
Option_B: Give IV ceftriaxone 2g immediately without waiting for investigation results
Option_C: Perform a lumbar puncture to confirm the diagnosis before starting antibiotics
Option_D: Request blood cultures and wait for results before deciding on antibiotics
Option_E: Start oral amoxicillin and observe overnight
Explanation: This is meningococcal meningitis/septicaemia — a medical EMERGENCY. The non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash with meningism is highly suggestive of Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcal disease). NICE CG102 (meningitis — bacterial) and the Meningitis Research Foundation recommend: (1) Give IV/IM CEFTRIAXONE immediately (2g in adults) — do NOT delay for any investigations. In the community (GP/paramedic), give IM benzylpenicillin if available. (2) Do NOT perform lumbar puncture if: raised ICP signs, GCS <12, focal neurology, coagulopathy, cardiovascular instability, or extensive purpura. This patient has GCS 13 and purpuric rash — LP is contraindicated initially. (3) Blood cultures should be taken but MUST NOT delay antibiotic administration. (4) CT head is not required before antibiotics — it may be needed before LP but LP itself should not delay treatment. (5) Notify Public Health England — close contacts require chemoprophylaxis (ciprofloxacin single dose or rifampicin 2 days). (6) Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg QDS for 4 days should be given with or before the first dose of antibiotics if bacterial meningitis is suspected (reduces complications, especially hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of productive cough with rust-coloured sputum, fever, pleuritic chest pain, and breathlessness. His observations show: temperature 38.8°C, respiratory rate 28, heart rate 105, blood pressure 105/65 mmHg, SpO2 93% on air, and confusion (new). His CURB-65 score is 4. Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. What is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Discharge with oral amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days
Option_B: Admit to a general medical ward and start IV amoxicillin alone
Option_C: Admit to HDU/ICU, start IV co-amoxiclav AND IV clarithromycin, and consider ITU review
Option_D: Prescribe oral doxycycline and arrange GP review in 48 hours
Option_E: Request a CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude PE before treating
Explanation: This is severe community-acquired pneumonia (CAP). The CURB-65 score predicts 30-day mortality: Confusion (new), Urea >7 mmol/L, Respiratory rate ≥30, Blood pressure (systolic <90 or diastolic ≤60), age ≥65. Each scores 1 point. NICE CG191 (pneumonia in adults) and BTS guidelines recommend: CURB-65 0-1 (low severity, <3% mortality): treat in community with oral amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 5 days (first-line) or doxycycline/clarithromycin if penicillin-allergic. CURB-65 2 (moderate severity, 3-15% mortality): consider hospital admission, oral or IV amoxicillin ± oral macrolide. CURB-65 3-5 (high severity, >15% mortality): urgent hospital admission (consider HDU/ICU), IV dual antibiotic therapy — typically IV co-amoxiclav (or benzylpenicillin) PLUS IV clarithromycin (a macrolide covers atypical organisms: Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila). This patient scores 4 (confusion, RR 28 approaches 30, BP borderline low, likely urea raised) — this is severe CAP requiring IV dual therapy and HDU/ICU-level care. Monotherapy is insufficient for severe CAP.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents to her GP with a painful, erythematous, warm, swollen left lower leg. She has a temperature of 38.3°C. There is a well-demarcated area of erythema over the shin with no fluctuance or crepitus. She has no significant past medical history and no drug allergies. She is haemodynamically stable. According to NICE guidelines, what is the first-line antibiotic for uncomplicated cellulitis?
Option_A: Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS for 5-7 days
Option_B: IV vancomycin and urgent surgical debridement
Option_C: Co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS for 14 days
Option_D: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD for 7 days
Option_E: Topical fusidic acid applied three times daily
Explanation: NICE NG141 (cellulitis and erysipelas) recommends: (1) First-line for non-severe cellulitis: oral FLUCLOXACILLIN 500 mg QDS (up to 1g QDS in severe infection) for 5-7 days. (2) If penicillin-allergic: clarithromycin 500 mg BD or doxycycline 200 mg on day 1 then 100 mg OD. (3) Cellulitis is predominantly caused by Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Strep) and Staphylococcus aureus — flucloxacillin covers both. (4) Mark the margins of erythema with a skin marker to monitor progression/response. (5) Eron classification helps guide setting of care: Class I (no systemic toxicity, no comorbidities) — outpatient oral antibiotics. Class II (systemically unwell or comorbidity) — may need initial IV then switch to oral. Class III/IV (severe sepsis, necrotising fasciitis) — IV antibiotics and surgical review. (6) Consider IV antibiotics if: systemically unwell, rapidly spreading, periorbital cellulitis, or failed oral therapy after 48 hours. (7) Elevate the affected limb to reduce oedema. (8) Topical antibiotics are NOT appropriate for cellulitis. Co-amoxiclav is not first-line for simple cellulitis.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 19-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 10-day history of sore throat, fatigue, malaise, and bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. On examination, she has tonsillar exudate, palatal petechiae, and tender splenomegaly. A blood film shows atypical lymphocytes. She was prescribed amoxicillin by an out-of-hours service 2 days ago and has now developed a widespread maculopapular rash. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute HIV seroconversion illness
Option_B: Drug allergy to amoxicillin (Type I hypersensitivity)
Option_C: Infectious mononucleosis (Epstein-Barr virus) with amoxicillin-induced rash
Option_D: Scarlet fever
Option_E: Streptococcal tonsillitis with secondary drug eruption
Explanation: This is infectious mononucleosis (glandular fever) caused by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV). The classic triad is: sore throat with tonsillar exudate, lymphadenopathy (especially posterior cervical), and fatigue/malaise. Key diagnostic features: (1) Atypical lymphocytes on blood film (activated T cells). (2) Positive heterophile antibody test (Monospot/Paul-Bunnell) — may be negative in first week. (3) EBV-specific serology (IgM to viral capsid antigen) confirms diagnosis. (4) Splenomegaly occurs in ~50% — patients should AVOID contact sports for 6-8 weeks due to risk of splenic rupture. The AMOXICILLIN-INDUCED RASH is a characteristic feature: 90-100% of patients with EBV who receive amoxicillin (or ampicillin) develop a widespread maculopapular rash. This is NOT a true penicillin allergy — it is a transient immune-mediated reaction specific to EBV infection, and these patients can safely receive penicillins in the future. It is important NOT to label these patients as penicillin-allergic. Management of EBV is supportive: rest, fluids, paracetamol/ibuprofen (avoid if splenomegaly), no antibiotics. Complications include splenic rupture, hepatitis, airway obstruction, and rarely Guillain-Barré syndrome.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 68-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and peripheral vascular disease presents with a 2-week history of a painful right foot ulcer that has not responded to oral flucloxacillin. On examination, the ulcer is 3 cm with surrounding erythema extending 5 cm, purulent discharge, and exposure of the underlying tendon. His temperature is 38.5°C and he has a WCC of 18 × 10⁹/L. What feature makes this a limb-threatening diabetic foot infection requiring urgent hospital admission?
Option_A: Exposure of underlying tendon (deep tissue involvement) with systemic features
Option_B: He has diabetes, so all foot infections are limb-threatening
Option_C: The erythema extending 5 cm indicates superficial cellulitis only
Option_D: The ulcer has been present for 2 weeks, suggesting chronicity rather than emergency
Option_E: WCC of 18 is mildly elevated and does not indicate severity
Explanation: NICE NG19 (diabetic foot problems) classifies diabetic foot infections by severity: MILD: superficial, erythema <2 cm from wound edges, no systemic signs. MODERATE: erythema >2 cm, or involving deeper structures (fascia, tendon, bone, joint), without systemic inflammatory response. SEVERE: any foot infection with systemic signs (fever, tachycardia, raised WCC, metabolic derangement) OR deep tissue involvement with systemic features. This patient has SEVERE infection based on: (1) Deep structure involvement — exposed tendon. (2) Systemic features — fever 38.5°C and WCC 18. (3) Failed oral therapy. Management of severe diabetic foot infection: (1) Urgent hospital admission. (2) IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering Gram-positives, Gram-negatives, and anaerobes — e.g., IV piperacillin-tazobactam or IV co-amoxiclav + IV metronidazole). (3) Urgent assessment by the multidisciplinary diabetic foot team within 24 hours. (4) Vascular assessment (ABPI, duplex) — PVD worsens prognosis. (5) X-ray foot to assess for osteomyelitis. (6) Deep wound swab/tissue sample for culture. (7) Optimise glycaemic control. (8) Consider surgical debridement if abscess, necrosis, or gas gangrene.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 3-year-old child is brought to the GP by her mother with a 2-day history of fever (38.7°C), irritability, and a widespread rash. The rash started on the trunk and consists of crops of lesions at different stages — macules, papules, vesicles, and crusted lesions coexisting simultaneously. The child is otherwise well, drinking fluids, and attending nursery. What is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Admit to hospital for IV aciclovir
Option_B: Prescribe oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7 days
Option_C: Prescribe oral antibiotics to prevent secondary bacterial infection
Option_D: Supportive care with paracetamol for fever, calamine lotion, and advise exclusion from nursery until all lesions have crusted over
Option_E: Vaccinate all close contacts immediately with the varicella vaccine
Explanation: This is chickenpox (varicella zoster virus). The hallmark clinical feature is a rash with lesions at DIFFERENT STAGES simultaneously (crops) — macules, papules, vesicles, pustules, and crusts. The rash typically starts on the trunk and spreads centrifugally. Management in an otherwise healthy child: (1) SUPPORTIVE CARE — paracetamol for fever and discomfort (AVOID ibuprofen in chickenpox — associated with increased risk of necrotising fasciitis and more severe skin complications). (2) Calamine lotion and cool baths for itch. (3) Keep fingernails short to prevent scratching and secondary bacterial infection. (4) Exclusion from nursery/school until ALL lesions have crusted over (usually day 5-6 of rash). (5) Oral aciclovir is NOT routinely recommended for healthy children with chickenpox. Aciclovir IS indicated for: immunocompromised patients, neonates, adults (more severe disease), pregnant women, and those with complications. IV aciclovir is reserved for severe/complicated chickenpox (pneumonitis, encephalitis, immunocompromised). (6) Varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) is given to high-risk exposed contacts (immunocompromised, neonates, pregnant women without immunity) — NOT routine post-exposure prophylaxis.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 52-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents to the emergency department with a 4-day history of cough productive of foul-smelling, blood-streaked sputum, fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Chest X-ray shows a thick-walled cavity in the right upper lobe with an air-fluid level. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Cavitating squamous cell lung carcinoma
Option_B: Lung abscess
Option_C: Pulmonary embolism with infarction
Option_D: Pulmonary tuberculosis
Option_E: Wegener granulomatosis (granulomatosis with polyangiitis)
Explanation: This is a lung abscess — a localised collection of pus within the lung parenchyma with cavity formation. Key features: (1) FOUL-SMELLING sputum — this is characteristic and indicates anaerobic infection. (2) Thick-walled cavity with AIR-FLUID LEVEL on CXR. (3) Subacute presentation with fever, night sweats, weight loss. Risk factors include: aspiration (alcoholism — most common cause — reduced consciousness, poor dentition, dysphagia), obstruction (bronchial carcinoma), and septic emboli (IV drug use, right-sided endocarditis). Common organisms: anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Peptostreptococcus), Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae (especially in alcoholics). Management: prolonged IV antibiotics (4-6 weeks — typically co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam ± metronidazole for anaerobic cover), postural drainage, and CT-guided drainage or surgery if not responding. TB can cause cavitation but typically in the apex without air-fluid level and with acid-fast bacilli. Cavitating squamous cell carcinoma lacks the foul-smelling sputum and acute infective features.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 28-year-old man returns from a trip to Sub-Saharan Africa and presents to the emergency department 10 days later with cyclical rigors, high fevers (40°C), profuse sweating, headache, and myalgia. Blood tests show: Hb 105 g/L, platelets 85 × 10⁹/L, bilirubin 45 μmol/L. Three thick and thin blood films are requested. What is the most important initial step while awaiting film results?
Option_A: Assume a viral illness and discharge with paracetamol
Option_B: Prescribe empirical doxycycline for possible rickettsial disease
Option_C: Request a malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) AND do not delay treatment — if clinical suspicion is high and parasitaemia >2%, treat as severe malaria with IV artesunate
Option_D: Send dengue serology and observe
Option_E: Start empirical quinine IV for all febrile travellers
Explanation: Malaria must be considered in ANY febrile patient returning from an endemic area — it is a medical emergency with significant mortality if diagnosis and treatment are delayed. This patient has classic features: cyclical rigors, fever, sweating (paroxysms correspond to erythrocyte lysis cycles), anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and raised bilirubin (haemolysis). Diagnosis: (1) THICK and THIN blood films — thick film detects parasites (more sensitive), thin film identifies species and quantifies parasitaemia. (2) Three films should be taken over 48-72 hours if initial films negative. (3) Rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) detect parasite antigens and provide faster results. Plasmodium falciparum is the most dangerous species (causes >95% of malaria deaths). Markers of SEVERE falciparum malaria include: parasitaemia >2%, cerebral malaria (altered consciousness), severe anaemia (Hb <80), renal failure, pulmonary oedema/ARDS, hypoglycaemia, metabolic acidosis, DIC. Management: UNCOMPLICATED falciparum — artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) e.g., artemether-lumefantrine (Riamet). SEVERE falciparum — IV ARTESUNATE (first-line, superior to IV quinine) with close HDU/ICU monitoring. Non-falciparum malaria — chloroquine (plus primaquine for P. vivax/ovale to eliminate liver hypnozoites).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 75-year-old woman in a care home develops acute-onset vomiting and watery diarrhoea. Over the next 24 hours, 12 other residents and 3 staff members develop identical symptoms. The vomiting is projectile and preceded the diarrhoea. Symptoms self-resolve within 48-72 hours. Stool cultures for bacterial pathogens are negative. What is the most likely causative organism and the key infection control measure?
Option_A: Campylobacter jejuni — prescribe empirical erythromycin for all affected individuals
Option_B: Clostridioides difficile — isolate patients and start oral vancomycin
Option_C: Escherichia coli O157 — notify Public Health England and send stool for culture
Option_D: Norovirus — isolate affected individuals, enforce hand washing with soap and water (alcohol gel is ineffective), and close the ward/facility to new admissions for 48 hours after the last case
Option_E: Salmonella enteritidis — identify and remove the contaminated food source
Explanation: This is norovirus (winter vomiting bug) — the most common cause of gastroenteritis outbreaks in closed/semi-closed settings (care homes, hospitals, cruise ships, schools). Key features: (1) Highly contagious — explosive outbreaks with high attack rate (affects staff AND patients). (2) Projectile VOMITING is the predominant symptom (often precedes diarrhoea). (3) Short incubation period (12-48 hours). (4) Self-limiting — symptoms resolve within 48-72 hours. (5) Negative bacterial stool cultures (viral cause). (6) Diagnosis: clinical (PCR of stool if confirmation needed). Infection control is CRITICAL: (1) Isolate affected individuals (cohort nursing). (2) Hand washing with SOAP AND WATER — alcohol gel does NOT effectively kill norovirus (non-enveloped virus). (3) Enhanced environmental cleaning with hypochlorite (bleach-based) solutions. (4) Close ward/facility to new admissions for 48 hours after the last case. (5) Staff exclusion for 48 hours after symptom resolution. (6) No specific antiviral treatment — management is supportive (oral/IV rehydration). Campylobacter and Salmonella have longer incubation periods and more prolonged illness. C. difficile is antibiotic-associated. E. coli O157 causes bloody diarrhoea.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A foundation doctor is prescribing antibiotics for a patient with community-acquired pneumonia. The hospital antimicrobial stewardship team has implemented a 'Start Smart — Then Focus' strategy. According to PHE guidance, which of the following best describes this approach?
Option_A: Prescribe the broadest-spectrum antibiotic available to ensure all possible organisms are covered, and continue for a minimum of 14 days regardless of clinical response
Option_B: Start empirical antibiotics based on local guidelines and clinical indication, then review at 48-72 hours to stop, switch (IV to oral), change, or continue antibiotics based on clinical response and microbiology results
Option_C: Start narrow-spectrum antibiotics and only broaden if the patient deteriorates, without reviewing microbiology results
Option_D: Use antibiotics only after culture and sensitivity results are available, even if this causes a delay of 48-72 hours
Option_E: Withhold antibiotics for the first 24 hours to allow a period of observation
Explanation: PHE 'Start Smart — Then Focus' is the cornerstone of antimicrobial stewardship in the UK. It has two phases: START SMART: (1) Only prescribe antibiotics when there is a clear clinical indication. (2) Take cultures BEFORE starting antibiotics (but do not delay treatment). (3) Prescribe according to LOCAL antimicrobial guidelines. (4) Document the indication, dose, route, and planned duration on the drug chart. (5) Use the shortest effective course. THEN FOCUS (at 48-72 hour review — the 'antimicrobial review'): (1) STOP if no evidence of infection. (2) SWITCH from IV to oral (IVOST — IV to oral switch therapy) if clinically improving, afebrile for 24-48 hours, and able to tolerate oral medication. (3) CHANGE to a narrower-spectrum antibiotic based on culture results (de-escalation). (4) CONTINUE only if there is a clear ongoing indication and document the planned duration. (5) Consider outpatient parenteral antibiotic therapy (OPAT) where appropriate. This structured approach reduces unnecessary antibiotic use, minimises resistance development, and reduces C. difficile risk.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 60-year-old man with community-acquired pneumonia has been receiving IV co-amoxiclav for 48 hours. He is now afebrile for 24 hours, his CRP is falling, his respiratory rate is 16, and he is eating and drinking normally. His oral absorption is not impaired. According to antimicrobial stewardship principles, what is the most appropriate next step?
Option_A: Complete a further 5 days of IV antibiotics before considering any change
Option_B: Continue IV co-amoxiclav until the CRP has fully normalised
Option_C: Discontinue all antibiotics immediately as the patient is improving
Option_D: Switch from IV to oral co-amoxiclav (IV-to-oral switch) and plan for discharge
Option_E: Switch to a broader-spectrum IV antibiotic such as meropenem to ensure complete eradication
Explanation: IV-to-oral switch (IVOST) is a key antimicrobial stewardship intervention. Criteria for switching from IV to oral antibiotics include: (1) Clinical improvement — afebrile for 24-48 hours, improving symptoms. (2) Falling inflammatory markers (CRP trending down — does NOT need to be normal). (3) Able to tolerate oral medication (eating, drinking, no vomiting, no impaired GI absorption). (4) No ongoing indication for IV route (e.g., meningitis, endocarditis, deep-seated abscess without source control require prolonged IV). Benefits of early IVOST: reduces line-related infections (phlebitis, line sepsis), reduces hospital length of stay, reduces healthcare costs, and improves patient comfort. This patient meets ALL criteria for IVOST. Continuing unnecessary IV antibiotics exposes the patient to line complications without additional benefit. Broadening the spectrum would be inappropriate — de-escalation, not escalation, is the stewardship principle when a patient is improving.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A hospital microbiologist reviews the quarterly antibiotic consumption data and notes a significant increase in ciprofloxacin prescribing across the trust. She is concerned about the impact on antimicrobial resistance. Which of the following statements about antibiotic resistance is most accurate?
Option_A: Antibiotic resistance develops only through spontaneous mutations and is unrelated to antibiotic prescribing patterns
Option_B: Broad-spectrum antibiotics carry a lower risk of promoting resistance than narrow-spectrum agents because they eliminate more organisms
Option_C: Fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) are classified as 'high-risk' antibiotics for driving Clostridioides difficile infection and selecting for resistant organisms including MRSA and resistant Gram-negative bacteria
Option_D: Restricting antibiotic prescribing has no impact on resistance rates once resistance genes have been established
Option_E: The main mechanism of antibiotic resistance is inadequate dosing rather than inappropriate prescribing
Explanation: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) are classified as HIGH-RISK antibiotics by PHE for several reasons: (1) Strong association with C. difficile infection — fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins, clindamycin, and co-amoxiclav are the highest-risk antibiotic classes. (2) Selection pressure for resistant organisms including MRSA, extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing Gram-negative bacteria, and fluoroquinolone-resistant E. coli. (3) Broad-spectrum activity disrupts the normal gut microbiome significantly. PHE antimicrobial stewardship targets include reducing total antibiotic consumption AND specifically reducing the proportion of high-risk broad-spectrum antibiotics. MHRA safety warnings for fluoroquinolones also include serious side effects: tendon rupture (especially in elderly and those on corticosteroids), peripheral neuropathy, and aortic aneurysm/dissection. Antibiotic resistance mechanisms include: horizontal gene transfer (plasmids, transposons — most important mechanism for spread), spontaneous mutations, and selective pressure from antibiotic use. Reducing antibiotic consumption DOES reduce resistance rates over time — this is well-evidenced in stewardship programmes.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A GP sees a 32-year-old woman with a 3-day history of sore throat, odynophagia, and mild fever (37.8°C). She has tonsillar exudate but no cough, no rhinorrhoea, and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Her FeverPAIN score is 4. She has no red flags. According to NICE NG84 (sore throat — acute), what is the most appropriate management?
Option_A: Immediate prescription of phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg QDS for 10 days
Option_B: No antibiotic prescription — advise supportive care only regardless of the score
Option_C: Offer a back-up (delayed) antibiotic prescription — phenoxymethylpenicillin 500 mg QDS for 5-10 days — to be used if symptoms do not improve within 3-5 days, alongside advice on self-care and analgesia
Option_D: Prescribe azithromycin 500 mg OD for 3 days as first-line treatment
Option_E: Refer urgently to ENT for tonsillectomy
Explanation: NICE NG84 (sore throat) recommends using the FeverPAIN or Centor scoring systems to guide antibiotic prescribing. FeverPAIN score (0-5): Fever (during previous 24 hours), Purulence (tonsillar exudate), Attend rapidly (within 3 days of onset), severely Inflamed tonsils, No cough or coryza. Interpretation: 0-1 (13-18% streptococcal): NO antibiotic. 2-3 (34-40% streptococcal): consider DELAYED (back-up) prescription. 4-5 (62-65% streptococcal): consider IMMEDIATE or DELAYED prescription. This patient scores 4 — NICE recommends either an immediate OR a back-up (delayed) antibiotic prescription. The delayed prescription strategy is a key antimicrobial stewardship tool: it empowers patients, reduces unnecessary antibiotic use (many patients never use the prescription), and maintains patient safety. When antibiotics are indicated for sore throat: (1) First-line: phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 500 mg QDS for 5-10 days. (2) If penicillin-allergic: clarithromycin 250-500 mg BD for 5 days. (3) Azithromycin is NOT first-line. Most sore throats are viral and self-limiting within 7 days regardless of cause.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 55-year-old man is admitted with a wound infection following elective hip replacement surgery. Wound swab culture grows methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Which of the following antibiotics is the most appropriate choice for treating a serious MRSA infection?
Option_A: Flucloxacillin — the standard anti-staphylococcal antibiotic
Option_B: Oral amoxicillin — effective against all staphylococci
Option_C: Oral ciprofloxacin — fluoroquinolones cover all resistant organisms
Option_D: Oral metronidazole — effective against all Gram-positive organisms
Option_E: Vancomycin (IV) — the standard treatment for serious MRSA infections
Explanation: MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) is resistant to ALL beta-lactam antibiotics, including flucloxacillin, co-amoxiclav, and all cephalosporins. This is because MRSA carries the mecA gene encoding an altered penicillin-binding protein (PBP2a) that has low affinity for beta-lactams. Treatment options for MRSA: (1) SERIOUS INFECTIONS: IV VANCOMYCIN (glycopeptide) — first-line for serious MRSA infections (bacteraemia, endocarditis, pneumonia, deep wound infections). Teicoplanin (another glycopeptide) is an alternative. (2) IV linezolid (oxazolidinone) — alternative for MRSA pneumonia or when vancomycin is contraindicated. Has good oral bioavailability. (3) IV daptomycin — for MRSA bacteraemia (NOT for pneumonia — inactivated by surfactant). (4) SKIN/SOFT TISSUE (mild): oral options include doxycycline, trimethoprim, or clindamycin depending on sensitivities. (5) MRSA DECOLONISATION (for carriers): nasal mupirocin ointment + chlorhexidine body wash for 5 days. MRSA screening: PHE recommends screening all elective surgical admissions (nasal swab ± groin/axilla) and decolonising positive patients pre-operatively to reduce surgical site infections.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A foundation year 1 doctor is asked to prescribe prophylactic antibiotics for a patient undergoing elective colorectal surgery. The local guideline recommends a single dose of IV co-amoxiclav at induction of anaesthesia. The patient's drug chart shows the surgeon has written 'co-amoxiclav IV TDS for 5 days — surgical prophylaxis'. According to antimicrobial stewardship and NICE NG125 (surgical site infections) guidance, what should the FY1 do?
Option_A: Follow the surgeon's prescription without question — senior instructions should always be followed
Option_B: Prescribe the 5-day course but add a note to review at 48 hours
Option_C: Query the prescription with the surgeon — NICE and local guidelines recommend a SINGLE prophylactic dose at induction, and prolonged prophylactic courses increase resistance and C. difficile risk without reducing infection rates
Option_D: Refuse to prescribe any antibiotics as prophylaxis is unnecessary for colorectal surgery
Option_E: Switch to oral co-amoxiclav for the full 5 days instead of IV
Explanation: NICE NG125 (surgical site infections) recommends: (1) Give antibiotic prophylaxis before skin incision (at induction of anaesthesia) for clean-contaminated and contaminated surgery (including colorectal). (2) Use a SINGLE DOSE in most cases — additional intra-operative doses only if the procedure is prolonged (>4 hours or >2 half-lives of the antibiotic) or there is major blood loss. (3) Do NOT continue prophylactic antibiotics after wound closure — there is NO evidence that extended courses reduce SSI rates, but they significantly increase antibiotic resistance, C. difficile risk, and adverse effects. (4) If the surgical field is contaminated (e.g., faecal peritonitis), this becomes TREATMENT rather than prophylaxis and a therapeutic course is appropriate. This scenario tests a key stewardship principle: the FY1 should question inappropriate prescribing regardless of seniority. GMC Good Medical Practice states that all doctors have a duty to raise concerns about patient safety. The correct approach is to discuss this professionally with the surgeon, citing the evidence and local guidelines. This is NOT about refusing to prescribe but about ensuring evidence-based prescribing.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A GP reviews a patient with an uncomplicated upper respiratory tract infection (common cold). The patient requests antibiotics because 'they always work when I get a cold'. The GP explains that antibiotics are not indicated. Which of the following is the strongest reason for NOT prescribing antibiotics for viral upper respiratory tract infections?
Option_A: Antibiotics are too expensive for the NHS to prescribe for minor illnesses
Option_B: Antibiotics cannot treat viral infections, provide no clinical benefit for the common cold, and inappropriate use drives antimicrobial resistance — which is a major public health threat
Option_C: Antibiotics cause severe side effects in all patients who take them
Option_D: NICE guidelines state that no antibiotics should ever be prescribed in primary care
Option_E: The common cold is caused by bacteria that are naturally resistant to all available antibiotics
Explanation: The common cold is caused by viruses (rhinovirus most commonly, also coronavirus, RSV, adenovirus) and antibiotics have NO effect on viral infections. NICE and PHE promote antimicrobial stewardship in primary care because: (1) Up to 20% of all antibiotic prescriptions in primary care are estimated to be unnecessary. (2) Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the greatest threats to global health — the O'Neill Review (2016) estimated that by 2050, AMR could cause 10 million deaths per year worldwide if unchecked. (3) Inappropriate prescribing drives resistance through selective pressure on bacteria. (4) Antibiotics can cause genuine harm: allergic reactions, C. difficile, disruption of normal flora, drug interactions. (5) The UK 5-year AMR strategy (2019-2024) targets a 25% reduction in antibiotic use. Strategies for managing patient expectations: (1) Explain the viral cause and self-limiting nature (7-10 days). (2) Provide self-care advice: paracetamol, fluids, rest, honey for cough. (3) Safety-netting: advise when to return (worsening symptoms, new red flags). (4) TARGET patient leaflets (NICE/PHE) provide evidence-based information to share. (5) Delayed prescriptions may be appropriate for some presentations (e.g., sore throat, acute otitis media) but NOT for simple colds.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 32-year-old man presents to a sexual health clinic requesting an HIV test. He had unprotected sex with a new partner 3 weeks ago. He currently has a 5-day history of fever, sore throat, widespread maculopapular rash, mouth ulcers, and cervical lymphadenopathy. An HIV antibody/antigen (4th generation) test is requested. What is the most appropriate interpretation and next step if the initial test is positive?
Option_A: A positive 4th generation test is definitive — start antiretroviral therapy immediately without further testing
Option_B: A positive result must be confirmed with a repeat sample using a different assay before informing the patient
Option_C: A reactive 4th generation test (which detects both p24 antigen and HIV antibodies) should be confirmed with a REPEAT test on a SECOND sample — once confirmed, refer urgently to HIV services for baseline investigations and initiation of antiretroviral therapy
Option_D: HIV antibody tests are unreliable within the first 3 months — retest in 3 months and do not act on this result
Option_E: The symptoms are consistent with glandular fever, not HIV — cancel the HIV test
Explanation: This patient presents with primary HIV seroconversion illness (acute retroviral syndrome), which occurs 2-6 weeks after infection and presents with a glandular fever-like illness: fever, rash (maculopapular), pharyngitis, oral ulcers, lymphadenopathy, myalgia, and sometimes aseptic meningitis. It is frequently misdiagnosed as EBV. HIV TESTING: (1) 4th generation (combined antigen/antibody) tests detect BOTH HIV p24 antigen (present from ~2 weeks post-infection, before antibodies develop) AND HIV-1/2 antibodies. The window period for 4th generation tests is approximately 4 WEEKS (reduced from 3 months for older antibody-only tests). (2) BHIVA guidelines require that a reactive (positive) screening test is CONFIRMED on a SECOND separate blood sample using a different assay before giving a definitive diagnosis. This prevents laboratory or identification errors. (3) Once confirmed positive: urgent referral to HIV specialist services. Baseline investigations include: CD4 count, HIV viral load, genotypic resistance testing, hepatitis B/C serology, STI screening, TB screening, toxoplasma serology, CMV serology, and baseline renal/liver/bone profile. (4) Current BHIVA guidelines recommend starting ART in ALL HIV-positive patients regardless of CD4 count (based on the START trial evidence). Early treatment reduces morbidity, mortality, and onward transmission.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 38-year-old man known to be HIV-positive with a CD4 count of 85 cells/μL (not on antiretroviral therapy) presents with a 3-week history of progressive headache, confusion, and low-grade fever. A CT head shows no mass lesion. A lumbar puncture is performed: opening pressure 28 cmH₂O, CSF protein mildly raised, glucose normal, WCC 15 (lymphocytes), and India ink stain shows encapsulated yeast organisms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Cerebral toxoplasmosis
Option_B: Cryptococcal meningitis
Option_C: HIV-associated progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)
Option_D: Primary CNS lymphoma
Option_E: Tuberculous meningitis
Explanation: This is cryptococcal meningitis — caused by Cryptococcus neoformans, an encapsulated yeast. It is one of the most important AIDS-defining opportunistic infections and typically occurs when CD4 count is <100 cells/μL. Key diagnostic features: (1) Subacute presentation with headache, confusion, and fever (over weeks). (2) Raised CSF opening pressure (often markedly elevated — >20 cmH₂O). (3) India ink stain shows encapsulated yeasts (budding yeast with clear halo). (4) CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) is highly sensitive and specific — the gold standard diagnostic test. (5) Serum CrAg is also useful. (6) CSF may show: mildly raised protein, normal or low glucose, lymphocytic pleocytosis (often modest). Treatment: (1) Induction: IV amphotericin B (liposomal) + flucytosine for 2 weeks. (2) Consolidation: fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks. (3) Maintenance: fluconazole 200 mg daily until immune reconstitution (CD4 >200 for ≥6 months on ART). (4) Repeated therapeutic lumbar punctures are essential to manage raised intracranial pressure (the main cause of mortality). Cerebral toxoplasmosis typically shows ring-enhancing lesions on CT/MRI. TB meningitis has low glucose. PML has no mass effect and is caused by JC virus.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A GP is counselling a patient newly diagnosed with HIV. The patient asks about the prognosis and what monitoring is needed. Which of the following best describes the two key blood tests used to monitor HIV disease progression and treatment response?
Option_A: CD4 count (measures immune function — target is to maintain or raise above 200 cells/μL) and HIV viral load (measures the amount of virus — the aim of treatment is to achieve an undetectable viral load)
Option_B: CRP and ESR — markers of inflammation that correlate with disease activity
Option_C: Full blood count and liver function tests — to monitor for drug side effects only
Option_D: HIV antibody titre and p24 antigen level — both should be checked monthly
Option_E: Lymphocyte count and serum immunoglobulin levels — to assess overall immune status
Explanation: The two key HIV monitoring tests are: (1) CD4 COUNT (CD4+ T-lymphocytes): measures immune function. Normal is 500-1500 cells/μL. The CD4 count determines the risk of opportunistic infections: >500 — generally immunocompetent. 200-500 — moderate immunodeficiency (increased risk of bacterial infections, oral candidiasis, TB). <200 — severe immunodeficiency (AIDS-defining illnesses: PCP, toxoplasmosis, CMV retinitis, Kaposi sarcoma, oesophageal candidiasis). <100 — profound immunodeficiency (cryptococcal meningitis, MAC, PML). <50 — CMV retinitis risk. (2) HIV VIRAL LOAD (HIV RNA copies/mL): measures the amount of actively replicating virus. The primary goal of antiretroviral therapy is to achieve an UNDETECTABLE viral load (<50 copies/mL). An undetectable viral load means the person is effectively untransmittable (U=U — Undetectable = Untransmittable — PARTNER 1 and 2 studies). Viral load is the best indicator of treatment response and adherence. A rising viral load on treatment suggests non-adherence or drug resistance. BHIVA recommends checking CD4 count and viral load at diagnosis, before starting ART, and then every 3-6 months (can be extended to annually once stable and CD4 >350). FBC, renal, liver, bone, and lipid profiles are also monitored for drug side effects.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A healthcare worker sustains a needlestick injury from a hollow-bore needle used on a patient known to be HIV-positive with a detectable viral load. The injury occurred 30 minutes ago. She presents to the emergency department. According to BASHH/PHE guidelines, what is the recommended post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP)?
Option_A: No PEP is needed if the healthcare worker has been vaccinated against hepatitis B
Option_B: Observe the healthcare worker for symptoms and only start PEP if seroconversion occurs
Option_C: PEP is no longer recommended for occupational needlestick injuries as the risk is very low
Option_D: Start antiretroviral PEP (typically Truvada + raltegravir) as soon as possible — ideally within 1 hour and no later than 72 hours after exposure, with a 28-day course
Option_E: Start single-agent zidovudine for 7 days
Explanation: HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) is a time-critical intervention. BASHH/PHE guidelines for OCCUPATIONAL PEP: (1) Risk assessment: the risk of HIV transmission from a single needlestick with a hollow-bore needle from an HIV-positive source with detectable viral load is approximately 0.3% (1 in 300). Risk is higher with: deep injury, visible blood on the device, hollow-bore needle, source with high viral load. (2) TIMING is critical: PEP should be started as soon as possible — ideally within 1 HOUR. It can be given up to 72 hours post-exposure but efficacy decreases with delay. After 72 hours, PEP is NOT recommended. (3) REGIMEN: typically a combination of two NRTIs (tenofovir/emtricitabine — Truvada) plus an integrase inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir) for 28 DAYS. Single-agent PEP is no longer used. (4) Baseline blood tests on the healthcare worker: HIV test, hepatitis B/C serology, FBC, renal function. (5) Follow-up HIV testing at 8-12 weeks and 6 months post-exposure. (6) Also consider hepatitis B (booster if needed) and hepatitis C exposure. SEXUAL EXPOSURE PEP (PEPSE) follows similar principles — available from sexual health clinics and emergency departments.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 25-year-old woman returns from a 2-week holiday in Southeast Asia. She presents 8 days later with high fever (40.2°C), severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, and a widespread blanching maculopapular rash. Blood tests show: WCC 2.8 × 10⁹/L, platelets 92 × 10⁹/L, ALT 85 U/L. She has no respiratory symptoms and her chest X-ray is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Chikungunya virus infection
Option_B: Dengue fever
Option_C: Leptospirosis
Option_D: Plasmodium falciparum malaria
Option_E: Typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi)
Explanation: This is dengue fever — a mosquito-borne flavivirus infection transmitted by Aedes aegypti (day-biting mosquito), endemic in Southeast Asia, Central/South America, Caribbean, and Sub-Saharan Africa. Classic features: (1) High fever with 'break-bone' myalgia and arthralgia. (2) Retro-orbital pain (pain behind the eyes — highly characteristic). (3) Blanching maculopapular rash (often appearing around day 3-5 as fever defervesces). (4) Leucopenia (low WCC) and thrombocytopenia — characteristic haematological findings. (5) Mildly elevated transaminases. (6) Headache. Diagnosis: dengue NS1 antigen (positive in first 5-7 days), dengue IgM/IgG serology (positive from day 5 onwards). Management: SUPPORTIVE — no specific antiviral. Paracetamol for fever/pain. AVOID NSAIDs and aspirin (increased bleeding risk due to thrombocytopenia). Monitor for dengue haemorrhagic fever (DHF) and dengue shock syndrome (DSS) — warning signs include abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, rising haematocrit, and rapid platelet drop. Malaria should always be excluded with blood films in any febrile returning traveller, but the leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, retro-orbital pain, and rash pattern are more consistent with dengue. Typhoid causes relative bradycardia and rose spots (sparse). Chikungunya causes prominent joint pain but less thrombocytopenia.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 58-year-old man presents to his GP with a 3-month history of fatigue, breathlessness on exertion, and pallor. Blood tests show: Hb 88 g/L, MCV 68 fL, ferritin 6 μg/L, serum iron low, TIBC raised. He has no history of dietary restriction and no obvious source of blood loss. According to NICE NG12 and BSG guidelines, what is the most important next investigation?
Option_A: Bone marrow biopsy to exclude myelodysplasia
Option_B: Commence oral iron replacement and recheck in 3 months
Option_C: Dietary advice and reassurance — low ferritin is common
Option_D: Refer for upper and lower GI investigations (OGD and colonoscopy) to exclude GI malignancy
Option_E: Request haemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude thalassaemia
Explanation: This is iron deficiency anaemia (IDA) — microcytic anaemia (low MCV) with low ferritin, low serum iron, and raised TIBC. The CRITICAL teaching point is that in any adult male or post-menopausal female with unexplained IDA, GI malignancy MUST be excluded. BSG guidelines and NICE NG12 (suspected cancer) recommend: (1) Urgent referral for BOTH upper GI endoscopy (OGD) AND colonoscopy — coeliac serology should also be checked. (2) The most common GI causes of IDA are: colorectal cancer, gastric cancer, coeliac disease, peptic ulcer disease, angiodysplasia, and NSAID-related gastropathy. (3) Do NOT simply treat with iron and assume the cause is dietary in this demographic — this delays cancer diagnosis. (4) NICE NG12 referral criteria for suspected colorectal cancer include unexplained IDA in men (any age) or non-menstruating women. In pre-menopausal women with IDA, menstrual blood loss is the most common cause, but GI investigation should still be considered if there are GI symptoms, family history, or failure to respond to iron.
