Iron studies interpretation: IDA — low ferritin, low iron, HIGH TIBC (the body is trying to absorb more iron). Anaemia of chronic disease — normal/high ferritin, low iron, LOW TIBC. Thalassaemia trait — normal ferritin, normal iron studies, raised red cell count.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 35-year-old woman presents with fatigue, glossitis, and paraesthesia in her hands and feet. Blood tests show: Hb 95 g/L, MCV 112 fL, blood film shows hypersegmented neutrophils and oval macrocytes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alcohol excess causing macrocytosis
Option_B: Folate deficiency anaemia
Option_C: Hypothyroidism
Option_D: Myelodysplastic syndrome
Option_E: Vitamin B12 deficiency (megaloblastic anaemia)
Explanation: This is megaloblastic anaemia due to vitamin B12 deficiency. The key diagnostic features are: (1) MACROCYTIC anaemia (MCV >100 fL). (2) HYPERSEGMENTED NEUTROPHILS on blood film (≥5 lobes) — this is pathognomonic for megaloblastic anaemia (B12 or folate deficiency). (3) OVAL MACROCYTES on blood film. (4) Neurological symptoms — peripheral neuropathy (paraesthesia), subacute combined degeneration of the cord (posterior columns and lateral corticospinal tracts), cognitive impairment. B12 deficiency causes neurological features; folate deficiency does NOT. (5) Glossitis (smooth, painful tongue). Causes of B12 deficiency: pernicious anaemia (most common — autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells, anti-intrinsic factor antibodies), dietary (vegans), terminal ileum disease (Crohn disease), gastrectomy, metformin (long-term use). Treatment: IM hydroxocobalamin — loading dose: 1 mg IM on alternate days for 2 weeks, then maintenance 1 mg IM every 3 months for life if pernicious anaemia. IMPORTANT: always check and replace B12 BEFORE giving folate — giving folate alone in B12 deficiency can precipitate or worsen subacute combined degeneration. Alcohol causes macrocytosis but NOT hypersegmented neutrophils.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 72-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on long-term methotrexate presents with fatigue. Blood tests show: Hb 100 g/L, MCV 108 fL, WCC 3.2 × 10⁹/L, platelets 110 × 10⁹/L. Her serum B12 is normal. What is the most likely cause of her macrocytic anaemia and pancytopenia?
Option_A: Alcohol-related liver disease
Option_B: Aplastic anaemia
Option_C: Folate deficiency secondary to methotrexate (a dihydrofolate reductase inhibitor)
Option_D: Hypothyroidism
Option_E: Pernicious anaemia
Explanation: Methotrexate is a dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) inhibitor that blocks the conversion of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate, impairing DNA synthesis. This causes: (1) Megaloblastic macrocytic anaemia (identical to dietary folate deficiency). (2) Pancytopenia (bone marrow suppression) — low WCC and platelets. (3) This is a well-known and important side effect of methotrexate. Prevention and management: (1) ALL patients on methotrexate should take FOLIC ACID supplementation (typically 5 mg once weekly, taken on a DIFFERENT day to methotrexate) to reduce haematological toxicity, mucositis, and hepatotoxicity. (2) Regular monitoring: FBC, LFTs, and U&Es — NICE recommends FBC and LFTs every 2-4 weeks until therapy stabilised, then every 2-3 months. (3) Methotrexate is prescribed WEEKLY — a critical patient safety point. Daily dosing errors have caused fatalities. (4) If severe pancytopenia develops: stop methotrexate, give folinic acid (leucovorin) rescue (NOT folic acid — folinic acid bypasses the DHFR block), and consider G-CSF if neutropenic. Other causes of macrocytosis: B12/folate deficiency, alcohol, hypothyroidism, liver disease, myelodysplasia, reticulocytosis, antiretroviral drugs (especially zidovudine).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 28-year-old woman of Afro-Caribbean descent presents to the emergency department with severe left-sided abdominal pain, fever, and a history of sickle cell disease (HbSS). Her Hb is 62 g/L (baseline Hb 80 g/L) and her reticulocyte count is very low (<1%). She has parvovirus B19 IgM positive. What type of sickle cell crisis is this?
Option_A: Aplastic crisis — parvovirus B19 infects erythroid precursors causing temporary red cell aplasia
Option_B: Haemolytic crisis — increased rate of red cell destruction
Option_C: Sequestration crisis — pooling of blood in the spleen
Option_D: Sickle chest syndrome
Option_E: Vaso-occlusive (painful) crisis
Explanation: This is an APLASTIC CRISIS in sickle cell disease, caused by parvovirus B19 infection. Parvovirus B19 has tropism for erythroid precursor cells and causes temporary cessation of red cell production (red cell aplasia) lasting 7-10 days. In patients with chronic haemolytic anaemias (sickle cell, thalassaemia, hereditary spherocytosis), where red cell turnover is already high and the marrow is working at maximum capacity, even a brief pause in production causes a dramatic drop in haemoglobin. Key features: (1) Sudden drop in Hb below baseline. (2) Very low or ABSENT reticulocyte count (the marrow is not producing new red cells — this distinguishes aplastic from haemolytic crisis where reticulocytes would be HIGH). (3) Parvovirus B19 IgM positive. Treatment: supportive — blood transfusion if symptomatic anaemia. The aplasia is self-limiting (recovery in 7-10 days). Types of sickle cell crisis: (1) VASO-OCCLUSIVE (most common): severe pain due to sickling and microvascular occlusion. (2) SEQUESTRATION: rapid splenic enlargement with blood pooling — presents with acute anaemia and splenomegaly (mainly in children — adults with HbSS have usually auto-infarcted their spleen). (3) HAEMOLYTIC: accelerated haemolysis with raised reticulocytes, raised bilirubin, raised LDH. (4) APLASTIC: as described above. (5) ACUTE CHEST SYNDROME: fever, chest pain, new infiltrate on CXR — medical emergency.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A GP reviews a 45-year-old woman with confirmed iron deficiency anaemia secondary to menorrhagia. She has no red flag symptoms and GI investigations are not indicated. She is commenced on oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg TDS. When should a response to treatment be assessed, and what would indicate an adequate response?
Option_A: Check FBC at 1 week — Hb should have normalised completely
Option_B: Check FBC at 2-4 weeks — an Hb rise of approximately 10-20 g/L over 2-4 weeks indicates adequate response, then continue iron for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish stores
Option_C: Check ferritin at 1 week — it should have normalised
Option_D: No follow-up needed — simply complete a 1-month course
Option_E: Recheck at 6 months — oral iron takes at least 6 months to show any effect
Explanation: BSG guidelines for iron deficiency anaemia recommend: (1) First-line treatment: ORAL IRON — ferrous sulfate 200 mg BD-TDS (65 mg elemental iron per tablet), or ferrous fumarate 210 mg BD-TDS. (2) Response assessment: recheck FBC at 2-4 weeks. An adequate response is an Hb rise of approximately 10-20 g/L over 2-4 weeks (approximately 10 g/L per week is expected). (3) Duration: continue oral iron for 3 MONTHS AFTER Hb has normalised to replenish iron stores (ferritin). (4) Common side effects: constipation, nausea, black stools, abdominal pain — these cause poor adherence. If not tolerated: try alternate-day dosing (evidence suggests similar absorption with fewer side effects), try a different preparation (ferrous fumarate or ferrous gluconate), take with/after food (reduces absorption slightly but improves tolerability). (5) Absorption tips: take on an empty stomach with vitamin C (orange juice); avoid taking with tea, coffee, calcium, or antacids (reduce absorption). (6) If no response to oral iron at 2-4 weeks: consider non-adherence, malabsorption (coeliac disease), ongoing blood loss, or incorrect diagnosis. IV iron (e.g., ferric carboxymaltose/Ferinject) is indicated for intolerance/non-adherence to oral iron, malabsorption, or need for rapid repletion.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 68-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (eGFR 18 mL/min) has persistent anaemia (Hb 85 g/L) despite adequate iron stores (ferritin 350 μg/L, transferrin saturation 28%). His MCV is 88 fL. What is the most likely cause of his anaemia?
Option_A: Anaemia of chronic disease due to rheumatoid arthritis
Option_B: Chronic blood loss from haemodialysis
Option_C: Erythropoietin deficiency due to chronic kidney disease (renal anaemia)
Option_D: Iron deficiency anaemia — ferritin is unreliable in CKD
Option_E: Myelodysplastic syndrome
Explanation: This is renal anaemia — caused by reduced erythropoietin (EPO) production by the failing kidneys. EPO is a glycoprotein hormone produced primarily by peritubular fibroblasts in the renal cortex in response to hypoxia. In CKD, progressive nephron loss reduces EPO production, leading to normocytic normochromic anaemia. Key features: (1) Normocytic (normal MCV). (2) Adequate iron stores (ferritin >100, transferrin saturation >20%). (3) Typically becomes clinically significant when eGFR <30 mL/min (CKD stage 4-5). NICE NG203 (CKD) and NICE NG8 recommend: (1) Investigate and treat correctable causes first (iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency). (2) If iron-replete and EPO-deficient: erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) — e.g., darbepoetin alfa or epoetin alfa — target Hb 100-120 g/L (do NOT target normal Hb — increases cardiovascular risk). (3) Maintain iron stores: ferritin >100 μg/L and transferrin saturation >20% for ESA efficacy. IV iron is often needed in CKD/dialysis patients. (4) Monitor Hb every 2-4 weeks during ESA initiation. Complications of ESAs: hypertension (most common), pure red cell aplasia (rare — anti-EPO antibodies), increased thromboembolic risk.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 22-year-old woman of Mediterranean descent is found to have a microcytic anaemia (Hb 105 g/L, MCV 65 fL) on routine blood tests. Her ferritin is normal (85 μg/L), serum iron is normal, and her blood film shows target cells. Her red cell count is raised (6.2 × 10¹²/L). Haemoglobin electrophoresis shows raised HbA2 (5.8%). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Alpha-thalassaemia trait
Option_B: Beta-thalassaemia major
Option_C: Beta-thalassaemia trait (carrier)
Option_D: Iron deficiency anaemia with laboratory error
Option_E: Sickle cell trait
Explanation: This is beta-thalassaemia trait (carrier state) — the most common cause of microcytic anaemia with NORMAL iron studies. Key features: (1) Microcytic anaemia with disproportionately LOW MCV relative to the degree of anaemia (MCV often 60-70 fL with only mild anaemia). (2) RAISED red cell count — this distinguishes thalassaemia from IDA (where red cell count is low/normal). (3) NORMAL ferritin and iron studies — crucial distinction from IDA. (4) Target cells on blood film. (5) Raised HbA2 (>3.5%) on haemoglobin electrophoresis — DIAGNOSTIC of beta-thalassaemia trait. HbA2 is composed of 2 alpha + 2 delta chains, and increases when beta-chain production is reduced. Thalassaemia trait is clinically benign and does NOT require treatment. The importance is: (1) Genetic counselling — if both parents are carriers, there is a 25% chance of beta-thalassaemia MAJOR (transfusion-dependent) in offspring. (2) Avoid unnecessary iron supplementation — iron overload can occur. (3) Do NOT misdiagnose as IDA and investigate for GI malignancy. Beta-thalassaemia major presents in infancy with severe anaemia, hepatosplenomegaly, bone marrow expansion (skull bossing, hair-on-end appearance on X-ray), and requires lifelong transfusions.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 50-year-old woman presents with fatigue and is found to have anaemia. Blood tests show: Hb 92 g/L, MCV 82 fL, ferritin 280 μg/L, serum iron low, TIBC low, CRP 65 mg/L. She has active rheumatoid arthritis. What is the most likely type of anaemia?
Option_A: Anaemia of chronic disease (anaemia of inflammation)
Option_B: Haemolytic anaemia
Option_C: Iron deficiency anaemia
Option_D: Sideroblastic anaemia
Option_E: Thalassaemia trait
Explanation: This is anaemia of chronic disease (ACD) — also called anaemia of inflammation. It is the second most common cause of anaemia worldwide (after IDA). ACD occurs in the context of chronic inflammatory, infectious, or malignant conditions. The pathophysiology centres on HEPCIDIN — an acute-phase protein produced by the liver in response to IL-6: (1) Hepcidin blocks iron absorption from the gut (via degradation of ferroportin). (2) Hepcidin traps iron in macrophages (prevents iron recycling from senescent red cells). (3) The result: functional iron deficiency — iron is present in stores but UNAVAILABLE for erythropoiesis. Iron studies in ACD vs IDA: ACD: ferritin NORMAL or HIGH (iron is trapped in stores), serum iron LOW, TIBC LOW (the body is NOT trying to absorb more iron), transferrin saturation variable. IDA: ferritin LOW, serum iron LOW, TIBC HIGH (the body IS trying to absorb more iron). This patient has raised ferritin, low iron, low TIBC, and raised CRP with active RA — classic ACD. Treatment: treat the underlying inflammatory condition. EPO may be considered in some cases. Do NOT give iron supplementation unless there is co-existing true iron deficiency (which can occur — check soluble transferrin receptor or reticulocyte Hb content).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 42-year-old man presents with jaundice, dark urine, and fatigue. Blood tests show: Hb 72 g/L, MCV 105 fL, reticulocyte count 15% (raised), unconjugated bilirubin 68 μmol/L, LDH markedly raised, haptoglobin undetectable. A direct antiglobulin test (DAT/Coombs test) is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (warm type)
Option_B: Gilbert syndrome
Option_C: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency
Option_D: Hereditary spherocytosis
Option_E: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
Explanation: This is autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (AIHA) — warm type. The hallmark is a POSITIVE direct antiglobulin test (DAT/Coombs test), which detects antibodies (usually IgG) or complement bound to the surface of red blood cells. Features of haemolysis: (1) Raised reticulocyte count (bone marrow compensation). (2) Raised unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin. (3) Raised LDH (released from lysed red cells). (4) LOW or undetectable haptoglobin (haptoglobin binds free haemoglobin and is consumed — this is the most sensitive marker of haemolysis). (5) Raised MCV — reticulocytes are larger than mature red cells. (6) Blood film: spherocytes and polychromasia. WARM AIHA (IgG antibodies, active at 37°C — most common type, ~80%): causes: idiopathic (50%), autoimmune diseases (SLE), lymphoproliferative disorders (CLL), drugs (methyldopa, penicillin). Treatment: corticosteroids (prednisolone) first-line, rituximab second-line, splenectomy for refractory cases. COLD AIHA (IgM antibodies, active at 4°C): causes: infections (Mycoplasma, EBV), lymphoproliferative disorders. DAT positive for complement (C3d). Treatment: keep warm, avoid cold exposure; rituximab for severe cases. G6PD deficiency and hereditary spherocytosis are DAT-NEGATIVE (non-immune haemolysis).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 55-year-old woman receiving chemotherapy for breast cancer presents to the emergency department with fever (38.7°C) and rigors. She had her last cycle of chemotherapy 10 days ago. Blood tests show: WCC 0.8 × 10⁹/L, neutrophils 0.3 × 10⁹/L. According to NICE CG151 (neutropenic sepsis), what is the most critical immediate action?
Option_A: Await blood culture results before starting any treatment to guide antibiotic choice
Option_B: Give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) within 1 HOUR of presentation — the door-to-needle time is the key determinant of outcome
Option_C: Give oral antibiotics and discharge with GP follow-up in 48 hours
Option_D: Perform a CT scan to identify the source of infection before starting treatment
Option_E: Start antifungal therapy as first-line treatment
Explanation: This is NEUTROPENIC SEPSIS — a medical emergency. NICE CG151 defines neutropenic sepsis as: neutrophil count <0.5 × 10⁹/L (or expected to fall below 0.5) AND either temperature ≥38°C OR other signs/symptoms of sepsis. It typically occurs 7-14 days after chemotherapy (nadir of neutrophil count). KEY MANAGEMENT (NICE CG151): (1) Treat as an EMERGENCY — give empirical IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 HOUR of presentation (door-to-needle time <60 minutes). (2) First-line: IV PIPERACILLIN-TAZOBACTAM (Tazocin) monotherapy — this is the NICE-recommended empirical agent. Alternative: meropenem if penicillin-allergic (after risk assessment). (3) Take blood cultures (peripheral AND central line if present) BEFORE antibiotics — but do NOT delay antibiotics. (4) Do NOT wait for investigation results. (5) Risk-stratify using the MASCC score — low-risk patients may step down to oral antibiotics after 48 hours (e.g., oral co-amoxiclav + ciprofloxacin). (6) If still febrile after 48 hours on empirical antibiotics: review cultures, consider adding IV vancomycin (if line infection suspected) or antifungals (if febrile >4-7 days despite antibiotics — consider invasive aspergillosis). (7) G-CSF (filgrastim) may be used to shorten duration of neutropenia. Mortality from untreated neutropenic sepsis is very high — early antibiotics save lives.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 70-year-old man presents with fatigue, recurrent infections, and easy bruising. FBC shows: Hb 82 g/L, WCC 52 × 10⁹/L (mature lymphocytes), platelets 88 × 10⁹/L. Blood film shows smear cells (smudge cells). Flow cytometry confirms a clonal B-cell population. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia
Option_B: Acute myeloid leukaemia
Option_C: Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia
Option_D: Chronic myeloid leukaemia
Option_E: Hairy cell leukaemia
Explanation: This is chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) — the most common leukaemia in adults in the Western world (median age at diagnosis ~72 years). Key features: (1) Marked lymphocytosis — raised WCC with MATURE lymphocytes (not blasts). (2) SMEAR CELLS (smudge cells) on blood film — fragile lymphocytes that rupture during preparation — pathognomonic for CLL. (3) Anaemia and thrombocytopenia due to bone marrow infiltration. (4) Lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly. (5) Recurrent infections due to hypogammaglobulinaemia (CLL cells produce dysfunctional immunoglobulins). Diagnosis: flow cytometry showing clonal B-cells co-expressing CD5, CD19, CD23 (diagnostic immunophenotype). Staging: Binet staging (A, B, C) or Rai staging. IMPORTANT: many patients are asymptomatic and diagnosed incidentally on blood tests. Treatment: NOT all patients need treatment — a 'watch and wait' approach is used for early-stage/asymptomatic CLL. Treatment indications: progressive marrow failure (anaemia, thrombocytopenia), massive/progressive lymphadenopathy, systemic symptoms (weight loss, fevers, night sweats). Treatment options include targeted therapies: ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor), venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor), and anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies (rituximab, obinutuzumab). Complications include autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (10-15%) and Richter transformation to aggressive lymphoma (5%).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 25-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of fatigue, bleeding gums, and bruising. FBC shows: Hb 75 g/L, WCC 2.1 × 10⁹/L, platelets 18 × 10⁹/L. Blood film shows blast cells comprising 40% of nucleated cells with Auer rods visible. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia
Option_B: Acute myeloid leukaemia
Option_C: Chronic myeloid leukaemia in blast crisis
Option_D: Myelodysplastic syndrome
Option_E: Vitamin B12 deficiency
Explanation: This is acute myeloid leukaemia (AML). Key diagnostic features: (1) PANCYTOPENIA — anaemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia due to bone marrow failure (leukaemic blast infiltration crowds out normal haematopoiesis). (2) BLAST CELLS on blood film (≥20% blasts in marrow is diagnostic — WHO criteria). (3) AUER RODS — rod-shaped crystalline inclusions in the cytoplasm of blasts — PATHOGNOMONIC for AML (never seen in ALL). Auer rods are composed of fused azurophilic granules. (4) Clinical presentation: bleeding/bruising (thrombocytopenia), infections (neutropenia), fatigue/pallor (anaemia), gum hypertrophy (especially in monocytic subtypes M4/M5). AML subtypes of particular note: (1) Acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APL, M3) — characterised by the t(15;17) translocation producing PML-RARA fusion gene. Very high risk of DIC at presentation. Treated with all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) + arsenic trioxide — has the best prognosis of all AML subtypes. (2) AML is the most common acute leukaemia in adults. ALL is most common in children (peak age 2-5 years). Investigation: bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy with immunophenotyping, cytogenetics, and molecular analysis. Treatment: intensive chemotherapy (typically daunorubicin + cytarabine — '3+7' regimen), and consideration for allogeneic stem cell transplant in eligible patients.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 22-year-old man presents with painless cervical lymphadenopathy that has been present for 6 weeks. He also reports drenching night sweats, weight loss of 8 kg, and pruritus. A lymph node biopsy shows Reed-Sternberg cells (large binucleate cells with prominent nucleoli — 'owl-eye' appearance). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Hodgkin lymphoma
Option_B: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell)
Option_C: Reactive lymphadenopathy
Option_D: Sarcoidosis
Option_E: Tuberculosis
Explanation: This is Hodgkin lymphoma (HL). The hallmark is the REED-STERNBERG CELL — a large binucleate or multinucleate cell with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, giving the classic 'owl-eye' appearance on histology. Key features: (1) Painless lymphadenopathy — typically cervical or supraclavicular (contiguous spread). (2) B SYMPTOMS (present in ~30%): drenching night sweats, unexplained weight loss >10% in 6 months, unexplained fever >38°C — these have prognostic significance and are incorporated into staging. (3) Pruritus (not classified as a B symptom but commonly associated). (4) Alcohol-induced lymph node pain — rare but specific for HL. (5) Bimodal age distribution — peak at age 20-30 and again at >55. Staging: Ann Arbor staging with Lugano modification: Stage I: single lymph node region. Stage II: ≥2 regions on same side of diaphragm. Stage III: both sides of diaphragm. Stage IV: extranodal involvement (liver, bone marrow, lung). Suffix A = no B symptoms, B = B symptoms present. PET-CT is the standard staging investigation. Treatment: early stage (I-IIA): ABVD chemotherapy (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) ± radiotherapy. Advanced stage: ABVD or escalated BEACOPP. Prognosis: excellent — >85% cure rate overall. HL has one of the best prognoses of all cancers.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 6-year-old boy presents with pallor, lethargy, bone pain, and hepatosplenomegaly. FBC shows: Hb 68 g/L, WCC 85 × 10⁹/L, platelets 22 × 10⁹/L. Blood film shows numerous lymphoblasts. What is the most likely diagnosis and the key prognostic factor?
Option_A: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia — age at presentation (1-10 years has the best prognosis)
Option_B: Acute myeloid leukaemia — blast morphology determines prognosis
Option_C: Burkitt lymphoma with leukaemic presentation — tumour burden determines prognosis
Option_D: Chronic myeloid leukaemia — Philadelphia chromosome determines prognosis
Option_E: Juvenile myelomonocytic leukaemia — response to chemotherapy determines prognosis
Explanation: This is acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL) — the most common cancer in children (peak incidence age 2-5 years). ALL accounts for ~80% of childhood leukaemias. Key features: (1) Pancytopenia with circulating LYMPHOBLASTS on blood film. (2) Bone pain (marrow infiltration — important symptom in children with unexplained limb pain). (3) Hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy. (4) CNS involvement possible (cranial nerve palsies, headaches). Prognostic factors: (1) AGE: 1-10 years has the BEST prognosis. Infants (<1 year) and adolescents/adults have worse outcomes. (2) WCC at presentation: WCC <50 is favourable. (3) Cytogenetics: hyperdiploidy (>50 chromosomes) and t(12;21) ETV6-RUNX1 are favourable. Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) BCR-ABL and MLL rearrangements are unfavourable. (4) Immunophenotype: common B-ALL (CD10+) has the best prognosis. T-ALL has intermediate prognosis. (5) Response to treatment: minimal residual disease (MRD) negativity after induction. Treatment: multi-agent chemotherapy over 2-3 years — induction, consolidation, CNS prophylaxis (intrathecal methotrexate), and maintenance (oral 6-mercaptopurine + methotrexate). Cure rates in childhood ALL exceed 90% with modern protocols.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 60-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department after a fall. She has a large scalp laceration. Her INR is 8.2 and there is no active major bleeding. According to NICE and BNF guidance, what is the most appropriate management of the over-anticoagulation?
Option_A: Continue warfarin at the usual dose and recheck INR in 1 week
Option_B: Give fresh frozen plasma (FFP) and restart warfarin immediately
Option_C: Give IV vitamin K (phytomenadione) 5 mg and stop warfarin — recheck INR in 24 hours and restart warfarin when INR <5
Option_D: Give prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) urgently as the INR is above 8
Option_E: Stop warfarin only and recheck INR in 2 days — no other intervention needed
Explanation: Management of over-anticoagulation with warfarin depends on the INR level and whether there is bleeding: (1) INR 5.0-8.0, NO significant bleeding: STOP warfarin, give oral vitamin K (phytomenadione) 1-5 mg. Recheck INR in 24 hours. Restart warfarin when INR <5.0 at a reduced dose. (2) INR >8.0, NO significant bleeding: STOP warfarin, give oral vitamin K (phytomenadione) 1-5 mg (or IV if oral not possible). Recheck INR in 24 hours. Some guidelines recommend IV vitamin K 1-5 mg when INR >8. (3) MAJOR BLEEDING (regardless of INR): STOP warfarin. Give IV vitamin K 5 mg PLUS prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC — contains factors II, VII, IX, X — e.g., Beriplex/Octaplex). PCC acts within MINUTES and is preferred over FFP (which takes longer, requires large volumes, and carries transfusion risks). FFP is used only if PCC is unavailable. This patient has INR 8.2 with no active MAJOR bleeding (the laceration is minor/moderate). IV vitamin K 5 mg and stopping warfarin is appropriate. PCC would be indicated if there were major/life-threatening bleeding (intracranial haemorrhage, GI haemorrhage with haemodynamic instability). Restart warfarin once INR is in range and the clinical situation is stable.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 30-year-old woman presents with menorrhagia and easy bruising for 6 months. FBC shows: Hb 108 g/L, WCC normal, platelets 28 × 10⁹/L. Blood film shows reduced platelets but no abnormal cells. Coagulation screen is normal (PT and APTT normal). She has no hepatosplenomegaly. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Disseminated intravascular coagulation
Option_B: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)
Option_C: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Option_D: Von Willebrand disease
Option_E: Warfarin over-anticoagulation
Explanation: This is immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) — an autoimmune condition where antiplatelet antibodies (usually IgG against platelet glycoproteins GPIIb/IIIa) cause increased platelet destruction by splenic macrophages. Key features: (1) ISOLATED thrombocytopenia — normal Hb (or mild anaemia from bleeding), normal WCC, normal blood film (apart from reduced platelets). (2) NORMAL coagulation screen (PT and APTT) — the clotting cascade is intact. (3) NO splenomegaly (important — presence of splenomegaly suggests an alternative diagnosis such as liver disease or lymphoproliferative disorder). (4) ITP is a DIAGNOSIS OF EXCLUSION — other causes of thrombocytopenia must be excluded. ITP in adults: typically chronic (>12 months), affects women more than men, may be secondary to autoimmune diseases (SLE), infections (HIV, hepatitis C), or drugs. Treatment (NICE/BSH guidelines): (1) Observation if platelets >30 × 10⁹/L and no significant bleeding. (2) First-line treatment when required: ORAL PREDNISOLONE (1 mg/kg). (3) IV immunoglobulin (IVIg) for rapid platelet rise (e.g., before surgery or active major bleeding). (4) Second-line: rituximab, thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim), splenectomy (last resort). (5) Platelet transfusions are generally INEFFECTIVE in ITP (antibodies destroy transfused platelets) but may be given in life-threatening bleeding.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with extensive bruising, bleeding from venepuncture sites, and haematuria. He was admitted 2 days ago with pneumococcal sepsis. Blood tests show: platelets 35 × 10⁹/L, PT 22 seconds (raised), APTT 48 seconds (raised), fibrinogen 0.8 g/L (low), D-dimer markedly elevated, blood film shows schistocytes (fragmented red cells). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
Option_B: Haemophilia A
Option_C: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Option_D: Liver failure with coagulopathy
Option_E: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
Explanation: This is disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — a life-threatening consumptive coagulopathy characterised by widespread activation of the coagulation cascade leading to simultaneous thrombosis AND haemorrhage. DIC is always SECONDARY to an underlying cause: sepsis (most common), trauma, malignancy (especially acute promyelocytic leukaemia), obstetric complications (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism), and massive blood transfusion. Key laboratory findings: (1) LOW platelets (consumed in microthrombi). (2) PROLONGED PT and APTT (clotting factors consumed). (3) LOW fibrinogen (consumed — normally an acute-phase reactant so should be raised in sepsis; a LOW fibrinogen in sepsis strongly suggests DIC). (4) RAISED D-dimer/fibrin degradation products (FDPs) — reflecting widespread fibrinolysis. (5) SCHISTOCYTES (fragmented red cells) on blood film — red cells are sheared as they pass through fibrin strands in damaged microvasculature (microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia). Management: (1) TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE — this is the most important step. (2) Supportive replacement therapy: platelets (if <30 or bleeding), FFP (replaces clotting factors), cryoprecipitate (replaces fibrinogen — target fibrinogen >1.5 g/L), packed red cells. (3) Consider heparin only in specific situations (dominant thrombotic phenotype).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A GP receives blood results for a 62-year-old man with back pain: Hb 98 g/L, raised ESR (105 mm/hr), raised total protein with raised globulins, calcium 3.1 mmol/L, creatinine 180 μmol/L, and a monoclonal band (M-band) on serum protein electrophoresis. Urine shows Bence Jones protein. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia
Option_B: Metastatic prostate cancer with bone involvement
Option_C: Multiple myeloma
Option_D: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Option_E: Waldenström macroglobulinaemia
Explanation: This is multiple myeloma — a malignant proliferation of plasma cells in the bone marrow producing a monoclonal immunoglobulin (paraprotein/M-band). The classic presentation is remembered by the mnemonic CRAB: (1) Calcium raised (hypercalcaemia — due to osteoclast activation and bone destruction). (2) Renal impairment (light chain deposition, hypercalcaemia, dehydration, NSAIDs). (3) Anaemia (bone marrow infiltration by plasma cells). (4) Bone disease — lytic lesions (NOT blastic — myeloma causes osteolytic lesions), pathological fractures, back pain. Additional features: (5) Raised ESR (often markedly >100 mm/hr) with normal CRP (ESR is raised due to the paraprotein causing rouleaux formation). (6) Monoclonal band (M-band) on serum protein electrophoresis (SPE) — IgG is most common (~55%), then IgA (~25%). (7) Bence Jones protein in urine (free light chains — kappa or lambda). (8) Hyperviscosity syndrome (less common than in Waldenström). Investigations: serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation, serum free light chains, urine for Bence Jones, bone marrow biopsy (≥10% clonal plasma cells), whole-body low-dose CT (replaced skeletal survey — better sensitivity for lytic lesions). NICE NG35 recommends urgent referral via the 2-week wait pathway if myeloma is suspected.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of progressive left calf swelling, pain, and warmth. She is 6 weeks post-partum. Her Wells score for DVT is 3 (high probability). A D-dimer is not performed. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
Option_A: CT venogram of the pelvis
Option_B: Compression (duplex) ultrasonography of the proximal leg veins
Option_C: D-dimer — this should always be performed before imaging regardless of clinical probability
Option_D: MRI of the left leg
Option_E: Venography (contrast-based) as the gold standard test
Explanation: NICE NG158 (VTE diagnosis) recommends a two-level Wells score to guide investigation of suspected DVT: (1) Wells score ≤1 (DVT unlikely): perform D-DIMER first. If D-dimer negative — DVT excluded. If D-dimer positive — proceed to proximal leg vein ultrasound. (2) Wells score ≥2 (DVT likely): proceed DIRECTLY to proximal leg vein COMPRESSION ULTRASONOGRAPHY — D-dimer is NOT required when clinical probability is high (it would not change management as a negative D-dimer cannot reliably exclude DVT in a high-probability patient). This patient has a Wells score of 3 (high probability) — go straight to ultrasound. Compression ultrasound (duplex USS) is the first-line imaging modality: (1) Sensitivity >95% for proximal DVT (above the knee). (2) A vein that does not compress fully under the ultrasound probe indicates thrombus. (3) If the initial scan is negative but clinical suspicion remains high, repeat USS in 6-8 days or perform D-dimer. Risk factors in this patient: post-partum state (pregnancy and puerperium are major risk factors for VTE due to hypercoagulability, venous stasis, and vascular injury). While awaiting imaging, if DVT is likely, start interim anticoagulation (LMWH or DOAC) pending the result.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 65-year-old man is diagnosed with an unprovoked proximal DVT. He has no identifiable risk factors. He is started on rivaroxaban. According to NICE NG158, what is the recommended duration of anticoagulation for an unprovoked DVT, and what additional investigation should be considered?
Option_A: 3 months of anticoagulation then stop — no further investigation needed
Option_B: 6 months of anticoagulation followed by lifelong aspirin
Option_C: At least 3 months of anticoagulation, then reassess the benefits and risks of continuing beyond 3 months — consider investigation for underlying cancer in patients with unprovoked VTE
Option_D: Lifelong anticoagulation is mandatory for all unprovoked DVTs without any reassessment
Option_E: No anticoagulation is needed for DVT — only PE requires anticoagulation
Explanation: NICE NG158 (VTE diagnosis and management) recommends: DURATION OF ANTICOAGULATION: (1) PROVOKED VTE (identifiable transient risk factor — surgery, immobilisation, OCP, pregnancy): 3 months of anticoagulation then STOP. (2) UNPROVOKED VTE (no identifiable risk factor): at least 3 months, then REASSESS — consider extending anticoagulation beyond 3 months (potentially long-term/indefinite) based on individual risk-benefit analysis. Factors favouring extended therapy: male sex, proximal DVT/PE, D-dimer positive after stopping anticoagulation, low bleeding risk. (3) RECURRENT VTE or active cancer: long-term/indefinite anticoagulation. CANCER SCREENING in unprovoked VTE: NICE recommends considering investigations for underlying malignancy as 5-10% of unprovoked VTE patients have an occult cancer diagnosed within 12 months. Investigations: history, examination, basic blood tests (FBC, calcium, LFTs, urinalysis), chest X-ray, and age-appropriate cancer screening (e.g., CT abdomen-pelvis). ANTICOAGULANT CHOICE: DOACs (rivaroxaban or apixaban) are first-line for VTE treatment in most patients (NICE NG158). LMWH is preferred in active cancer (though edoxaban and rivaroxaban are alternatives) and in pregnancy.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 12-year-old boy has a history of prolonged bleeding after dental extractions and easy bruising since childhood. His mother also has a history of menorrhagia and easy bruising. Blood tests show: PT normal, APTT prolonged (42 seconds), bleeding time prolonged, platelet count normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Bernard-Soulier syndrome
Option_B: Factor V Leiden mutation
Option_C: Haemophilia A
Option_D: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Option_E: Von Willebrand disease
Explanation: This is von Willebrand disease (vWD) — the most common inherited bleeding disorder, affecting up to 1% of the population. It is typically inherited in an AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT pattern (both sexes affected — note his mother also has bleeding symptoms). Von Willebrand factor (vWF) has two key functions: (1) Mediates platelet adhesion to damaged endothelium (via GPIb receptor). (2) Acts as a carrier protein for Factor VIII (protecting it from degradation). Therefore, vWF deficiency causes: (1) Prolonged BLEEDING TIME (impaired platelet adhesion). (2) Prolonged APTT (due to secondary Factor VIII deficiency — vWF stabilises Factor VIII). (3) NORMAL platelet count (the platelets are present but cannot adhere properly). (4) NORMAL PT (extrinsic pathway is unaffected). Clinical features: mucocutaneous bleeding — easy bruising, menorrhagia, epistaxis, prolonged bleeding after dental procedures or surgery. Types: Type 1 (most common, ~70%): partial quantitative deficiency — mild/moderate symptoms. Type 2: qualitative deficiency (dysfunctional vWF). Type 3: severe quantitative deficiency — rare, autosomal recessive, severe bleeding. Treatment: (1) DESMOPRESSIN (DDAVP) — stimulates release of vWF from endothelial stores — effective for Type 1 and some Type 2. (2) vWF-containing factor concentrates for severe disease or Type 3. (3) Tranexamic acid as adjunctive therapy. Haemophilia A is X-LINKED (affects males, carrier females) and has a normal bleeding time.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 20-year-old man presents with a spontaneous haemarthrosis of his right knee. He has a family history of bleeding disorders in males on his mother's side. Coagulation studies show: PT normal, APTT prolonged (55 seconds), bleeding time normal, platelet count normal. Factor VIII activity is 3% (severely reduced). What is the diagnosis?
Option_A: Factor V Leiden thrombophilia
Option_B: Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency)
Option_C: Haemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency)
Option_D: Von Willebrand disease Type 3
Option_E: Vitamin K deficiency
Explanation: This is haemophilia A — a deficiency of Factor VIII, inherited in an X-LINKED RECESSIVE pattern (affects males, transmitted by carrier females — hence the family history of bleeding in males on the MATERNAL side). Key features: (1) PROLONGED APTT (Factor VIII is in the intrinsic pathway). (2) NORMAL PT (extrinsic pathway is intact). (3) NORMAL bleeding time and platelet count (primary haemostasis is intact — vWF is normal). (4) Haemarthrosis (bleeding into joints) is the hallmark — most commonly knee, ankle, and elbow. Repeated haemarthroses cause chronic arthropathy. Severity classification based on Factor VIII activity: Severe (<1%): spontaneous bleeding, haemarthroses from early childhood. Moderate (1-5%): bleeding with minor trauma. Mild (5-40%): bleeding mainly after surgery or significant trauma. Haemophilia A (Factor VIII) is ~4-5 times more common than Haemophilia B (Factor IX — Christmas disease). They are clinically indistinguishable — factor assays differentiate them. Treatment: (1) On-demand or prophylactic Factor VIII replacement (recombinant products). (2) Emicizumab — a bispecific antibody that mimics Factor VIII function — transformative for prophylaxis, especially in patients with inhibitors. (3) Desmopressin may be used in MILD haemophilia A (stimulates vWF release which stabilises endogenous Factor VIII). (4) Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs. (5) Tranexamic acid as adjunctive therapy for mucosal bleeding.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with headache, confusion, fever, petechial rash, and jaundice. Blood tests show: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 22 × 10⁹/L, reticulocytes raised, LDH markedly raised, haptoglobin undetectable, blood film shows schistocytes, creatinine 185 μmol/L, DAT (Coombs test) negative. Coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen) is NORMAL. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Disseminated intravascular coagulation
Option_B: Haemolytic uraemic syndrome
Option_C: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Option_D: Systemic lupus erythematosus
Option_E: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Explanation: This is thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) — a medical emergency caused by deficiency of ADAMTS13 (a metalloprotease that cleaves ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers). Without ADAMTS13, uncleaved vWF multimers cause widespread platelet aggregation in the microvasculature. The classic pentad (though not always complete): (1) Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA) — schistocytes on blood film, raised LDH, raised reticulocytes, low haptoglobin, DAT NEGATIVE. (2) THROMBOCYTOPENIA — platelets consumed in microthrombi. (3) NEUROLOGICAL symptoms — confusion, seizures, focal deficits. (4) RENAL impairment. (5) FEVER. CRITICAL distinguishing features from DIC: the coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen) is NORMAL in TTP — there is no consumptive coagulopathy. In DIC, PT and APTT are prolonged and fibrinogen is low. TTP vs HUS: HUS predominantly causes renal failure with less neurological involvement — typically follows E. coli O157:H7 infection (Shiga toxin-producing) in children. TTP predominantly causes neurological features. Management of TTP: (1) PLASMA EXCHANGE (plasmapheresis) — removes anti-ADAMTS13 antibodies and replaces ADAMTS13. This is life-saving and should be initiated immediately (mortality >90% without treatment, <20% with). (2) Corticosteroids. (3) Caplacizumab — anti-vWF nanobody. (4) Do NOT transfuse platelets — this can worsen thrombosis and organ damage ('adding fuel to the fire').

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 55-year-old woman is admitted for elective total hip replacement surgery. She weighs 80 kg and has no history of VTE. She has no contraindications to pharmacological thromboprophylaxis. According to NICE NG89 (VTE prophylaxis), what is the most appropriate thromboprophylaxis regimen?
Option_A: Aspirin 75 mg daily is sufficient VTE prophylaxis for all surgical patients
Option_B: Compression stockings alone — pharmacological prophylaxis is not needed for elective surgery
Option_C: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) starting 6-12 hours post-operatively and continued for 28-35 days post-discharge, combined with anti-embolism stockings
Option_D: Start warfarin 2 weeks before surgery and continue throughout the admission
Option_E: Unfractionated heparin infusion starting on the day of surgery only, discontinued at discharge
Explanation: NICE NG89 (VTE in over 16s — reducing the risk of hospital-acquired VTE) recommends VTE risk assessment for ALL admitted patients and appropriate prophylaxis: For ELECTIVE HIP REPLACEMENT: (1) Pharmacological: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) starting 6-12 hours POST-operatively — continued for 28-35 DAYS post-operatively (extended prophylaxis). This is because VTE risk persists for weeks after major orthopaedic surgery. (2) Alternative pharmacological options: rivaroxaban 10 mg OD for 35 days (licensed for hip replacement VTE prophylaxis). (3) MECHANICAL: anti-embolism stockings (TED stockings) or intermittent pneumatic compression devices, used in combination with pharmacological prophylaxis. For ELECTIVE KNEE REPLACEMENT: LMWH or rivaroxaban for 14 days. General principles: (1) All patients should be mobilised as early as possible. (2) VTE risk assessment using the Department of Health VTE risk assessment tool on admission. (3) Balance VTE risk against bleeding risk. (4) Contraindications to pharmacological prophylaxis: active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, known HIT. (5) Warfarin is NOT used for surgical VTE prophylaxis (slow onset, unpredictable effect). Aspirin alone is not recommended by NICE as thromboprophylaxis (though some international guidelines include it as an option).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 35-year-old man with a known peanut allergy accidentally eats a biscuit containing peanuts in a restaurant. Within 10 minutes he develops widespread urticaria, facial and tongue swelling, wheeze, stridor, and his blood pressure drops to 78/40 mmHg. According to the Resuscitation Council UK anaphylaxis guidelines, what is the FIRST drug to administer?
Option_A: IM adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh — this is the first-line life-saving treatment
Option_B: IV adrenaline 1 mg (1 mL of 1:1000) as a rapid bolus
Option_C: IV chlorphenamine 10 mg as the primary treatment
Option_D: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg — steroids are the most important drug in anaphylaxis
Option_E: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg — bronchodilators should be given first
Explanation: Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction (Type I — IgE-mediated). The Resuscitation Council UK anaphylaxis algorithm states: FIRST-LINE TREATMENT: IM ADRENALINE (epinephrine) — this is the MOST IMPORTANT drug and should be given IMMEDIATELY. Adult dose: 0.5 mg IM (0.5 mL of 1:1000 concentration) into the anterolateral thigh. Can be repeated every 5 minutes if no improvement. Paediatric doses: <6 years: 0.15 mg IM; 6-12 years: 0.3 mg IM; >12 years: 0.5 mg IM. NEVER give adrenaline IV at 1:1000 concentration — this causes fatal arrhythmias. IV adrenaline (1:10,000) is ONLY used by experienced specialists in a monitored setting (e.g., ICU) for refractory anaphylaxis. Adrenaline works by: alpha-1 (vasoconstriction — raises BP, reduces oedema), beta-1 (increases cardiac output), beta-2 (bronchodilation, reduces mediator release from mast cells). Additional treatments (SECOND-LINE — given AFTER adrenaline): IV fluid resuscitation (rapid 500 mL-1L crystalloid boluses), IV chlorphenamine 10 mg (antihistamine), IV hydrocortisone 200 mg (prevents biphasic reaction — takes 4-6 hours to work so NOT the acute treatment). After stabilisation: observe for minimum 6-12 hours (risk of biphasic reaction), measure serum TRYPTASE at 1, 6, and 24 hours (to confirm mast cell degranulation), refer to allergy clinic, prescribe TWO adrenaline auto-injectors on discharge, and provide an anaphylaxis action plan.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 42-year-old woman tells her GP she is 'allergic to penicillin' because she developed a non-specific rash as a child when given amoxicillin. She now needs antibiotics for a dental abscess. According to NICE guidance on drug allergy (CG183), what is the most appropriate approach?
Option_A: Avoid all beta-lactam antibiotics for life including cephalosporins — prescribe clarithromycin
Option_B: Challenge with IV penicillin in the GP surgery to test for allergy
Option_C: Prescribe amoxicillin anyway as childhood rashes are never allergic
Option_D: Take a detailed drug allergy history — a non-specific childhood rash with amoxicillin has a very low probability of true penicillin allergy, and a cephalosporin (e.g., cefalexin) can be used safely if clinical assessment supports this
Option_E: Refer to an immunologist before prescribing any antibiotics for the dental abscess
Explanation: NICE CG183 (drug allergy) emphasises the importance of accurate drug allergy assessment. Key points: (1) Up to 10% of the population report a 'penicillin allergy', but >90% of these are NOT truly allergic when formally tested. (2) A detailed DRUG ALLERGY HISTORY is essential: what drug, what reaction, timing of reaction, severity, was it confirmed? (3) Non-specific childhood rashes with amoxicillin are extremely common (often viral exanthems or the amoxicillin-EBV reaction) and have a very low probability of being true IgE-mediated allergy. (4) True penicillin allergy features: urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis occurring within 1 hour of administration (immediate/Type I hypersensitivity). (5) PENICILLIN-CEPHALOSPORIN cross-reactivity: the historical figure of 10% cross-reactivity is outdated. Current evidence suggests the true cross-reactivity rate is 1-2% for modern cephalosporins with dissimilar side chains. Patients with a non-severe penicillin reaction can generally safely receive cephalosporins. (6) Patients with a CONFIRMED severe reaction (anaphylaxis) to penicillin should avoid all penicillins AND be cautious with cephalosporins — use carbapenems or non-beta-lactams. (7) NICE recommends referral for formal allergy testing (skin prick test, specific IgE, drug provocation test) to DE-LABEL patients who are likely not truly allergic. Incorrect penicillin allergy labels lead to use of broader-spectrum alternatives, increased resistance, and worse outcomes.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 5-year-old boy has a history of recurrent sinopulmonary infections, including 4 episodes of pneumonia in the past 2 years. He also had a severe episode of Giardia lamblia gastroenteritis. Blood tests show very low serum immunoglobulin levels (IgG, IgA, IgM all markedly reduced). His lymphocyte subsets show absent B cells but normal T cell numbers. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID)
Option_B: DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion)
Option_C: HIV infection
Option_D: Selective IgA deficiency
Option_E: X-linked agammaglobulinaemia (Bruton disease)
Explanation: This is X-linked agammaglobulinaemia (XLA/Bruton disease) — caused by mutations in the BTK (Bruton tyrosine kinase) gene, which is essential for B-cell maturation. Key features: (1) Affects MALES only (X-linked recessive). (2) Presents after 6 months of age (when maternal IgG wanes). (3) ABSENT B CELLS on flow cytometry (B-cell development is arrested at the pre-B cell stage in the bone marrow). (4) VERY LOW or absent immunoglobulins (ALL classes — IgG, IgA, IgM). (5) T cells are NORMAL. (6) Recurrent BACTERIAL infections (sinopulmonary infections are most common — Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae). (7) Susceptibility to enteric infections (Giardia is characteristic). (8) Susceptibility to enteroviral meningoencephalitis. Treatment: lifelong immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IVIG or subcutaneous Ig). Distinguishing from CVID: CVID also causes hypogammaglobulinaemia but typically presents later (adolescence/adulthood), affects both sexes, and patients have B cells present (though dysfunctional). Selective IgA deficiency has isolated low IgA with normal IgG/IgM. DiGeorge syndrome primarily affects T cells (thymic hypoplasia) with 22q11.2 deletion features (cardiac defects, palatal anomalies, hypocalcaemia).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 25-year-old woman with a known history of asthma and eczema presents to her GP with persistent rhinorrhoea, nasal congestion, sneezing, and itchy watery eyes that worsen during spring and summer. Symptoms have been present for several years and significantly affect her quality of life. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
Option_A: Intranasal corticosteroid spray (e.g., fluticasone or mometasone) with oral or intranasal antihistamines as needed
Option_B: Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 30 mg daily for 2 weeks)
Option_C: Oral montelukast as first-line monotherapy
Option_D: Referral for immunotherapy as first-line treatment
Option_E: Topical nasal decongestant spray (e.g., oxymetazoline) for long-term daily use
Explanation: This is allergic rhinitis (seasonal — hay fever). The triad of asthma, eczema, and allergic rhinitis constitutes the 'atopic triad' — these are Type I (IgE-mediated) hypersensitivity conditions and commonly coexist. NICE CKS and BSACI guidelines recommend a stepwise approach: (1) ALLERGEN AVOIDANCE where possible (keep windows closed during high pollen, shower after outdoor exposure, use wraparound sunglasses). (2) FIRST-LINE pharmacological: intranasal CORTICOSTEROID spray (most effective treatment for allergic rhinitis — reduces inflammation, congestion, rhinorrhoea, and sneezing). Examples: fluticasone propionate, mometasone furoate, beclometasone. (3) ORAL or INTRANASAL antihistamines as needed — non-sedating preferred (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine). (4) Eye symptoms: sodium cromoglicate eye drops or antihistamine eye drops (e.g., olopatadine). (5) For refractory symptoms: consider adding montelukast (leukotriene receptor antagonist) or ipratropium nasal spray. (6) IMMUNOTHERAPY (sublingual or subcutaneous) is reserved for severe symptoms unresponsive to maximal medical therapy — requires specialist referral. (7) Do NOT use topical nasal decongestants (e.g., oxymetazoline) for more than 7 days — causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa). Oral corticosteroids: short courses only for severe flares, NOT long-term. IM depot steroid injections (Kenalog) are NO LONGER recommended due to adverse effects.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 40-year-old man undergoes a routine blood test and is found to have a low IgA level (<0.07 g/L) with normal IgG and IgM levels. He is asymptomatic. What is the most important clinical implication he should be counselled about?
Option_A: He must avoid all dairy products as IgA deficiency causes lactose intolerance
Option_B: He must receive monthly IgA replacement infusions to prevent infection
Option_C: He requires lifelong prophylactic antibiotics
Option_D: He should carry a medical alert indicating IgA deficiency, as transfusion of blood products containing IgA could cause anaphylaxis in patients who develop anti-IgA antibodies
Option_E: No clinical implications — selective IgA deficiency is always entirely benign
Explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is the MOST COMMON primary immunodeficiency (prevalence ~1 in 500 in Caucasians). Key points: (1) Most patients are ASYMPTOMATIC and diagnosed incidentally. (2) Some patients have increased susceptibility to: sinopulmonary infections, GI infections (especially Giardia), autoimmune conditions (coeliac disease — 10-15 times more common in IgA deficiency; SLE, thyroiditis). (3) IMPORTANT TRANSFUSION RISK: patients with severe IgA deficiency may develop anti-IgA antibodies (IgG or IgE class). If they subsequently receive blood products containing IgA (FFP, platelets, red cells with plasma residue), they can have a severe ANAPHYLACTIC transfusion reaction. These patients should carry a medical alert and receive WASHED red cells or blood products from IgA-deficient donors. (4) IgA replacement therapy is NOT available or recommended (unlike IgG replacement). (5) Also important: IgA deficiency causes FALSE-NEGATIVE coeliac serology (as the standard test — anti-tTG — is IgA-based). If coeliac disease is suspected in an IgA-deficient patient, use IgG-based antibodies (anti-tTG IgG or anti-deamidated gliadin peptide IgG). (6) Prophylactic antibiotics are not routinely indicated unless recurrent infections are a significant problem.

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A foundation doctor is asked to classify types of hypersensitivity reactions. A patient developed contact dermatitis to nickel in a belt buckle after repeated exposure. What type of hypersensitivity reaction is this?
Option_A: Type I — IgE-mediated (immediate) hypersensitivity
Option_B: Type II — antibody-mediated (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity
Option_C: Type III — immune complex-mediated hypersensitivity
Option_D: Type IV — delayed-type (cell-mediated) hypersensitivity
Option_E: Type V — stimulatory hypersensitivity
Explanation: Contact dermatitis is a TYPE IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity reaction — mediated by T CELLS (not antibodies). The Gell and Coombs classification of hypersensitivity: TYPE I (Immediate — IgE-mediated): IgE on mast cells binds antigen causing degranulation (histamine release). Occurs within minutes. Examples: anaphylaxis, allergic rhinitis, asthma, urticaria, food allergy. TYPE II (Cytotoxic — antibody-mediated): IgG or IgM antibodies bind to cell surface antigens, leading to cell destruction via complement, phagocytosis, or ADCC. Examples: autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, Goodpasture syndrome, transfusion reactions, haemolytic disease of the newborn, Graves disease (stimulatory variant — sometimes called Type V). TYPE III (Immune complex-mediated): antigen-antibody complexes deposit in tissues, activating complement and causing inflammation. Examples: SLE, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, serum sickness, Arthus reaction, hypersensitivity pneumonitis (farmer's lung). TYPE IV (Delayed-type — T cell-mediated): T cells (CD4+ helper or CD8+ cytotoxic) respond to antigen, causing inflammation over 48-72 hours. Examples: contact dermatitis (nickel, poison ivy), tuberculin skin test (Mantoux), transplant rejection, coeliac disease, granulomatous inflammation (TB, sarcoidosis). The key distinction: Types I-III are antibody-mediated; Type IV is CELL-mediated (T cells).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 30-year-old woman with a family history of Huntington disease asks her GP about genetic testing. Her father was diagnosed with the condition at age 45. She currently has no symptoms. She wishes to know whether she carries the gene mutation. According to UK genetic testing guidelines, what is the most appropriate approach?
Option_A: Arrange a blood test for the Huntington gene (HTT) mutation in the GP surgery and give the result by telephone
Option_B: Decline testing as there is no treatment for Huntington disease so testing serves no purpose
Option_C: Refer to a clinical genetics service for pre-test counselling, informed consent, and predictive genetic testing in a supportive setting — this is a complex process that should not be rushed
Option_D: Tell her she has a 25% chance of inheriting the condition and does not need testing
Option_E: Test her father first to confirm his diagnosis before considering testing for her
Explanation: Huntington disease is caused by a trinucleotide CAG repeat expansion in the HTT gene on chromosome 4, inherited in an AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT pattern. Each child of an affected parent has a 50% risk (not 25%) of inheriting the mutation. Predictive (presymptomatic) genetic testing for Huntington disease — testing an at-risk individual who is currently unaffected — carries profound psychological, social, and ethical implications. UK guidelines and international best practice (following the International Huntington Association/World Federation of Neurology guidelines) recommend: (1) Referral to a CLINICAL GENETICS SERVICE — this is NOT a test that should be arranged in primary care or given casually. (2) PRE-TEST COUNSELLING over multiple sessions: discussion of implications of a positive result (certainty of developing an incurable neurodegenerative condition), impact on insurance, employment, family planning, relationships, and psychological wellbeing. (3) Informed CONSENT — the individual must fully understand the implications. (4) The right NOT to know must be respected — many at-risk individuals choose not to be tested. (5) A cooling-off period between counselling and blood test. (6) Results given IN PERSON by a trained professional with psychological support available. (7) Post-test support regardless of result. This is also relevant to other late-onset autosomal dominant conditions (familial cancer syndromes, BRCA1/2, Lynch syndrome).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A couple attend a genetics clinic. The woman is a carrier of cystic fibrosis (heterozygous CFTR mutation). Her partner has no family history of cystic fibrosis and is from a Northern European background where the carrier frequency is approximately 1 in 25. He has not been tested. What is the approximate probability that their child will have cystic fibrosis?
Explanation: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is an AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE condition caused by mutations in the CFTR gene on chromosome 7. The most common mutation is F508del (ΔF508). For a child to be affected, they must inherit TWO copies of the mutant gene (one from each parent — both parents must be carriers or affected). The calculation: Mother is a CONFIRMED carrier (probability = 1). Father: unknown genotype but from Northern European background where carrier frequency = 1 in 25 (probability of being a carrier = 1/25). IF both parents are carriers, the chance of an affected child = 1 in 4 (Mendelian autosomal recessive — 25%). Overall probability = (probability mother carrier) × (probability father carrier) × (probability child affected if both carriers) = 1 × 1/25 × 1/4 = 1/100. Therefore the risk is approximately 1 in 100 (1%). This calculation is fundamental genetics used in clinical practice. If the father is tested and found to be a carrier, the risk increases to 1 in 4. If the father tests negative, the risk becomes very low (but not zero — testing does not detect all mutations). Genetic counselling should discuss: carrier testing for the partner, prenatal testing options (CVS at 11-13 weeks or amniocentesis at 15+ weeks), preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) with IVF, and newborn screening (CF is on the UK newborn bloodspot screening programme — Guthrie test).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A newborn baby is found to have Down syndrome (trisomy 21) on karyotyping. The mother is 25 years old. The karyotype shows 46,XX,rob(14;21),+21 — a Robertsonian translocation. What is the significance of this finding compared to standard trisomy 21?
Option_A: It indicates a more severe form of Down syndrome with worse prognosis
Option_B: It means one parent is likely a balanced translocation carrier, and the recurrence risk for future pregnancies is significantly higher than standard trisomy 21 — parental karyotyping is essential
Option_C: It means the diagnosis is incorrect — translocation carriers cannot have Down syndrome
Option_D: The recurrence risk is the same as standard trisomy 21 (1% or age-related risk)
Option_E: Translocation Down syndrome only occurs in older mothers
Explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) has three cytogenetic mechanisms: (1) NON-DISJUNCTION (standard trisomy 21, 47,XX/XY,+21) — 95% of cases. Free trisomy. Recurrence risk: ~1% or age-related risk (whichever is higher). Associated with advanced maternal age. (2) ROBERTSONIAN TRANSLOCATION — ~4% of cases. The extra chromosome 21 material is attached to another acrocentric chromosome (most commonly chromosome 14 → rob(14;21)). This is the most clinically significant type because ONE PARENT may be a BALANCED TRANSLOCATION CARRIER (they have 45 chromosomes but are phenotypically normal). The recurrence risk is MUCH HIGHER than non-disjunction: if the mother carries the translocation → ~10-15% recurrence risk; if the father carries it → ~1-3%. If it is a de novo translocation (neither parent carries it) → recurrence risk is low. (3) MOSAICISM (~1%): some cells have trisomy 21, some are normal (46 chromosomes). Variable phenotype depending on proportion of trisomic cells. PARENTAL KARYOTYPING is essential when translocation Down syndrome is identified, to: determine if a parent is a balanced carrier, counsel on recurrence risk, and offer testing to other family members. Notably, translocation Down syndrome can occur at ANY maternal age — it is not associated with advanced maternal age (unlike non-disjunction).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A GP is asked about the UK newborn bloodspot screening programme (heel-prick test). Which of the following conditions is screened for in ALL newborns in the UK at day 5 of life?
Option_A: Coeliac disease
Option_B: Cystic fibrosis, congenital hypothyroidism, sickle cell disease, phenylketonuria, and medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (MCADD) — plus four additional inherited metabolic diseases
Option_C: Down syndrome
Option_D: Fragile X syndrome
Option_E: Galactosaemia
Explanation: The UK Newborn Blood Spot Screening Programme screens ALL newborns using a blood spot sample taken from a heel prick at DAY 5 of life (ideally on day 5, acceptable up to day 8). The nine conditions currently screened are: (1) PHENYLKETONURIA (PKU) — deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase. Early detection and dietary phenylalanine restriction prevents severe intellectual disability. (2) CONGENITAL HYPOTHYROIDISM (CHT) — TSH-based screening. Early thyroxine replacement prevents cretinism. (3) SICKLE CELL DISEASE — haemoglobin electrophoresis/HPLC. (4) CYSTIC FIBROSIS — immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) followed by genetic testing for CFTR mutations if raised. (5) MEDIUM-CHAIN ACYL-CoA DEHYDROGENASE DEFICIENCY (MCADD) — fatty acid oxidation disorder causing hypoketotic hypoglycaemia. Plus four additional inherited metabolic diseases added in 2015: (6) Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD). (7) Isovaleric acidaemia (IVA). (8) Glutaric aciduria type 1 (GA1). (9) Homocystinuria (HCU). Key principles of screening (Wilson and Jungner criteria): the condition must be an important health problem, there must be a recognisable latent/early stage, there must be an accepted treatment, and early treatment must improve outcomes. Down syndrome and galactosaemia are NOT part of the newborn blood spot programme. Down syndrome is screened antenatally (combined test at 11-14 weeks).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 32-year-old woman is referred to the genetics service after her sister is diagnosed with breast cancer at age 34. Her mother died of ovarian cancer at age 52. Genetic testing reveals her sister carries a pathogenic BRCA1 mutation. The patient is considering predictive genetic testing. If she is found to carry the BRCA1 mutation, what is the approximate lifetime risk of breast cancer?
Option_A: 10-15% — similar to the general population
Option_B: 20-30% — moderately increased
Option_C: 40-50% — significantly increased
Option_D: 60-80% — very high lifetime risk
Option_E: 100% — all BRCA1 carriers will develop breast cancer
Explanation: BRCA1 and BRCA2 are tumour suppressor genes involved in DNA double-strand break repair (homologous recombination). Pathogenic mutations are inherited in an AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT pattern with variable penetrance. Lifetime cancer risks for BRCA1 mutation carriers: BREAST CANCER: approximately 60-80% lifetime risk (by age 80). OVARIAN CANCER: approximately 40-60% lifetime risk. Contralateral breast cancer: ~40-60% within 20 years of first diagnosis. BRCA2 carriers: breast cancer ~45-70%, ovarian cancer ~10-30%. Management options for BRCA1/2 carriers (NICE CG164 — familial breast cancer): (1) ENHANCED SURVEILLANCE: annual MRI breast screening from age 30 (or earlier depending on family history). Mammography from age 40. (2) RISK-REDUCING SURGERY: bilateral risk-reducing mastectomy (reduces breast cancer risk by >90%); bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) at 35-40 for BRCA1 or 40-45 for BRCA2 (reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80% and breast cancer risk by ~50% if done pre-menopausally). (3) CHEMOPREVENTION: tamoxifen or raloxifene may be offered to reduce risk. (4) CASCADE TESTING: offer BRCA testing to all first-degree relatives. NICE recommends referral to genetics if ≥10% probability of a BRCA mutation (using validated risk assessment tools such as Manchester score or BOADICEA).

Subject: Infectious Disease / Haematology / Immunology
Question: A 28-year-old man is diagnosed with Marfan syndrome after presenting with a spontaneous pneumothorax. He is tall and thin with arachnodactyly and a high-arched palate. Echocardiography shows aortic root dilatation of 42 mm. What is the most serious cardiovascular complication he is at risk of, and what is the inheritance pattern?
Option_A: Aortic root dilatation leading to aortic dissection and rupture — autosomal dominant (FBN1 gene mutation)
Option_B: Aortic stenosis requiring valve replacement — autosomal recessive
Option_C: Atrial septal defect — X-linked recessive
Option_D: Coarctation of the aorta — polygenic inheritance
Option_E: Mitral valve prolapse requiring lifelong anticoagulation — autosomal dominant
Explanation: Marfan syndrome is an autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene (encoding fibrillin-1) on chromosome 15. Fibrillin-1 is a key component of elastic fibres in connective tissue. Clinical features (multisystem): SKELETAL: tall stature, arachnodactyly (long spider-like fingers — positive Steinberg and Walker-Murdoch signs), high-arched palate, pectus excavatum/carinatum, scoliosis, joint hypermobility. CARDIOVASCULAR (most important — determines prognosis): AORTIC ROOT DILATATION — the most serious complication, leading to aortic regurgitation, aortic dissection, and rupture (the leading cause of death). Mitral valve prolapse (common but usually benign). OCULAR: lens subluxation (ectopia lentis) — typically UPWARD subluxation (in homocystinuria, the lens subluxes DOWNWARD). Myopia, retinal detachment. RESPIRATORY: spontaneous pneumothorax (tall patients with apical blebs). Management: (1) Regular echocardiographic surveillance (at least annually — more frequent if aortic root is dilating). (2) BETA-BLOCKERS (or ARBs — losartan) to reduce aortic wall stress and slow aortic dilatation. (3) Prophylactic aortic root replacement when diameter reaches 45-50 mm (or earlier if rapidly dilating or family history of dissection). (4) Avoid contact sports, heavy isometric exercise. (5) Genetic counselling — 50% chance of transmission to offspring.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 78-year-old woman is admitted with diarrhoea and vomiting for 3 days. She takes ramipril, amlodipine, and ibuprofen for osteoarthritis. Observations: BP 95/60 mmHg, HR 105 bpm. Blood tests show: creatinine 285 μmol/L (baseline 90 μmol/L 3 months ago), urea 22 mmol/L, potassium 5.8 mmol/L. Urine output has been <100 mL in the past 6 hours. What is the KDIGO stage of AKI and the most likely cause?
Option_A: KDIGO Stage 1, post-renal AKI due to ureteric obstruction
Option_B: KDIGO Stage 2, intrinsic renal AKI due to interstitial nephritis from ibuprofen
Option_C: KDIGO Stage 3, pre-renal AKI due to hypovolaemia exacerbated by ACE inhibitor and NSAID use — this is the most common type of AKI in hospitalised patients
Option_D: KDIGO Stage 3, renal AKI due to glomerulonephritis
Option_E: Not AKI — this is chronic kidney disease progression
Explanation: This is KDIGO STAGE 3 AKI, pre-renal in aetiology. KDIGO AKI staging: Stage 1: creatinine rise ≥26 μmol/L within 48 hours OR 1.5-1.9 times baseline OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6-12 hours. Stage 2: creatinine 2.0-2.9 times baseline OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for ≥12 hours. Stage 3: creatinine ≥3.0 times baseline OR rise to ≥354 μmol/L OR urine output <0.3 mL/kg/hr for ≥24 hours OR anuria for ≥12 hours OR initiation of RRT. This patient: creatinine 285 from baseline 90 = 3.17 times baseline → STAGE 3. Also urine output <0.3 mL/kg/hr for >6 hours → consistent with Stage 3. PRE-RENAL AKI (most common, ~55-60% of all AKI): reduced renal perfusion due to: (1) Hypovolaemia (diarrhoea and vomiting — fluid depletion). (2) ACE inhibitor (ramipril) — blocks angiotensin II which normally constricts the efferent arteriole to maintain GFR in low-flow states. (3) NSAID (ibuprofen) — blocks prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar vasodilatation. The combination of hypovolaemia + ACE inhibitor + NSAID is the 'triple whammy' — a well-recognised and preventable cause of AKI. NICE AKI guideline emphasises: STOP nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, diuretics), give IV fluid resuscitation, monitor urine output, check and manage hyperkalaemia urgently.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 65-year-old man with AKI Stage 3 (creatinine 620 μmol/L, oliguria) develops the following: potassium 7.2 mmol/L with ECG showing peaked T waves and widened QRS, pH 7.08 (severe metabolic acidosis), and pulmonary oedema unresponsive to IV furosemide. According to NICE and Renal Association guidelines, what is the definitive management?
Option_A: Continue medical management with insulin-dextrose infusions and calcium gluconate only
Option_B: High-dose IV furosemide infusion and fluid restriction — dialysis is never needed for AKI
Option_C: Renal replacement therapy (haemodialysis or haemofiltration) — this patient has multiple indications for urgent dialysis
Option_D: Sodium bicarbonate infusion to correct the acidosis and reassess in 24 hours
Option_E: Transfer to ITU for observation only — dialysis should be avoided in the elderly
Explanation: This patient has MULTIPLE indications for urgent renal replacement therapy (RRT — haemodialysis or continuous haemofiltration). The indications for urgent dialysis in AKI can be remembered by the mnemonic AEIOU: (A) Acidosis — severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1) refractory to medical management. (E) Electrolytes — refractory hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >6.5 with ECG changes not responding to medical treatment). (I) Ingestion — certain drug overdoses (e.g., lithium, salicylates, methanol, ethylene glycol). (O) Overload — fluid overload (pulmonary oedema) not responding to diuretics. (U) Uraemia — symptomatic uraemia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, uraemic bleeding). This patient has THREE indications: (1) Refractory hyperkalaemia (K⁺ 7.2 with ECG changes). (2) Severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.08). (3) Pulmonary oedema unresponsive to furosemide. Immediate medical management of hyperkalaemia WHILE ARRANGING DIALYSIS: (1) 10 mL 10% calcium gluconate IV (cardioprotection — stabilises myocardium but does NOT lower K⁺). (2) Insulin 10 units + 50 mL 50% glucose IV (shifts K⁺ intracellularly — lowers K⁺ temporarily). (3) Nebulised salbutamol 10-20 mg. (4) Calcium resonium (oral/rectal — slow acting). (5) Sodium bicarbonate if acidotic. These are TEMPORISING measures — dialysis is definitive.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 70-year-old man with known benign prostatic hyperplasia presents with inability to pass urine for 18 hours, lower abdominal pain, and a palpable distended bladder. Blood tests show creatinine 310 μmol/L (baseline 100 μmol/L). After urinary catheterisation, 1800 mL of urine drains immediately and his creatinine improves to 130 μmol/L over 48 hours. What type of AKI was this?
Option_A: Intrinsic renal AKI due to acute tubular necrosis
Option_B: Post-renal AKI due to bladder outflow obstruction — the most important type to identify as it is often reversible with prompt intervention
Option_C: Pre-renal AKI due to dehydration
Option_D: Pre-renal AKI due to renal artery stenosis
Option_E: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
Explanation: This is POST-RENAL AKI (obstructive uropathy) — caused by bladder outflow obstruction from benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). Post-renal AKI accounts for ~5-10% of AKI but is the most important to identify because it is often REVERSIBLE with prompt relief of obstruction. Causes of post-renal obstruction: (1) Lower urinary tract: BPH (most common in elderly men), prostate cancer, urethral stricture, blood clots, bladder tumour. (2) Upper urinary tract: bilateral ureteric obstruction (stones, tumour compression, retroperitoneal fibrosis) — unilateral obstruction rarely causes AKI if the other kidney is normal. Clinical features: (1) Anuria or significant oliguria (though partial obstruction may cause normal or even polyuria). (2) Palpable distended bladder (lower tract obstruction). (3) History of urinary symptoms (hesitancy, poor stream, nocturia in BPH). Investigation: (1) BLADDER SCAN (post-void residual) — first-line to assess for urinary retention. (2) Urinary CATHETERISATION — both diagnostic and therapeutic. (3) Renal ultrasound — look for HYDRONEPHROSIS (dilated renal pelvis and calyces). Note: ultrasound may not show hydronephrosis in very acute obstruction (<24-48 hours) or dehydrated patients. Management: (1) Relieve obstruction urgently (catheterisation for lower tract; nephrostomy or ureteric stent for upper tract). (2) Monitor for POST-OBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS — after relief of chronic obstruction, patients can develop massive polyuria (>200 mL/hr). Requires IV fluid replacement to prevent dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 55-year-old man is admitted to ICU with septic shock secondary to pneumonia. Despite adequate fluid resuscitation and vasopressors, he develops AKI Stage 3 with creatinine rising from 90 to 380 μmol/L over 48 hours. Urinalysis shows granular ('muddy brown') casts. What is the most likely intrinsic renal pathology?
Option_A: Acute interstitial nephritis
Option_B: Acute tubular necrosis (ATN)
Option_C: IgA nephropathy
Option_D: Renal vein thrombosis
Option_E: Thrombotic microangiopathy
Explanation: This is acute tubular necrosis (ATN) — the most common cause of INTRINSIC RENAL AKI (~85% of intrinsic cases). ATN occurs when renal tubular epithelial cells are damaged by ischaemia or nephrotoxins. Causes: (1) ISCHAEMIC ATN (most common): prolonged pre-renal hypoperfusion (sepsis, hypovolaemia, surgery, haemorrhage) that has progressed beyond reversible pre-renal failure. The tubular cells are particularly vulnerable to ischaemia because of their high metabolic demand and oxygen consumption. (2) NEPHROTOXIC ATN: aminoglycosides (gentamicin), contrast media, cisplatin, myoglobin (rhabdomyolysis), haemoglobin (intravascular haemolysis). Key diagnostic features: (1) MUDDY BROWN GRANULAR CASTS on urinalysis — these are pathognomonic for ATN (sloughed tubular epithelial cells and debris forming casts). (2) Urine sodium >40 mmol/L (tubules cannot reabsorb sodium — contrast with pre-renal where urine Na <20). (3) Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) >2% (pre-renal: <1%). (4) Urine osmolality <350 mOsm/kg (tubules cannot concentrate urine — pre-renal: >500). Clinical course: ATN typically takes 1-3 weeks to recover as tubular cells regenerate. Supportive management: maintain euvolaemia, avoid further nephrotoxins, dialysis if indicated (AEIOU criteria). Prognosis depends on the underlying cause and severity.

Subject: Renal / Urology
