Question: A 32-year-old woman presents with fever, rash, and arthralgia 2 weeks after starting amoxicillin for a urinary tract infection. Blood tests show AKI with creatinine 220 μmol/L (baseline normal), eosinophilia (1.8 × 10⁹/L), and raised IgE. Urinalysis shows white cell casts and eosinophiluria. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced)
Option_B: Acute tubular necrosis from sepsis
Option_C: Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity
Option_D: Post-infectious glomerulonephritis
Option_E: Urinary tract obstruction
Explanation: This is acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) — an inflammatory condition of the renal interstitium, most commonly DRUG-INDUCED. Key features — the classic triad (present in <30% of cases but highly suggestive): (1) FEVER. (2) RASH (maculopapular). (3) EOSINOPHILIA. Plus: AKI developing days to weeks after starting the offending drug. Urinalysis: WHITE CELL CASTS and EOSINOPHILURIA (eosinophils in urine — suggestive but not diagnostic). Common causative drugs: (1) ANTIBIOTICS: penicillins (especially amoxicillin/ampicillin), cephalosporins, rifampicin, sulphonamides, fluoroquinolones. (2) NSAIDs (can cause AIN with nephrotic syndrome). (3) PPIs — increasingly recognised as a significant cause. (4) Allopurinol. (5) 5-aminosalicylates (mesalazine). Pathophysiology: Type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity reaction — T-cell mediated inflammation. Not dose-dependent. Management: (1) STOP the offending drug immediately — this is the most important step. (2) Renal function usually improves within days to weeks of drug withdrawal. (3) Corticosteroids (prednisolone) may be considered if no improvement after drug withdrawal — evidence is limited but commonly used. (4) Renal biopsy may be needed if the diagnosis is unclear or the patient is not improving — shows interstitial oedema and inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: An FY2 doctor is asked to prescribe IV fluids for a patient with AKI. Which of the following drugs should be STOPPED in a patient with AKI to prevent further renal injury?
Option_A: Amlodipine, paracetamol, and levothyroxine
Option_B: Aspirin, clopidogrel, and atorvastatin
Option_C: Metformin, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, NSAIDs, and diuretics — these should be withheld during AKI ('sick day rules' drugs)
Option_D: Only gentamicin needs to be stopped — all other drugs are safe
Option_E: Prednisolone, omeprazole, and insulin
Explanation: In AKI, several drug classes should be STOPPED or WITHHELD to prevent further renal injury. The NICE AKI guideline and Think Kidneys 'sick day rules' identify these as drugs to temporarily stop during acute illness or AKI: (1) NSAIDs — reduce prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar vasodilatation, reducing renal blood flow. (2) ACE inhibitors and ARBs — block efferent arteriolar constriction, reducing GFR. In hypovolaemic states, these can precipitate or worsen AKI. (3) DIURETICS (especially loop diuretics) — can exacerbate hypovolaemia and pre-renal AKI. However, may be continued if needed for fluid overload in discussion with senior clinicians. (4) METFORMIN — must be stopped in AKI as it is renally cleared and can accumulate, causing life-threatening lactic acidosis. (5) Other drugs to review: aminoglycosides (dose adjustment or hold), lithium (renally cleared — toxicity risk), digoxin (renally cleared), potassium-sparing diuretics (hyperkalaemia risk). The 'DAMN' mnemonic for drugs to stop in AKI: Diuretics, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, Metformin, NSAIDs. SICK DAY RULES: patients on these medications should be educated to temporarily stop them during acute illness with dehydration (vomiting, diarrhoea, fever) and restart when eating and drinking normally. This is a key patient safety intervention.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 22-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being found collapsed following a night out. He is confused and has dark brown urine. Blood tests show: creatinine 450 μmol/L, potassium 6.8 mmol/L, calcium 1.9 mmol/L (low), phosphate 2.5 mmol/L (high), CK 85,000 U/L (markedly elevated), urate raised. Urinalysis is positive for blood on dipstick but no red cells on microscopy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute glomerulonephritis
Option_B: Haemolytic uraemic syndrome
Option_C: Leptospirosis
Option_D: Rhabdomyolysis causing AKI
Option_E: Urinary tract infection with haematuria
Explanation: This is rhabdomyolysis — massive skeletal muscle breakdown releasing intracellular contents into the circulation, causing AKI. Key diagnostic features: (1) Massively elevated CK (creatine kinase) — typically >10,000 U/L (often >50,000). CK is the most sensitive marker. (2) DARK BROWN URINE — due to myoglobinuria. (3) Urine dipstick positive for BLOOD but NO red cells on microscopy — the dipstick detects the haem moiety of myoglobin (cross-reacts with haemoglobin on the dipstick). This discrepancy is a classic exam finding. (4) AKI — myoglobin is directly nephrotoxic to renal tubules (causes ATN), exacerbated by hypovolaemia and acidic urine. (5) Hyperkalaemia — potassium released from damaged muscle cells. (6) Hyperphosphataemia — phosphate released from muscle. (7) Hypocalcaemia — calcium sequestered in damaged muscle (precipitates as calcium phosphate). (8) Hyperuricaemia — nucleotide release. Causes: crush injury, prolonged immobilisation (found collapsed), excessive exercise, drugs/alcohol, seizures, malignant hyperthermia, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, statins (rare). Management: (1) AGGRESSIVE IV FLUID RESUSCITATION — aim for urine output 200-300 mL/hr (this is the most important intervention — dilutes myoglobin and prevents tubular precipitation). (2) Monitor and treat hyperkalaemia urgently. (3) Monitor for compartment syndrome. (4) Dialysis if refractory AKI. (5) Avoid calcium supplementation unless symptomatic (risk of soft tissue calcification). Prognosis: usually good if managed early — renal function recovers in most cases.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 60-year-old woman with AKI Stage 2 is being managed on the acute medical unit. The FY1 doctor reviews her fluid balance chart and notes she has received 3 litres of IV normal saline over 24 hours. Her repeat blood tests show: sodium 145 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 118 mmol/L (raised), bicarbonate 16 mmol/L (low), pH 7.28. What is the most likely cause of her metabolic acidosis?
Option_A: Diabetic ketoacidosis
Option_B: Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis from excessive normal saline administration
Option_C: Lactic acidosis from sepsis
Option_D: Renal tubular acidosis
Option_E: Uraemic acidosis from worsening AKI
Explanation: This is hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis) caused by excessive administration of 0.9% normal saline. Normal saline contains 154 mmol/L of both sodium AND chloride — the chloride content is significantly HIGHER than plasma chloride (98-106 mmol/L). Large-volume normal saline infusion causes: (1) HYPERCHLORAEMIA — raised chloride displaces bicarbonate to maintain electroneutrality. (2) NON-ANION GAP metabolic acidosis (the anion gap is normal because the acidosis is due to bicarbonate loss/displacement, not accumulation of unmeasured anions). (3) This is increasingly recognised as a significant clinical problem — the SPLIT trial and SMART trial showed that balanced crystalloids (Hartmann solution/Ringer's lactate) are associated with fewer adverse renal outcomes compared to normal saline. NICE guidelines on IV fluid therapy recommend: (1) Use BALANCED CRYSTALLOIDS (Hartmann/Plasmalyte) as the default resuscitation and maintenance fluid in most patients. (2) Normal saline is still appropriate in specific situations: hypochloraemia, hyperkalaemia (Hartmann contains 5 mmol/L K⁺), and as a vehicle for certain drug infusions. (3) Avoid normal saline as the sole maintenance fluid in large volumes. (4) In AKI: ensure adequate fluid resuscitation but avoid fluid overload — fluid balance should be carefully monitored.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 75-year-old man is found to have AKI on routine blood tests (creatinine 165 μmol/L, baseline 85 μmol/L). He is asymptomatic but takes ramipril 10 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, and ibuprofen PRN. According to NICE guidelines, what is the FIRST step in managing this AKI?
Option_A: Arrange urgent renal biopsy
Option_B: Commence IV fluids and temporarily stop nephrotoxic medications (ramipril, furosemide, ibuprofen) — reassess fluid status and monitor
Option_C: Refer for urgent haemodialysis
Option_D: Start IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis
Option_E: Start IV dopamine to improve renal perfusion
Explanation: The initial management of AKI follows a systematic approach as recommended by NICE: (1) IDENTIFY AND TREAT THE CAUSE: most commonly pre-renal (hypovolaemia, sepsis, drugs). (2) STOP NEPHROTOXIC DRUGS: this patient is on the 'triple whammy' combination — ACE inhibitor (ramipril), diuretic (furosemide), and NSAID (ibuprofen). ALL should be temporarily withheld. (3) ASSESS FLUID STATUS: if hypovolaemic — IV fluid resuscitation with crystalloid (250-500 mL boluses, reassessing after each). If euvolaemic — maintenance fluids. If fluid-overloaded — cautious diuretic use. (4) MONITOR: urine output (catheterise if needed), daily U&Es, fluid balance chart. (5) CHECK FOR OBSTRUCTION: bladder scan, consider renal ultrasound within 24 hours if no obvious cause or if upper tract obstruction suspected. (6) CHECK POTASSIUM — treat hyperkalaemia urgently if present. (7) Avoid contrast media and further nephrotoxins. (8) Adjust doses of renally excreted drugs. IV dopamine (renal-dose) was historically used but has NO evidence of benefit in AKI and is NOT recommended. Renal biopsy is reserved for suspected glomerulonephritis or when the cause is unclear despite initial assessment. Dialysis is for specific indications (AEIOU) not for all AKI.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 45-year-old man undergoes a CT coronary angiogram with iodinated contrast. He has CKD stage 3 (eGFR 38 mL/min) and type 2 diabetes. Forty-eight hours later, his creatinine rises from 140 to 210 μmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis and how could it have been prevented?
Option_A: Acute interstitial nephritis from contrast allergy — prevented by pre-treatment with antihistamines
Option_B: Cholesterol crystal embolisation — prevented by anticoagulation
Option_C: Contrast-induced AKI (CI-AKI) — could have been reduced by pre- and post-procedure IV hydration with normal saline or sodium bicarbonate
Option_D: Post-renal obstruction from contrast extravasation
Option_E: Renal artery dissection — prevented by using lower contrast volumes
Explanation: This is contrast-induced AKI (CI-AKI, previously called contrast-induced nephropathy) — AKI occurring within 48-72 hours of iodinated contrast administration. Risk factors: (1) Pre-existing CKD (most important — eGFR <45 significantly increases risk). (2) Diabetes mellitus (especially with CKD). (3) Heart failure. (4) Dehydration/hypovolaemia. (5) High contrast volume. (6) Concurrent nephrotoxic drugs. Prevention (NICE and Renal Association guidelines): (1) IV HYDRATION — pre- and post-procedure IV saline (1 mL/kg/hr for 6-12 hours before and 6-12 hours after) or IV sodium bicarbonate 1.26%. This is the most effective preventive measure. (2) Use lowest possible contrast volume. (3) Use iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast agents. (4) STOP metformin on the day of contrast and for 48 hours after (risk of lactic acidosis if AKI develops). (5) Withhold NSAIDs and diuretics if possible. (6) N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — historically used but evidence from the PRESERVE trial showed NO benefit. No longer routinely recommended. (7) Check renal function 48-72 hours post-contrast in high-risk patients. Pathophysiology: direct tubular toxicity + renal vasoconstriction causing medullary ischaemia. Most cases are self-limiting with creatinine peaking at 3-5 days and returning to baseline within 1-2 weeks.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A foundation doctor reviews a patient with AKI. The consultant asks about the urine sodium concentration. In PRE-RENAL AKI with intact tubular function, what would you expect the urine sodium to be?
Option_A: Urine sodium <20 mmol/L — the kidneys are avidly retaining sodium and water to restore circulating volume
Option_B: Urine sodium >40 mmol/L — the kidneys are wasting sodium in pre-renal states
Option_C: Urine sodium is irrelevant in AKI and should not be measured
Option_D: Urine sodium is normal (40-220 mmol/L) regardless of AKI type
Option_E: Urine sodium of exactly 20 mmol/L confirms the diagnosis of ATN
Explanation: In PRE-RENAL AKI, the kidney's tubular function is INTACT — the tubules are appropriately responding to reduced renal perfusion by maximally reabsorbing sodium and water to try to restore circulating volume. Therefore: PRE-RENAL AKI: Urine sodium <20 mmol/L (avid sodium retention). Urine osmolality >500 mOsm/kg (concentrated urine). Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) <1%. Urine:plasma osmolality ratio >1.5. Urine:plasma urea ratio >8. INTRINSIC RENAL AKI (ATN): tubular function is DAMAGED — the tubules CANNOT reabsorb sodium or concentrate urine appropriately. Urine sodium >40 mmol/L (sodium wasting). Urine osmolality <350 mOsm/kg (dilute urine). FENa >2%. Urine:plasma osmolality ratio ~1 (isosthenuric). These biochemical parameters help distinguish pre-renal from intrinsic renal AKI, though they have limitations: (1) Diuretics invalidate urine sodium measurements (cause sodium wasting even in pre-renal states). (2) FENa is unreliable in patients on diuretics — use fractional excretion of UREA (FEUrea) instead (<35% suggests pre-renal). (3) In early ATN evolving from pre-renal failure, there may be an overlap period. Clinical context always takes priority over any single test.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 68-year-old woman with AKI Stage 3 develops ECG changes: peaked T waves, prolonged PR interval, and widened QRS complex. Her potassium is 7.1 mmol/L. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the correct ORDER of emergency hyperkalaemia management?
Option_A: Calcium gluconate IV → insulin-dextrose IV → nebulised salbutamol → calcium resonium → consider dialysis
Option_B: Calcium resonium first → then insulin-dextrose → then calcium gluconate only if cardiac arrest occurs
Option_C: Insulin-dextrose IV first → then calcium gluconate → then salbutamol
Option_D: Nebulised salbutamol first → followed by insulin-dextrose → then calcium gluconate last
Option_E: Sodium bicarbonate IV as the sole treatment
Explanation: Emergency management of hyperkalaemia follows a specific order based on mechanism and urgency. The UK Renal Association and Resuscitation Council guidelines recommend: STEP 1 — CARDIAC PROTECTION (immediate): 10 mL of 10% CALCIUM GLUCONATE IV over 2-5 minutes. This STABILISES the cardiac membrane (raises the threshold potential), reducing the risk of arrhythmias. It does NOT lower potassium. Onset: 1-3 minutes. Can be repeated after 5 minutes if ECG changes persist. STEP 2 — SHIFT POTASSIUM INTO CELLS: (a) INSULIN 10 units + 50 mL 50% GLUCOSE IV over 15-30 minutes. Onset: 15-30 minutes. Duration: 4-6 hours. Lowers K⁺ by 0.5-1.2 mmol/L. Monitor blood glucose hourly for 6 hours (hypoglycaemia risk). (b) NEBULISED SALBUTAMOL 10-20 mg (adjunctive). Onset: 15-30 minutes. Lowers K⁺ by 0.5-1.0 mmol/L. STEP 3 — REMOVE POTASSIUM FROM THE BODY: (a) Calcium resonium (sodium/calcium polystyrene sulfonate) — oral or rectal. Binds potassium in the GI tract. Slow onset (hours). (b) IV sodium bicarbonate — if metabolic acidosis present. (c) Loop diuretics (furosemide) — if the patient has urine output. (d) DIALYSIS — definitive treatment for refractory or severe hyperkalaemia, especially in AKI/CKD with oliguria. KEY POINT: calcium gluconate is ALWAYS first (protects the heart immediately while other treatments take effect).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 62-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has an eGFR of 42 mL/min/1.73m² confirmed on two occasions 3 months apart, with an albumin:creatinine ratio (ACR) of 8 mg/mmol. According to NICE NG203 (CKD), what stage of CKD does she have and what does the ACR indicate?
Option_A: CKD stage 2 with normal albumin excretion
Option_B: CKD stage 3a (G3aA2) — moderately decreased GFR with microalbuminuria, indicating increased cardiovascular and renal progression risk
Option_C: CKD stage 3b with macroalbuminuria
Option_D: CKD stage 4 requiring urgent nephrology referral
Option_E: CKD stage 5 approaching dialysis requirement
Explanation: NICE NG203 (CKD) classifies CKD using BOTH GFR (G) and albuminuria (A) categories: GFR staging: G1 (≥90), G2 (60-89), G3a (45-59), G3b (30-44), G4 (15-29), G5 (<15). Albuminuria staging (ACR): A1 (<3 mg/mmol — normal to mildly increased), A2 (3-30 mg/mmol — moderately increased, previously 'microalbuminuria'), A3 (>30 mg/mmol — severely increased, previously 'macroalbuminuria'). This patient: eGFR 42 = G3a, ACR 8 = A2. Therefore: CKD G3aA2. The ACR is crucial because albuminuria is an INDEPENDENT risk factor for both cardiovascular disease and CKD progression — even with preserved GFR. Key CKD management principles (NICE NG203): (1) OPTIMISE BP — target <140/90 (or <130/80 if ACR ≥70 or diabetes with ACR ≥30). (2) ACE inhibitor or ARB — first-line for CKD with diabetes and ACR ≥3, or CKD with ACR ≥30 regardless of diabetes. (3) SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) — recommended for CKD with ACR ≥30 (significant renoprotective and cardiovascular benefit — DAPA-CKD trial). (4) Refer to nephrology if: eGFR <30 (G4-5), ACR ≥70 mg/mmol, sustained eGFR decline of ≥25% or ≥15 mL/min within 12 months, or 5-year risk of requiring RRT ≥5%.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 58-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22 mL/min) secondary to diabetic nephropathy has blood tests showing: calcium 2.05 mmol/L (low), phosphate 1.85 mmol/L (high), PTH 28 pmol/L (markedly raised), vitamin D (25-OH) 18 nmol/L (low). What is the underlying pathophysiology of this biochemical pattern?
Option_A: Primary hyperparathyroidism — the high PTH is driving the low calcium
Option_B: Renal osteodystrophy (CKD-mineral bone disorder) — failing kidneys cannot convert 25-hydroxyvitamin D to active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, leading to hypocalcaemia, hyperphosphataemia, and secondary hyperparathyroidism
Option_C: Sarcoidosis causing excessive vitamin D activation
Option_D: Vitamin D deficiency from poor diet — unrelated to CKD
Option_E: Vitamin D toxicity causing suppressed PTH
Explanation: This is CKD-mineral bone disorder (CKD-MBD), previously called renal osteodystrophy. The pathophysiology: (1) The kidneys contain 1-alpha hydroxylase, the enzyme that converts 25-hydroxyvitamin D (inactive storage form) to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol — the active form). In CKD, this enzyme activity is progressively lost. (2) Reduced calcitriol → reduced calcium absorption from the gut → HYPOCALCAEMIA. (3) Failing kidneys cannot excrete phosphate → HYPERPHOSPHATAEMIA. (4) Both hypocalcaemia and hyperphosphataemia stimulate the parathyroid glands → SECONDARY HYPERPARATHYROIDISM (raised PTH). (5) Chronically elevated PTH causes bone resorption → renal osteodystrophy (bone pain, fractures, osteitis fibrosa cystica). (6) FGF23 (fibroblast growth factor 23) from osteocytes also rises early in CKD to increase phosphate excretion, but this also suppresses 1-alpha hydroxylase, worsening vitamin D deficiency. Management (NICE NG203 and KDIGO): (1) PHOSPHATE BINDERS (calcium carbonate, sevelamer, lanthanum) — taken with meals to reduce phosphate absorption. (2) Active vitamin D analogues (alfacalcidol or calcitriol) — bypass the defective renal hydroxylation step. (3) Dietary phosphate restriction. (4) Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) — for tertiary hyperparathyroidism or if PTH remains uncontrolled. (5) Target: maintain phosphate, calcium, and PTH within recommended ranges.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3b (eGFR 35 mL/min) has a blood pressure of 148/92 mmHg despite amlodipine 10 mg daily. His ACR is 45 mg/mmol. According to NICE NG203, what antihypertensive should be added?
Option_A: Atenolol — beta-blockers are first-line in CKD with diabetes
Option_B: Doxazosin — alpha-blockers are preferred in CKD
Option_C: Ramipril (ACE inhibitor) — first-line for CKD with diabetes and ACR ≥30, with monitoring of creatinine and potassium
Option_D: Spironolactone — potassium-sparing diuretics are safe in all CKD stages
Option_E: No additional antihypertensive is needed — amlodipine alone is sufficient
Explanation: NICE NG203 recommends ACE inhibitors (or ARBs if ACE inhibitor-intolerant) as first-line antihypertensive in CKD when: (1) Diabetes with ANY degree of albuminuria (ACR ≥3). (2) Non-diabetic CKD with ACR ≥30. ACE inhibitors/ARBs provide RENOPROTECTION beyond blood pressure lowering — they reduce intraglomerular pressure by dilating the efferent arteriole and reduce proteinuria. BP targets: <140/90 for most CKD patients; <130/80 if ACR ≥70 or diabetes with ACR ≥30. Important monitoring: (1) Check creatinine and potassium BEFORE starting and 1-2 weeks AFTER starting or dose change. (2) Accept up to a 30% rise in creatinine or a rise of up to 45 μmol/L — if >30% rise, investigate for renal artery stenosis. (3) Stop if potassium >6.0 mmol/L. (4) Do NOT combine ACE inhibitor with ARB (dual RAAS blockade — ONTARGET trial showed increased harm). Spironolactone should be used with extreme caution in CKD due to hyperkalaemia risk. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) — now recommended for CKD with ACR ≥30 regardless of diabetes (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY trials) and provide additional renoprotective and cardiovascular benefit.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A GP reviews the medication list of a 70-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 18 mL/min). Which of the following medications requires dose adjustment or avoidance in advanced CKD?
Option_A: Amlodipine — dose reduction required
Option_B: Levothyroxine — must be halved in CKD
Option_C: Metformin — should be stopped when eGFR falls below 30 mL/min due to risk of lactic acidosis
Option_D: Paracetamol — contraindicated in all stages of CKD
Option_E: Simvastatin — contraindicated in CKD
Explanation: Drug prescribing in CKD requires careful consideration of renal clearance, active metabolites, and nephrotoxicity. Key drugs requiring dose adjustment or avoidance in CKD: (1) METFORMIN: must be reviewed if eGFR <45 (reduce dose); STOP if eGFR <30 (risk of lactic acidosis — metformin is renally excreted). (2) NSAIDs: avoid in CKD — reduce renal blood flow and worsen kidney function. (3) GENTAMICIN and aminoglycosides: dose by levels — nephrotoxic and ototoxic. (4) LITHIUM: renally excreted — reduced dose with close level monitoring. (5) DIGOXIN: reduce dose — renally excreted. (6) OPIOIDS: morphine and codeine — active metabolites (morphine-6-glucuronide) accumulate in CKD causing prolonged sedation and respiratory depression. Use oxycodone or buprenorphine instead. (7) Low-molecular-weight heparins: dose adjustment needed. (8) GABAPENTIN and pregabalin: dose reduction required. (9) ALLOPURINOL: start at reduced dose. (10) DOACs: apixaban can be used with dose reduction; rivaroxaban with caution; avoid dabigatran in severe CKD. Drugs NOT requiring dose adjustment: amlodipine, levothyroxine, simvastatin, paracetamol (safe in CKD at standard doses). The BNF appendix on renal impairment should always be consulted. NICE recommends a medication review at each CKD stage change.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 65-year-old man with CKD stage 5 (eGFR 8 mL/min) is being prepared for renal replacement therapy. He asks about his options. According to NICE NG107 (renal replacement therapy), which of the following is the preferred initial modality for most patients?
Option_A: Conservative management is the only option for patients over 60
Option_B: Haemodialysis via a temporary femoral line is the recommended long-term access
Option_C: Kidney transplantation (if suitable) offers the best long-term survival and quality of life — otherwise, home dialysis (peritoneal dialysis or home haemodialysis) is preferred over in-centre haemodialysis where possible
Option_D: Peritoneal dialysis is contraindicated in all diabetic patients
Option_E: There is no choice — all patients must have in-centre haemodialysis three times per week
Explanation: NICE NG107 (renal replacement therapy and conservative management) recommends: (1) KIDNEY TRANSPLANTATION is the gold standard for renal replacement — offers the best long-term survival, quality of life, and cost-effectiveness. Pre-emptive transplant (before dialysis is needed) has the best outcomes. Options: living donor (best outcomes), deceased donor (DBD or DCD). Not all patients are suitable (age, comorbidities, fitness for surgery). (2) HOME-based therapies are preferred when transplant is not immediately available: peritoneal dialysis (PD — CAPD or APD) or home haemodialysis. Home therapies offer better quality of life, flexibility, and preservation of residual renal function. (3) IN-CENTRE HAEMODIALYSIS: typically 3 sessions per week, 4 hours per session. Requires vascular access — arteriovenous FISTULA (AVF) is the preferred long-term access (created surgically 6 months in advance). AV graft or tunnelled central venous catheter (Permcath) are alternatives. Temporary femoral lines carry high infection risk and are NOT for long-term use. (4) CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT (supportive/palliative care without dialysis): an appropriate choice for some patients — especially frail elderly with multiple comorbidities where dialysis may not improve quality or quantity of life. Must be an informed shared decision. Pre-dialysis preparation: (1) Referral to nephrology when eGFR <30. (2) Patient education about all modalities. (3) AVF creation at eGFR ~15-20. (4) Start dialysis based on symptoms, not a specific eGFR threshold.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 50-year-old woman with CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52 mL/min) is seen in a primary care CKD review. Her blood pressure is 128/78 mmHg, ACR is 4 mg/mmol, and HbA1c is 48 mmol/mol. She has no proteinuria on urine dipstick. How often should her renal function be monitored according to NICE NG203?
Option_A: Annually — CKD G3aA1 is low-risk and annual monitoring is appropriate
Option_B: Daily blood tests are required at this stage
Option_C: Every 2 weeks — all CKD patients require fortnightly monitoring
Option_D: Every 6 months — this stage always requires 6-monthly review
Option_E: No monitoring is needed until eGFR drops below 30
Explanation: NICE NG203 provides a monitoring frequency grid based on the GFR-Albuminuria risk map: LOW RISK (green): G1-2A1, G3aA1 → monitor ANNUALLY. MODERATE RISK (yellow): G1-2A2, G3aA2, G3bA1 → monitor at LEAST annually. HIGH RISK (orange): G1-2A3, G3aA3, G3bA2, G4A1 → monitor TWICE yearly. VERY HIGH RISK (red): G3bA3, G4A2-3, G5 → monitor THREE or FOUR times yearly. This patient: eGFR 52 = G3a, ACR 4 = A2 (wait — ACR 4 is A2, not A1). Actually, let me reconsider: ACR 4 mg/mmol is in the A2 range (3-30 mg/mmol). This would be G3aA2, which is moderate risk → at least annually. However, the question states 'no proteinuria on dipstick' and ACR 4 — since ACR 3-30 is microalbuminuria (not detected on standard dipstick), this is correctly G3aA2. For this question, annual monitoring is the most appropriate answer of those given. The monitoring should include: eGFR, ACR, blood pressure, potassium, calcium, phosphate, PTH (from G3b onwards), FBC (from G3b), and bicarbonate. CKD should be confirmed with TWO eGFR measurements ≥90 days apart before diagnosis.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 48-year-old man with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) presents to his GP. Ultrasound shows bilateral enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts. His eGFR is 55 mL/min. He asks about his prognosis. What is the most important complication to monitor for in ADPKD?
Option_A: Adrenal insufficiency — ADPKD frequently involves the adrenal glands
Option_B: Hypertension and progressive renal failure — ADPKD is the most common inherited cause of kidney failure, and hypertension accelerates disease progression
Option_C: Isolated hepatic failure — the liver cysts cause synthetic dysfunction
Option_D: Malignant transformation of renal cysts — ADPKD carries a high cancer risk
Option_E: Pulmonary fibrosis — a common extra-renal manifestation
Explanation: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is the most common inherited kidney disease (1 in 400-1000) and the most common inherited cause of end-stage renal disease (ESRD), accounting for ~10% of patients on dialysis. Caused by mutations in PKD1 (chromosome 16, ~85% — more severe) or PKD2 (chromosome 4, ~15% — milder). Key features: (1) Bilateral renal enlargement with multiple cysts. (2) Progressive renal failure — typically ESRD by age 50-60 for PKD1. (3) HYPERTENSION — occurs early (often before renal impairment) and is the most important modifiable risk factor for disease progression. Target BP <130/80. (4) Flank/abdominal pain (cyst expansion, haemorrhage, infection). (5) Haematuria (cyst rupture). (6) Recurrent UTIs and cyst infections. Extra-renal manifestations: (1) LIVER CYSTS (most common, ~80%) — rarely cause liver failure. (2) INTRACRANIAL ANEURYSMS (5-10%) — risk of subarachnoid haemorrhage. Screen with MRA if family history of aneurysm or SAH. (3) Mitral valve prolapse. (4) Pancreatic cysts. (5) Hernias. Management: BP control (ACE inhibitor first-line), adequate hydration, avoid nephrotoxins. TOLVAPTAN (vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist) — slows cyst growth and renal decline in selected patients with rapidly progressive disease (NICE TA877). Cysts do NOT undergo malignant transformation.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A foundation doctor notices that a patient with CKD stage 4 has a serum bicarbonate of 16 mmol/L (low) on their routine blood tests. The patient has no acute illness. According to NICE NG203, what is the recommended management?
Option_A: Commence oral sodium bicarbonate supplementation to maintain serum bicarbonate ≥22 mmol/L — metabolic acidosis accelerates CKD progression
Option_B: Ignore the low bicarbonate — it is a normal finding in CKD
Option_C: Prescribe IV sodium bicarbonate infusions weekly
Option_D: Start acetazolamide to correct the acidosis
Option_E: Switch the patient to a high-protein diet to generate more bicarbonate
Explanation: Metabolic acidosis is a common complication of CKD, typically developing from stage 4 onwards as the kidneys lose the ability to excrete hydrogen ions and regenerate bicarbonate. NICE NG203 recommends: (1) Offer oral SODIUM BICARBONATE supplementation to maintain serum bicarbonate ≥22 mmol/L. (2) Typical dose: 500 mg-1g TDS (adjusted to maintain target). (3) Rationale: chronic metabolic acidosis contributes to: muscle wasting (protein catabolism), bone disease (buffering of acid by bone mineral), accelerated CKD progression, hyperkalaemia (H⁺/K⁺ exchange), cardiovascular risk. (4) The BICAR-ICU and other studies support bicarbonate supplementation in CKD. (5) Monitor serum bicarbonate regularly. (6) Be aware that sodium bicarbonate increases sodium intake — may need to adjust other sodium-containing medications and monitor for fluid retention and hypertension. Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase INHIBITOR that causes renal bicarbonate wasting — it would WORSEN metabolic acidosis, not treat it. High-protein diets increase acid load (protein metabolism generates hydrogen ions) and would worsen the acidosis. Dietary advice in CKD generally includes moderate protein restriction, low potassium, low phosphate, and low sodium.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: An FY1 doctor is asked to prescribe IV maintenance fluids for a 70 kg man who is nil by mouth after elective bowel surgery. He has no ongoing fluid losses, normal renal function, and normal electrolytes. According to NICE CG174 (IV fluid therapy), what are the daily maintenance requirements for water, sodium, and potassium?
Option_A: 25-30 mL/kg/day water, 1 mmol/kg/day sodium, 1 mmol/kg/day potassium — typically 1 bag of 0.9% saline + 1 bag of 5% dextrose with 20-40 mmol KCl per bag over 24 hours
Option_B: 50 mL/kg/day water, 5 mmol/kg/day sodium, 5 mmol/kg/day potassium
Option_C: All post-operative patients should receive 3L of 0.9% normal saline daily regardless of weight
Option_D: Maintenance fluids are never needed — patients should drink water instead
Option_E: Only 5% dextrose should be given — saline is contraindicated post-operatively
Explanation: NICE CG174 (IV fluid therapy in adults in hospital) provides clear guidance on maintenance fluid prescribing: DAILY MAINTENANCE REQUIREMENTS: (1) WATER: 25-30 mL/kg/day. For a 70 kg man: 1750-2100 mL/day. (2) SODIUM: 1 mmol/kg/day (~70 mmol/day). (3) POTASSIUM: 1 mmol/kg/day (~70 mmol/day). (4) GLUCOSE: 50-100 g/day (to prevent starvation ketosis). A typical prescription: 1 bag 0.9% NaCl 1000 mL (contains 154 mmol Na) + 1 bag 5% dextrose 1000 mL (glucose for energy) + 20-40 mmol KCl added to each bag. Alternatively: 0.18% saline/4% dextrose with 20-40 mmol KCl per bag × 2 bags. KEY PRINCIPLES: (1) ASSESS fluid status before prescribing (hydration, urine output, fluid balance). (2) Prescribe using the 'ASSESS, RESUSCITATE, ROUTINE MAINTENANCE, REPLACEMENT AND REDISTRIBUTION' framework. (3) Avoid excessive 0.9% saline — causes hyperchloraemic acidosis. Use balanced crystalloids (Hartmann) for resuscitation. (4) Monitor daily: fluid balance, weight, U&Es. (5) Reassess and adjust daily — do NOT continue unnecessary IV fluids. (6) Potassium must be replaced — post-operative patients commonly develop hypokalaemia. Rate of KCl: maximum 10 mmol/hr peripherally (higher rates require central access and cardiac monitoring).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 68-year-old woman on bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily for hypertension presents with confusion and lethargy. Blood tests show: sodium 118 mmol/L, serum osmolality 250 mOsm/kg (low), urine sodium 45 mmol/L, urine osmolality 450 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated). She is clinically euvolaemic. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Addison disease causing hyponatraemia
Option_B: Diabetes insipidus
Option_C: Hypervolaemic hyponatraemia due to heart failure
Option_D: Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia from dehydration
Option_E: Syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH) — likely secondary to thiazide diuretic use
Explanation: This is SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion). Diagnostic criteria: (1) Hyponatraemia (Na <135 mmol/L). (2) LOW serum osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg). (3) Inappropriately CONCENTRATED urine (osmolality >100 mOsm/kg — the kidneys should be producing dilute urine in hypo-osmolar states, but ADH prevents this). (4) Urine sodium >30 mmol/L. (5) EUVOLAEMIC — no signs of dehydration or fluid overload. (6) Normal thyroid and adrenal function (must exclude hypothyroidism and Addison disease). Causes of SIADH: (1) DRUGS (most common): thiazide diuretics, SSRIs, carbamazepine, opioids, cyclophosphamide. (2) CNS: stroke, SAH, meningitis, head injury. (3) LUNG: pneumonia, lung cancer (small cell — ectopic ADH production), TB. (4) Other: post-operative (pain, nausea stimulate ADH), HIV. Management of hyponatraemia depends on severity and symptoms: SEVERE (Na <120 or seizures/coma): hypertonic 3% saline with close monitoring — aim to raise Na by no more than 8-10 mmol/L in first 24 hours (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome/central pontine myelinolysis if corrected too rapidly). MODERATE: fluid restriction (750-1000 mL/day) is first-line for SIADH. STOP the causative drug. Consider: demeclocycline or tolvaptan (vasopressin receptor antagonist) if fluid restriction fails.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 55-year-old man with small cell lung cancer is found to have a sodium of 115 mmol/L. He is drowsy but not seizing. The medical registrar plans to correct the sodium. What is the maximum safe rate of sodium correction to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome?
Option_A: Correct by no more than 1-2 mmol/L per hour with no daily limit
Option_B: Correct by no more than 8-10 mmol/L in the first 24 hours and no more than 18 mmol/L in the first 48 hours
Option_C: Correct to normal (135 mmol/L) within 6 hours
Option_D: No correction is needed — sodium of 115 is not dangerous
Option_E: Rapid correction with 3% hypertonic saline bolus to normal levels within 12 hours
Explanation: The safe rate of sodium correction is critical to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS, previously called central pontine myelinolysis). ODS occurs when chronic hyponatraemia is corrected TOO RAPIDLY — brain cells that have adapted to the hypo-osmolar state (by losing organic osmolytes) cannot re-adapt quickly enough to rising osmolality, causing demyelination predominantly in the pons. Clinical features of ODS appear 2-6 days after overcorrection: dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome — often irreversible. Safe correction rate: (1) Maximum 8-10 mmol/L in the first 24 hours. (2) Maximum 18 mmol/L in the first 48 hours. (3) In patients at HIGH RISK of ODS (sodium <105, chronic hyponatraemia, alcoholism, malnourishment, hypokalaemia, liver disease): even slower correction — maximum 6-8 mmol/L in 24 hours. Management approach: ACUTE symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, reduced consciousness): give 100 mL 3% hypertonic saline IV bolus over 10-20 minutes (may repeat up to 3 times). Aim to raise sodium by 4-6 mmol/L in first few hours to treat acute symptoms. Then SLOW DOWN. CHRONIC hyponatraemia: fluid restriction first-line. Correct slowly. Monitor sodium every 4-6 hours during correction. If overcorrection occurs: give IV dextrose 5% ± desmopressin (DDAVP) to re-lower sodium (a rescue strategy).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 45-year-old man is admitted with severe vomiting for 3 days. Blood tests show: potassium 2.5 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, chloride 88 mmol/L (low), bicarbonate 36 mmol/L (raised), pH 7.52. What is the acid-base disturbance and the underlying mechanism?
Option_A: Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis from normal saline excess
Option_B: Metabolic alkalosis due to loss of gastric acid (HCl) from vomiting — with hypokalaemia from renal potassium wasting
Option_C: Mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis
Option_D: Respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation
Option_E: Type 1 (distal) renal tubular acidosis
Explanation: This is metabolic alkalosis from protracted vomiting. The mechanism: (1) Vomiting loses gastric acid (HCl) — loss of H⁺ ions causes alkalosis, loss of Cl⁻ causes hypochloraemia. (2) Volume depletion from vomiting activates the RAAS → aldosterone → renal sodium reabsorption at the expense of potassium and hydrogen ion excretion → HYPOKALAEMIA and perpetuation of alkalosis. (3) The kidneys normally excrete excess bicarbonate, but in volume depletion and chloride depletion, the kidneys must reabsorb sodium with bicarbonate (because chloride is not available) — this maintains the alkalosis. (4) Hypokalaemia itself perpetuates alkalosis: intracellular K⁺ shifts out in exchange for H⁺ moving into cells, and the kidney excretes more H⁺ to conserve K⁺. Blood gas/biochemistry: pH >7.45, raised bicarbonate (>30), low potassium, low chloride — this is a HYPOKALAEMIC HYPOCHLORAEMIC METABOLIC ALKALOSIS. Treatment: (1) IV 0.9% NORMAL SALINE — replaces volume AND chloride (chloride is essential for correcting the alkalosis — this is 'chloride-responsive' metabolic alkalosis). (2) IV potassium replacement (essential — alkalosis will not correct until potassium is repleted). (3) Treat the underlying cause (anti-emetics, address cause of vomiting). Other causes of metabolic alkalosis: diuretic use (loop/thiazide), Cushing syndrome, Conn syndrome, excessive bicarbonate administration.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 62-year-old woman with a history of thyroidectomy presents with perioral tingling, carpopedal spasm, and a positive Chvostek sign. Blood tests show: corrected calcium 1.65 mmol/L (low), phosphate 1.9 mmol/L (high), magnesium 0.6 mmol/L (low), PTH 0.8 pmol/L (very low). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Hungry bone syndrome post-thyroidectomy
Option_B: Hypoparathyroidism secondary to inadvertent removal or damage of the parathyroid glands during thyroidectomy
Option_C: Pseudohypoparathyroidism — PTH resistance
Option_D: Vitamin D deficiency
Option_E: Vitamin D-dependent rickets type 1
Explanation: This is hypoparathyroidism — the most common cause is inadvertent damage to or removal of the parathyroid glands during thyroid or parathyroid surgery. The biochemical pattern: LOW calcium, HIGH phosphate, LOW/absent PTH = hypoparathyroidism. PTH normally: (1) Increases calcium reabsorption from kidneys. (2) Stimulates osteoclastic bone resorption (releases calcium). (3) Stimulates 1-alpha hydroxylase (active vitamin D production → increases gut calcium absorption). (4) Decreases phosphate reabsorption (phosphaturic). Without PTH: calcium falls, phosphate rises. Clinical features of hypocalcaemia: (1) NEUROMUSCULAR excitability: paraesthesia (perioral, fingers, toes), muscle cramps, carpopedal spasm (Trousseau sign — BP cuff inflation induces hand spasm), Chvostek sign (tapping over facial nerve causes ipsilateral facial muscle twitch). (2) Severe: laryngospasm, seizures, QT prolongation on ECG (risk of arrhythmias). Emergency management of symptomatic hypocalcaemia: 10 mL 10% CALCIUM GLUCONATE IV over 10 minutes with cardiac monitoring (can be repeated or followed by infusion). Also check and replace MAGNESIUM — hypomagnesaemia impairs PTH secretion and causes PTH resistance, making hypocalcaemia refractory to treatment until magnesium is corrected. Long-term management: oral calcium supplements + alfacalcidol (active vitamin D analogue). Pseudohypoparathyroidism has LOW calcium but HIGH PTH (PTH resistance — end-organ insensitivity).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 58-year-old woman with primary hyperparathyroidism is found to have a corrected calcium of 3.2 mmol/L. She presents with polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, abdominal pain, and confusion. Her ECG shows a shortened QT interval. What is the most important initial management?
Option_A: Emergency parathyroidectomy within 1 hour
Option_B: IV furosemide 80 mg as the first-line treatment
Option_C: IV normal saline rehydration (3-4 litres over 24 hours) — aggressive fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of acute hypercalcaemia management
Option_D: Oral bisphosphonate and discharge
Option_E: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily
Explanation: This is symptomatic hypercalcaemia — a medical emergency when calcium >3.0 mmol/L or symptomatic at any level. Symptoms of hypercalcaemia (mnemonic: 'stones, bones, groans, and psychic moans'): Stones (renal calculi, nephrocalcinosis), Bones (bone pain, osteoporosis), Groans (abdominal pain, constipation, nausea, pancreatitis), Psychic moans (confusion, depression, fatigue, psychosis). Plus: polyuria and polydipsia (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus — calcium impairs aquaporin function), shortened QT interval on ECG. Management of acute hypercalcaemia: STEP 1: IV 0.9% NORMAL SALINE — aggressive rehydration (3-4 L in first 24 hours). This is the MOST IMPORTANT initial step. Hypercalcaemia causes renal water loss (nephrogenic DI) leading to dehydration, which further concentrates calcium. Rehydration restores renal calcium excretion. STEP 2: IV BISPHOSPHONATE — zoledronic acid 4 mg IV (single dose) or pamidronate 60-90 mg IV. Takes 2-4 days to work. Inhibits osteoclastic bone resorption. STEP 3: Consider IV furosemide ONLY if fluid-overloaded — historically used to promote calciuresis but NOT routine first-line (can worsen dehydration if given before adequate rehydration). Other options: calcitonin (rapid but transient effect), denosumab (if bisphosphonate-refractory), steroids (for sarcoidosis, myeloma, lymphoma — granulomatous disease with excess 1,25-vitamin D production). Definitive treatment of primary hyperparathyroidism: parathyroidectomy (planned, not emergency).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 70-year-old man is admitted with AKI and his potassium is 6.9 mmol/L. An ECG is performed. Which of the following ECG changes is the EARLIEST sign of hyperkalaemia?
Option_A: Absent P waves
Option_B: Broad QRS complexes
Option_C: Peaked (tall, tented) T waves
Option_D: Sine wave pattern
Option_E: ST segment depression
Explanation: The ECG changes of hyperkalaemia follow a characteristic progression with rising potassium: (1) K⁺ 5.5-6.5 mmol/L: PEAKED (TALL, TENTED) T WAVES — this is the EARLIEST ECG sign. The T waves become symmetrically tall and narrow ('tenting'). (2) K⁺ 6.5-7.5 mmol/L: prolonged PR interval → flattened or absent P waves → widened QRS complex. (3) K⁺ 7.5-8.0 mmol/L: further QRS widening → the QRS merges with T waves → SINE WAVE pattern (a pre-arrest rhythm). (4) K⁺ >8.0 mmol/L: ventricular fibrillation, asystole, death. IMPORTANT: the correlation between potassium level and ECG changes is not always linear — some patients develop life-threatening arrhythmias at lower levels, especially if the rate of rise is rapid or there is co-existing cardiac disease. Any ECG change with hyperkalaemia warrants EMERGENCY TREATMENT. The ECG should be obtained IMMEDIATELY when hyperkalaemia is identified. Calcium gluconate (10 mL of 10% IV) protects the myocardium by stabilising the cardiac membrane potential — it does NOT reduce potassium but buys time. Always treat ECG changes BEFORE waiting for repeat blood tests. Also check for pseudohyperkalaemia: haemolysed sample (most common artefact), prolonged tourniquet use, high platelet/WCC count (potassium released from cells during clotting).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 30-year-old woman on long-term omeprazole for gastro-oesophageal reflux disease presents with muscle weakness, tremor, and cardiac palpitations. Blood tests show: magnesium 0.45 mmol/L (very low), calcium 2.0 mmol/L (low-normal), potassium 3.0 mmol/L (low). Her ECG shows prolonged QT interval. What is the most likely cause of her hypomagnesaemia?
Option_A: Conn syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism)
Option_B: Gitelman syndrome
Option_C: Long-term proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use — an increasingly recognised cause of hypomagnesaemia
Option_D: Malnutrition from anorexia nervosa
Option_E: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Explanation: PPI-induced hypomagnesaemia is an important and increasingly recognised adverse effect of long-term PPI use (typically after >3 months, but more common after >1 year). MHRA issued a safety alert about this association. Mechanism: PPIs impair intestinal magnesium absorption via the TRPM6 transporter in the colon. Key clinical features of hypomagnesaemia: (1) Neuromuscular: tremor, muscle weakness, cramps, tetany, seizures. (2) Cardiac: arrhythmias, PROLONGED QT interval (risk of torsades de pointes). (3) REFRACTORY HYPOKALAEMIA — magnesium is required for the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump. Hypokalaemia will NOT correct until magnesium is repleted. (4) REFRACTORY HYPOCALCAEMIA — magnesium is needed for PTH secretion and PTH action. Important PPI side effects to know: (1) Hypomagnesaemia. (2) Increased fracture risk (impaired calcium absorption). (3) C. difficile infection (reduced gastric acid barrier). (4) Community-acquired pneumonia. (5) Vitamin B12 deficiency (long-term). (6) Acute interstitial nephritis. (7) Rebound acid hypersecretion on withdrawal. Management: (1) Stop or reduce PPI if possible. (2) IV magnesium replacement if symptomatic (8 mmol MgSO₄ IV over 15-30 minutes). (3) Oral magnesium supplementation if mild. (4) Replace potassium and calcium concurrently. NICE recommends reviewing PPI indication regularly and using the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A foundation doctor is managing a patient with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). The initial potassium is 5.5 mmol/L. After 2 hours of insulin and IV fluid therapy, the potassium drops to 3.2 mmol/L. Why does potassium fall during DKA treatment, and what is the risk?
Option_A: Insulin drives potassium INTO cells — total body potassium in DKA is actually DEPLETED despite initially normal/high serum levels, and replacement must be given to prevent life-threatening hypokalaemia
Option_B: Insulin has no effect on potassium — the fall is coincidental
Option_C: Potassium falls because the kidneys excrete more during alkalosis only
Option_D: The fall in potassium is clinically insignificant and does not need treatment
Option_E: The potassium drop is due to haemodilution from IV fluids alone
Explanation: In DKA, potassium handling is critically important and frequently tested. The paradox: serum potassium may be NORMAL or HIGH at presentation despite the patient being profoundly TOTAL BODY potassium-DEPLETED. Mechanism: (1) In DKA, insulin deficiency and acidosis cause potassium to shift OUT of cells into the extracellular space (H⁺ moves into cells in exchange for K⁺ moving out). (2) Simultaneously, osmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia causes massive renal potassium losses — total body potassium stores are depleted by 3-5 mmol/kg. (3) When insulin treatment is started: insulin drives potassium BACK INTO cells (via Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase stimulation). Correction of acidosis also shifts K⁺ intracellularly. Serum potassium can fall rapidly and dangerously. NICE and JBDS DKA guidelines: (1) DO NOT start insulin if K⁺ <3.5 mmol/L — correct potassium FIRST. (2) Replace potassium in EVERY bag of IV fluids (typically 40 mmol KCl per litre). (3) Target potassium 4.0-5.5 mmol/L during DKA treatment. (4) Monitor potassium HOURLY during DKA management. (5) If initial K⁺ >5.5: do not add KCl to first bag but recheck in 1 hour. Hypokalaemia during DKA treatment can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias — it is one of the most common preventable causes of death in DKA.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 65-year-old man presents to his GP with a single episode of painless visible (frank) haematuria. He is a lifelong smoker. He has no dysuria, frequency, or loin pain. Urine dipstick confirms blood 3+, and MSU culture shows no bacterial growth. According to NICE NG12 (suspected cancer), what is the most appropriate referral?
Option_A: 2-week wait urgent cancer referral for investigation of urological malignancy — visible haematuria in patients ≥45 years requires urgent investigation to exclude bladder and renal cancer
Option_B: No referral needed — a single episode of haematuria is normal in smokers
Option_C: Reassure and repeat dipstick in 3 months
Option_D: Routine urology referral within 6 months
Option_E: Start empirical antibiotics for a presumed UTI despite negative culture
Explanation: NICE NG12 (suspected cancer — recognition and referral) recommends a 2-WEEK WAIT urgent referral for urological cancer investigation if: (1) VISIBLE (frank/macroscopic) haematuria in patients aged ≥45 years — even a SINGLE episode. (2) VISIBLE haematuria with dysuria in patients aged ≥60 years. (3) NON-VISIBLE (microscopic) haematuria in patients aged ≥60 with either dysuria or raised WCC on blood test. Visible haematuria is the most common presenting symptom of BLADDER CANCER (~80% of bladder cancers present with painless haematuria). Risk factors for bladder cancer: SMOKING (most important — 3-4x risk), occupational exposure to aromatic amines (dye, rubber, leather workers), male sex, increasing age, cyclophosphamide use, chronic cystitis/schistosomiasis. Investigation of haematuria: (1) Urine culture (exclude UTI). (2) Flexible CYSTOSCOPY — direct visualisation of the bladder (gold standard for detecting bladder cancer). (3) CT urogram — evaluates upper tracts (kidneys, ureters) for renal cell carcinoma or upper tract TCC. (4) Bloods: FBC, U&Es, PSA (in men). (5) Urine cytology (low sensitivity but may detect high-grade TCC). Non-visible haematuria in younger patients or without cancer risk factors: investigate with renal function, BP check, ACR (to exclude glomerular disease), and consider nephrology referral if proteinuria or renal impairment.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe left-sided colicky loin pain radiating to the groin, associated with nausea and vomiting. He is unable to keep still and is constantly moving around. Urine dipstick shows blood 2+. What is the most likely diagnosis and the recommended first-line imaging?
Option_A: Acute appendicitis — CT abdomen-pelvis with contrast
Option_B: Renal cell carcinoma — MRI kidney
Option_C: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm — CT angiogram
Option_D: Testicular torsion — Doppler ultrasound
Option_E: Ureteric colic (kidney stone) — non-contrast CT KUB (CT-kidneys, ureters, bladder) is the gold standard investigation
Explanation: This is ureteric colic (renal/ureteric calculus). Key features: (1) COLICKY pain — severe, spasmodic, comes in waves. (2) Loin-to-groin radiation (follows the path of the ureter). (3) Patient CANNOT keep still — rolling around in agony (unlike peritonitis where patients lie still). (4) Nausea and vomiting. (5) Haematuria on dipstick (present in ~85%). Investigation: NON-CONTRAST CT KUB (CT-kidneys, ureters, bladder) is the GOLD STANDARD — sensitivity >95% for detecting stones. It is non-contrast because most stones are radio-opaque on CT (except indinavir stones — rare). Ultrasound can be used first-line in pregnancy and children. Types of kidney stones: (1) Calcium oxalate (most common, ~80%) — radio-opaque on X-ray. (2) Calcium phosphate (~10%). (3) Uric acid (~5-10%) — radiolucent on X-ray (but visible on CT). (4) Struvite/magnesium ammonium phosphate ('staghorn' stones) — associated with urease-producing bacteria (Proteus). (5) Cystine (~1%) — associated with cystinuria. Acute management: (1) ANALGESIA — IM/IV diclofenac 75 mg (NSAID is FIRST-LINE — more effective than opioids for renal colic) or IV paracetamol. (2) Anti-emetic. (3) Alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) — medical expulsive therapy for stones 5-10 mm in the distal ureter. (4) Most stones <5 mm pass spontaneously. (5) Intervention (ureteroscopy, shockwave lithotripsy, PCNL) for stones >10 mm, persistent obstruction, infection, or failed conservative management.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 72-year-old man presents with urinary hesitancy, poor stream, terminal dribbling, nocturia (×4), and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying. Digital rectal examination reveals a smoothly enlarged, non-tender prostate with no hard nodules. His PSA is 3.2 ng/mL (normal for age). What is the most likely diagnosis and the first-line medical treatment?
Option_A: Benign prostatic hyperplasia — first-line: alpha-1 adrenoceptor blocker (e.g., tamsulosin) which relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck
Option_B: Chronic bacterial prostatitis — first-line: 4-week course of ciprofloxacin
Option_C: Overactive bladder syndrome — first-line: oxybutynin
Option_D: Prostate cancer — urgent 2-week wait referral
Option_E: Urethral stricture — first-line: urethral dilatation
Explanation: This is benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — the most common cause of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) in older men. BPH is a non-malignant enlargement of the prostate gland causing bladder outflow obstruction. Symptoms are classified as: STORAGE (irritative): frequency, urgency, nocturia, urge incontinence. VOIDING (obstructive): hesitancy, poor stream, straining, intermittent stream, terminal dribbling, incomplete emptying. NICE NG131 (LUTS in men) recommends: FIRST-LINE: (1) ALPHA-1 ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKER — tamsulosin 400 mcg OD or alfuzosin 10 mg OD. Mechanism: relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate capsule, prostatic urethra, and bladder neck. Rapid onset (days). Side effects: postural hypotension, dizziness, retrograde ejaculation, intraoperative floppy iris syndrome (inform ophthalmologist before cataract surgery). SECOND-LINE or COMBINATION: (2) 5-ALPHA REDUCTASE INHIBITOR — finasteride 5 mg OD or dutasteride 0.5 mg OD. Mechanism: blocks conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), shrinks the prostate over 3-6 months. Used for prostates >30g. Side effects: sexual dysfunction, reduced PSA by ~50% (important — double the PSA value for interpretation). (3) Combination therapy (alpha-blocker + 5-ARI) for moderate-to-severe LUTS with large prostate. Surgical options: TURP (transurethral resection of prostate), HoLEP (holmium laser enucleation) — for failed medical therapy or complications (recurrent retention, renal impairment, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 14-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe left testicular pain that began 2 hours ago during football. The left testis is high-riding, horizontally oriented, and extremely tender. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. What is the diagnosis and the most critical management step?
Option_A: Epididymo-orchitis — prescribe antibiotics and arrange outpatient follow-up
Option_B: Hydrocele — arrange elective surgical repair
Option_C: Testicular torsion — this is a surgical emergency requiring scrotal exploration and detorsion within 6 hours to save the testis
Option_D: Torsion of the testicular appendage (hydatid of Morgagni) — conservative management
Option_E: Varicocele — reassure and discharge
Explanation: This is TESTICULAR TORSION — a surgical emergency. The spermatic cord twists, cutting off the testicular blood supply. If not detorted within 6 HOURS, there is a >90% risk of testicular infarction and loss. Key clinical features: (1) Sudden onset severe unilateral testicular pain (often during physical activity or sleep). (2) HIGH-RIDING testis (retracted upwards). (3) HORIZONTAL LIE (normally the testis hangs vertically). (4) ABSENT cremasteric reflex (normally, stroking the inner thigh causes ipsilateral testicular retraction — absence is highly suggestive of torsion). (5) Negative Prehn sign (elevating the testis does NOT relieve pain — in epididymitis, elevation typically does relieve pain, though this is unreliable). Peak age: 12-18 years (but can occur at any age). Management: (1) IMMEDIATE surgical exploration — do NOT delay for imaging if clinical suspicion is high. Ultrasound (Doppler) may show absent blood flow but a normal scan does NOT exclude torsion. (2) If torsion confirmed: detort and FIX BOTH testes (bilateral orchidopexy — the contralateral testis is also at risk). (3) If the testis is non-viable: orchidectomy + contralateral fixation. (4) Manual detorsion can be attempted while awaiting theatre (outward rotation — 'open the book') but surgical exploration is still required. Bell-clapper deformity (abnormal horizontal lie of the testis with inadequate gubernacular attachment) predisposes to torsion and is bilateral — hence bilateral fixation is mandatory.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 28-year-old man presents with a painless, hard, non-tender lump in his right testis that he noticed while showering. It does not transilluminate. Tumour markers show: AFP raised, beta-hCG raised, LDH raised. Ultrasound confirms a solid intratesticular mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Epididymal cyst
Option_B: Hydrocele
Option_C: Inguinal hernia descending into the scrotum
Option_D: Testicular germ cell tumour (seminoma or non-seminomatous GCT)
Option_E: Varicocele
Explanation: This is a testicular germ cell tumour (GCT). Testicular cancer is the most common solid malignancy in men aged 15-44 years. Key features: (1) PAINLESS hard testicular lump — the classic presentation. (2) Does NOT transilluminate (solid mass — hydroceles transilluminate). (3) Intratesticular on ultrasound (epididymal cysts are extratesticular). Tumour markers: SEMINOMA: may have raised beta-hCG (in ~20%), AFP is always NORMAL. NON-SEMINOMATOUS GCT (NSGCT — including embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumour, choriocarcinoma, teratoma): AFP raised (yolk sac tumour component), beta-hCG raised (choriocarcinoma component). LDH is a non-specific marker of tumour burden. This patient has BOTH AFP and beta-hCG raised → NSGCT (or mixed GCT). Investigation: (1) Testicular ultrasound (first-line). (2) Tumour markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH). (3) CT chest-abdomen-pelvis for staging. Treatment: (1) RADICAL INGUINAL ORCHIDECTOMY — approach is through the GROIN (not the scrotum) to avoid disrupting lymphatic drainage. (2) Surveillance, chemotherapy (BEP — bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin), or radiotherapy depending on histology and stage. Prognosis: EXCELLENT — overall cure rate >95%. Even metastatic disease has >80% cure rate with chemotherapy. Risk factors: cryptorchidism (undescended testis — 5-10x risk), family history, previous contralateral GCT, Klinefelter syndrome.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 75-year-old man presents with urinary retention (residual volume 900 mL), bilateral hydroureteronephrosis on ultrasound, and a creatinine of 350 μmol/L. His PSA is 85 ng/mL. DRE reveals a hard, irregular, craggy prostate with loss of the median sulcus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
Option_B: Chronic bacterial prostatitis
Option_C: Prostate cancer with obstructive uropathy
Option_D: Renal cell carcinoma
Option_E: Urethral stricture
Explanation: This is locally advanced prostate cancer causing bilateral obstructive uropathy with AKI. Key diagnostic features: (1) Very high PSA (85 ng/mL — PSA >100 is highly suggestive of metastatic prostate cancer). Normal PSA is age-dependent but generally <4 ng/mL. (2) HARD, IRREGULAR, CRAGGY prostate on DRE with loss of the median sulcus — classic malignant features. BPH causes a smooth, symmetrical, rubbery enlargement. (3) Bilateral hydroureteronephrosis — tumour invading/compressing both ureters. (4) Obstructive AKI (post-renal). NICE NG131 recommends 2-week wait referral if: DRE suggests malignancy, or PSA above age-specific reference range. Diagnosis: (1) Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) prostate — first-line before biopsy (NICE NG131 update based on PROMIS trial). (2) If MRI Likert score 3-5: targeted transperineal prostate biopsy. (3) Staging: CT chest-abdomen-pelvis and bone scan (prostate cancer characteristically causes osteoBLASTIC — sclerotic — bone metastases, unlike most cancers which cause lytic lesions). Gleason score (now Grade Group system): grades tumour differentiation — higher grade = worse prognosis. Treatment: localised — active surveillance, radical prostatectomy, radiotherapy. Locally advanced/metastatic — androgen deprivation therapy (GnRH agonists — goserelin, or antagonists — degarelix), plus docetaxel chemotherapy or abiraterone/enzalutamide.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 50-year-old woman presents with recurrent urinary tract infections (4 episodes in the past year). She has no structural urological abnormality on imaging. According to NICE NG109, what is the recommended management strategy for recurrent UTIs in women?
Option_A: Continuous prophylactic antibiotics without any behavioural advice
Option_B: Immediate referral for cystoscopy after the second UTI
Option_C: Long-term high-dose ciprofloxacin as prophylaxis
Option_D: Review behavioural and personal hygiene measures first; if recurrent UTIs persist, consider antibiotic prophylaxis (low-dose trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin at night) for 6 months, then reassess
Option_E: Vaginal oestrogen cream only — antibiotics play no role
Explanation: NICE NG109 (UTI — recurrent) recommends a stepwise approach for recurrent UTIs in women (defined as ≥2 UTIs in 6 months or ≥3 in 12 months): STEP 1 — BEHAVIOURAL AND SELF-CARE ADVICE: adequate fluid intake (aim 2-3 L/day), avoid constipation, complete voiding (double voiding), post-coital voiding, wipe front to back, avoid spermicides. STEP 2 — If recurrences persist: ANTIBIOTIC PROPHYLAXIS — options: (a) Continuous low-dose prophylaxis: trimethoprim 100 mg at night OR nitrofurantoin 50-100 mg at night — for 6 MONTHS then stop and reassess. (b) Post-coital prophylaxis: single dose of trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin after sexual intercourse (if UTIs are clearly related to intercourse). (c) Self-start/standby antibiotics: patient keeps a supply and starts a 3-day course at onset of symptoms (requires reliable patient). STEP 3 — In post-menopausal women: VAGINAL OESTROGEN cream (estriol) — restores vaginal lactobacilli and reduces pH, reducing colonisation with uropathogenic E. coli. Can be used alone or in combination with other measures. (4) Investigate with renal ultrasound and consider urology referral if: haematuria, unusual organisms, suspected structural abnormality, or failure to respond to prophylaxis. Cranberry products: insufficient evidence to recommend (NICE). D-mannose: some evidence of benefit but not in NICE guidance.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 60-year-old man presents with acute urinary retention. He is unable to pass urine and has a palpable bladder. A urinary catheter is inserted, draining 1200 mL. His blood tests are normal. He has a history of BPH but was not on any medication. According to NICE and urological guidelines, what is the most appropriate management after catheterisation?
Option_A: Leave the catheter in permanently and arrange long-term catheter care
Option_B: Remove the catheter immediately and observe — most patients void successfully without medication
Option_C: Start tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) and arrange a trial without catheter (TWOC) in 2-3 days
Option_D: Take the patient directly to theatre for emergency TURP
Option_E: Trial of voiding without any alpha-blocker medication is sufficient
Explanation: Acute urinary retention (AUR) in men is most commonly caused by BPH. Management after catheterisation: (1) START an ALPHA-BLOCKER (tamsulosin 400 mcg OD) — this relaxes prostatic and bladder neck smooth muscle and significantly increases the success rate of subsequent voiding trials. Should be started BEFORE attempting catheter removal. (2) TRIAL WITHOUT CATHETER (TWOC): remove the catheter after 2-3 days of alpha-blocker therapy (to allow the drug to reach therapeutic effect). Monitor post-void residual. (3) If TWOC SUCCESSFUL: continue alpha-blocker, arrange urology outpatient follow-up, consider adding 5-ARI (finasteride) if prostate is large. (4) If TWOC FAILS: re-catheterise and arrange urology assessment for surgical intervention (TURP or alternatives). NICE recommends: (1) All men with AUR should have blood tests including creatinine (to assess for obstructive nephropathy). (2) Monitor for post-obstructive diuresis after catheterisation (especially if chronically obstructed) — may need IV fluid replacement if urine output >200 mL/hr. (3) Precipitating causes should be identified and addressed: constipation, UTI, medications (anticholinergics, opioids, sympathomimetics), post-operative (anaesthesia-related). (4) Emergency TURP is NOT indicated — surgery is planned electively after failed conservative management.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 45-year-old man presents with a 4-week history of perineal pain, dysuria, frequency, and pain on ejaculation. He has had low-grade fevers intermittently. DRE reveals a tender, boggy prostate. Urine culture is positive for E. coli. What is the most likely diagnosis and the appropriate antibiotic duration?
Option_A: Acute bacterial prostatitis — 2-4 weeks of a quinolone (e.g., ciprofloxacin) or trimethoprim that penetrates prostatic tissue
Option_B: Benign prostatic hyperplasia — alpha-blocker only
Option_C: Bladder cancer — urgent 2-week wait referral
Option_D: Chronic non-bacterial prostatitis — no antibiotics needed
Option_E: Urethritis — single-dose azithromycin
Explanation: This is acute bacterial prostatitis — infection of the prostate gland, most commonly by E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria, similar to UTI pathogens). Clinical features: (1) Perineal/suprapubic/lower back pain. (2) Dysuria, frequency, urgency. (3) Pain on ejaculation. (4) Systemic symptoms: fever, malaise, myalgia. (5) DRE: TENDER, boggy, warm, swollen prostate (perform DRE GENTLY — vigorous examination can cause bacteraemia). (6) Positive urine culture (and potentially positive blood cultures if septic). Management (NICE CKS — prostatitis): (1) ANTIBIOTICS that penetrate prostatic tissue — the prostate has a blood-prostate barrier that limits penetration of many antibiotics. Effective agents: ciprofloxacin (or other fluoroquinolones), trimethoprim, doxycycline. Beta-lactams (amoxicillin, co-amoxiclav) have POOR prostatic penetration and are less effective. (2) Duration: 2-4 WEEKS for acute bacterial prostatitis (much longer than simple UTI — 6-12 weeks for chronic bacterial prostatitis). (3) Analgesia (NSAIDs preferred). (4) Alpha-blockers may help urinary symptoms. (5) If systemically unwell (sepsis): hospital admission for IV antibiotics. Complications: prostatic abscess (suspected if failure to improve despite appropriate antibiotics — diagnosed with transrectal ultrasound or MRI), urinary retention, sepsis, chronic prostatitis.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 7-year-old boy presents with periorbital oedema and generalised swelling (anasarca). Urine dipstick shows protein 4+ and no blood. Blood tests show: albumin 15 g/L (very low), cholesterol 9.5 mmol/L (very high), creatinine normal. His 24-hour urine protein is 8 g (massively elevated). What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Option_B: Henoch-Schönlein purpura nephritis
Option_C: IgA nephropathy
Option_D: Nephrotic syndrome — most likely minimal change disease in this age group
Option_E: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
Explanation: This is NEPHROTIC SYNDROME — defined by the triad: (1) Proteinuria >3.5 g/24 hours (or protein:creatinine ratio >350 mg/mmol). (2) Hypoalbuminaemia (albumin <25 g/L). (3) OEDEMA — periorbital (especially morning), peripheral, ascites, pleural effusions, anasarca. Additional features: (4) Hyperlipidaemia and lipiduria. (5) Hypercoagulability (loss of antithrombin III and other anticoagulant proteins in urine — increased VTE risk). In CHILDREN, the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome is MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE (MCD, ~80% of childhood nephrotic syndrome). Features of MCD: (1) Peak age 2-6 years. (2) Selective proteinuria (predominantly albumin). (3) Normal complement levels. (4) Light microscopy: NORMAL (hence 'minimal change'). (5) Electron microscopy: FUSION OF PODOCYTE FOOT PROCESSES (the only abnormality). (6) Treatment: CORTICOSTEROIDS — most children respond dramatically to prednisolone (steroid-sensitive — >90%). NICE recommends NOT performing a renal biopsy in children aged 1-12 with typical nephrotic syndrome — treat empirically with steroids and biopsy only if steroid-resistant (no remission after 4-6 weeks). In ADULTS, the most common cause is membranous nephropathy (primary — anti-PLA2R antibodies, or secondary — malignancy, SLE, hepatitis B). Distinguish from NEPHRITIC syndrome: haematuria (red cell casts), hypertension, modest proteinuria, oliguria, raised creatinine.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 25-year-old man presents with visible haematuria that coincides with an upper respiratory tract infection. He reports similar episodes with previous colds. Blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg. Urine shows red cell casts and proteinuria (ACR 55 mg/mmol). His complement levels (C3, C4) are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Option_B: Alport syndrome
Option_C: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease)
Option_D: Minimal change disease
Option_E: Thin basement membrane disease
Explanation: This is IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) — the most common primary glomerulonephritis WORLDWIDE. Key features: (1) Episodic VISIBLE (macroscopic) haematuria COINCIDING with upper respiratory or GI infections — this is the hallmark ('synpharyngitic' haematuria — occurs AT THE SAME TIME as the infection, not 1-3 weeks after). (2) Between episodes: persistent microscopic haematuria ± proteinuria. (3) Red cell casts (glomerular origin of haematuria). (4) NORMAL complement levels (C3, C4) — this distinguishes IgA nephropathy from post-streptococcal GN and lupus nephritis (both consume complement). Pathophysiology: deposition of IgA immune complexes in the glomerular mesangium causing mesangial proliferation. Diagnosis: renal biopsy showing mesangial IgA deposition on immunofluorescence. Course: variable — 25% progress to ESRD over 20-25 years. Poor prognostic indicators: heavy proteinuria, hypertension, raised creatinine at diagnosis, crescentic GN on biopsy. Management: (1) ACE inhibitor/ARB for BP control and proteinuria reduction. (2) If proteinuria >1g/day despite maximal RAAS blockade: consider corticosteroids (TESTING study). (3) Fish oil supplements: limited evidence. Compare with POST-STREPTOCOCCAL GN: haematuria occurs 1-3 WEEKS AFTER streptococcal infection (latent period), LOW C3, raised ASO titre. Most common in children aged 5-12.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 60-year-old man presents with rapidly deteriorating renal function (creatinine rising from 120 to 450 μmol/L over 2 weeks), haematuria with red cell casts, and haemoptysis. He has a positive ANCA (anti-PR3/c-ANCA). Chest X-ray shows pulmonary haemorrhage. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease)
Option_B: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener granulomatosis) — an ANCA-associated vasculitis causing a pulmonary-renal syndrome
Option_C: IgA nephropathy with coincidental pneumonia
Option_D: Lupus nephritis
Option_E: Microscopic polyangiitis
Explanation: This is granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) — an ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis causing a pulmonary-renal syndrome. Key features: (1) RAPIDLY PROGRESSIVE GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (RPGN) — creatinine rising over days to weeks, haematuria with red cell casts. (2) PULMONARY HAEMORRHAGE (haemoptysis, pulmonary infiltrates) — pulmonary-renal syndrome. (3) c-ANCA positive (anti-PR3 antibodies) — ~90% of GPA is c-ANCA/anti-PR3 positive. (4) Classic triad of GPA: upper respiratory tract (sinusitis, nasal crusting, saddle-nose deformity, epistaxis), lower respiratory tract (pulmonary nodules, haemorrhage, cavitating lesions), and renal (glomerulonephritis). GPA is a medical EMERGENCY — untreated RPGN leads to irreversible renal failure within weeks. Treatment: (1) INDUCTION: IV cyclophosphamide + high-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone pulses then oral prednisolone). Rituximab is an alternative to cyclophosphamide (RAVE and RITUXVAS trials). (2) Plasma exchange (PLEX) — consider for severe pulmonary haemorrhage or dialysis-dependent renal failure (PEXIVAS trial showed benefit for alveolar haemorrhage). (3) MAINTENANCE: azathioprine or rituximab (to prevent relapse — GPA has a high relapse rate ~50%). Distinguish from: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM antibodies, linear IgG on biopsy), microscopic polyangiitis (p-ANCA/anti-MPO), Churg-Strauss/EGPA (eosinophilia, asthma). Renal biopsy in ANCA vasculitis shows pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis (crescents with little or no immune complex deposition).

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 55-year-old woman with SLE presents with oedema, hypertension, and a creatinine of 180 μmol/L. Urine shows protein 3+ and blood 2+ with red cell casts. Blood tests show low C3 and C4 complement levels, raised anti-dsDNA antibodies. What renal complication of SLE is most likely?
Option_A: Drug-induced nephrotoxicity from hydroxychloroquine
Option_B: Lupus nephritis (immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis)
Option_C: Renal amyloidosis
Option_D: Renal artery stenosis
Option_E: Renal vein thrombosis
Explanation: This is lupus nephritis — one of the most serious manifestations of SLE, occurring in up to 50-60% of patients. It is an immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis (Type III hypersensitivity). Key features: (1) Active urinary sediment: proteinuria, haematuria, red cell casts. (2) Raised creatinine. (3) LOW COMPLEMENT (C3 and C4) — immune complex consumption. This distinguishes lupus nephritis from IgA nephropathy and ANCA vasculitis (both have normal complement). (4) Raised anti-dsDNA antibodies (correlate with disease activity, especially renal). (5) Hypertension and oedema. ISN/RPS classification: Class I (minimal mesangial) → Class VI (advanced sclerosing). Classes III (focal proliferative) and IV (diffuse proliferative) are the most common and most serious — require aggressive immunosuppressive treatment. Class V is membranous lupus nephritis. Diagnosis: RENAL BIOPSY — essential for classification and treatment guidance. Shows immune complex deposition (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q — 'full house' pattern on immunofluorescence). Treatment: Class III/IV: induction with mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) or IV cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids. Maintenance: MMF or azathioprine. Hydroxychloroquine: recommended for ALL lupus patients — reduces flares and has renal protective effects. ACE inhibitor/ARB for proteinuria and BP control. Monitoring: complement levels, anti-dsDNA, urinalysis, creatinine, and proteinuria as markers of activity.

Subject: Renal / Urology
Question: A 50-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has progressive proteinuria over 5 years. Her ACR has risen from 5 to 150 mg/mmol. She has diabetic retinopathy, eGFR 35 mL/min, and blood pressure 155/95 mmHg. Urine shows no haematuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Option_A: Diabetic nephropathy — the most common cause of end-stage renal disease in the UK
Option_B: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
Option_C: IgA nephropathy coinciding with diabetes
Option_D: Membranous nephropathy
Option_E: Renal artery stenosis from diabetes-related atherosclerosis
Explanation: This is diabetic nephropathy (diabetic kidney disease) — the most common cause of ESRD in the UK and worldwide. It accounts for ~25-30% of patients starting renal replacement therapy. Key features: (1) Progressive proteinuria over years (microalbuminuria → macroalbuminuria → nephrotic-range proteinuria). (2) Declining eGFR. (3) Hypertension. (4) Usually NO haematuria (the absence of haematuria helps distinguish from glomerulonephritis). (5) Co-existing diabetic retinopathy — the presence of retinopathy supports the diagnosis of diabetic nephropathy (both are microvascular complications). (6) Long duration of diabetes (typically >10 years for type 2, >5 years for type 1). Pathology: glomerular basement membrane thickening, mesangial expansion, Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (nodular glomerulosclerosis — pathognomonic). Management: (1) TIGHT GLYCAEMIC CONTROL — HbA1c target 48-58 mmol/mol. (2) BP control — ACE inhibitor or ARB first-line (renoprotective). Target <130/80 if ACR ≥30. (3) SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) — significant renoprotective and cardiovascular benefit (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD trials). Now recommended by NICE for CKD with albuminuria. (4) Finerenone (non-steroidal mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) — additional renal and cardiovascular benefit (FIDELIO-DKD trial). (5) GLP-1 receptor agonists: cardiovascular and renal benefit. (6) Avoid nephrotoxins, smoking cessation, lipid management. Renal biopsy is NOT routinely needed if the clinical picture is consistent with diabetic nephropathy. Biopsy if: atypical features, rapid decline in eGFR, active urinary sediment, or absence of retinopathy.

Subject: Renal / Urology
